name: Home
title: Home
description: Functional entry page for international shipping where users can start shipments or quotes, track packages, schedule pickups, find locations, and review recent shipping activity.

core_features: Initialize the page by calling getHomeOverview to retrieve recent_shipments, draft_shipments, saved_customs_estimates, has_in_transit_shipments, saved_locations_count, and address_book_count for the current user.

Provide primary action shortcuts (Create Shipment, Get a Quote, Track, Schedule Pickup, Find Locations, Customs & Duties Estimator) that navigate to the appropriate flows with URL parameters (e.g., ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment).
Offer a quick tracking form that accepts a tracking number and navigates to track.html?tracking_number={enteredTrackingNumber}.
Offer a quick shipment/quote starter form where users enter basic origin/destination and shipping type information and are redirected to ship_rates.html with the appropriate mode URL parameter.
Display summaries of recent shipments and draft shipments from getHomeOverview, with each shipment clickable for tracking and a "View all" action that navigates to account_overview.html.
Display a summary of saved customs estimates from getHomeOverview with a shortcut to the full Customs & Duties Estimator tool (customs_estimator.html).

Show compact account-related indicators derived from getHomeOverview (has_in_transit_shipments, saved_locations_count, address_book_count) to guide users toward managing locations, address book, and in-transit shipments.
Use URL parameters (without localStorage) to control which sections of the homepage are focused or expanded (for example, shipments vs drafts, or whether the tracking form is emphasized).
user_workflows: Start a new shipment: 1) User selects the "Create Shipment" primary action. 2) The click handler constructs a URL ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment (and may append optional parameters derived from the quick shipment starter form if used). 3) The browser navigates to that URL, transferring the user into the shipment creation flow handled on ship_rates.html.

Get a shipping quote: 1) User selects the "Get a Quote" primary action. 2) The click handler navigates to ship_rates.html?mode=quote (optionally including query parameters from the quick shipment starter form). 3) The user completes details on ship_rates.html to see rate options.

Quick track from homepage: 1) User enters a tracking number into the quick tracking form. 2) User submits the form. 3) The form submit handler validates that a tracking number value is present (client-side only). 4) If valid, the handler redirects the browser to track.html?tracking_number={userInput}. 5) The track.html page then handles tracking details based on the URL parameter.

Schedule a pickup: 1) User clicks the "Schedule Pickup" shortcut tile. 2) The click handler navigates directly to pickup_schedule.html. 3) The user schedules a pickup on that page using its own flows.

Find service locations: 1) User clicks the "Find Locations" shortcut tile. 2) The click handler navigates to locations_search.html. 3) The user searches and manages locations there. The homepage exposes the count of saved locations from getHomeOverview as context but does not modify it.

Use Customs & Duties Estimator: 1) User clicks the "Customs & Duties Estimator" link in the tools section. 2) The click handler navigates to customs_estimator.html. 3) On the estimator page, the user can view, create, or save customs estimates. Any saved estimates that belong to the current user are later surfaced on the homepage via getHomeOverview.saved_customs_estimates.

Review recent shipments: 1) On page load, home-controller calls getHomeOverview and binds recent_shipments to the recent shipments component. 2) The component lists a subset of recent shipments. 3) User may click an individual shipment row to navigate to track.html?tracking_number={shipment.tracking_number} if a tracking_number exists. 4) User may click a "View all" link to navigate to account_overview.html with optional URL parameters indicating that the shipments sub-section should be shown.

Resume draft shipments: 1) getHomeOverview provides draft_shipments, which are listed in a dedicated summary component. 2) User clicks a draft shipment row. 3) The click handler navigates to ship_rates.html with mode=create_shipment and optional URL parameters referencing the draft shipment identifier if that convention is supported by ship_rates.html. 4) On ship_rates.html, the draft is resumed using its own logic.

Access saved customs estimates: 1) getHomeOverview provides saved_customs_estimates, which are listed in the customs estimates summary. 2) User clicks a saved estimate entry. 3) The click handler navigates to customs_estimator.html with URL parameters that identify or prepopulate that estimate, if supported by that page. 4) The estimator page handles loading and editing of the selected estimate.

Focus specific homepage sections via URL parameters: 1) User lands on index.html with optional parameters (for example, index.html?focus=track or index.html?shipmentsView=drafts). 2) home-controller reads these parameters on load. 3) Based on parameter values, it sets initial state flags, such as which tab in the shipments/drafts section is active or whether the track form is focused. 4) UI components read these state flags to render the correct active section. Any change of section (e.g., user clicks a tab) updates the URL parameters using history APIs without reloading, so that page state is restorable on refresh or when sharing the URL.
interactions: Click on primary action buttons (Create Shipment, Get a Quote, Track, Schedule Pickup, Find Locations, Customs & Duties Estimator) to trigger navigation to the appropriate pages with the required URL parameters.
Submit the quick tracking form to navigate to track.html with tracking_number set to the user-entered value.

Interact with the quick shipment starter form fields (origin, destination, shipment type, etc.) to build up initial shipping search parameters that can be passed to ship_rates.html when the user chooses Create Shipment or Get a Quote.
Click on a recent shipment entry to navigate to tracking for that shipment using its tracking_number if present.
Click on a draft shipment entry to navigate to ship_rates.html for editing or completing that draft (mode=create_shipment and optional draft-identifying query parameters).

Click on the "View all" control in the shipments/drafts summary area to navigate to account_overview.html, optionally adding URL parameters to indicate whether the shipments or drafts list should be in focus.
Click on a saved customs estimate entry to navigate to customs_estimator.html with URL parameters that identify or prepopulate the selected estimate.

Click on summary tiles or links related to saved locations and address book counts to navigate to the appropriate management areas (for example, to locations_search.html for locations; to account_overview.html or another account area configured to manage addresses).

Use section toggles or tabs (for example, between recent shipments and drafts) that update the URL parameters to reflect the active view instead of using localStorage; navigation changes within the page do not trigger full page reloads.
page_functionality.state_logic: The homepage does not require URL parameters to function, but it reacts to them for section focus and subview selection. On initial load of index.html, the home-controller reads the current URL query string. Optional parameters can include keys such as focus (e.g., focus=track, focus=shipments, focus=tools) to determine which primary section receives focus or is scrolled into view, and shipmentsView (e.g., shipmentsView=recent or shipmentsView=drafts) to decide whether the recent shipments or drafts list is the active subview. No state is persisted in localStorage; instead, when the user changes the active subview (for example by clicking a tab or toggle), the page updates the browser URL using history.replaceState or history.pushState to reflect the new state (for example, modifying shipmentsView). This allows users to refresh or share the URL while preserving the same homepage configuration. The only external navigation from this page uses explicit URL parameters required by the target pages: ship_rates.html?mode=quote for quote flows, ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment for shipment creation or draft continuation, track.html?tracking_number={userInputOrShipmentTrackingNumber} for tracking, pickup_schedule.html for pickup scheduling, locations_search.html for location search, customs_estimator.html for customs and duties estimation, and account_overview.html (optionally with additional parameters) for account-level shipment and draft management.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and page-level state. On page load, it calls getHomeOverview, binds the response to child components, and reads URL parameters to set initial section focus and active subviews.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: invoke getHomeOverview and apply response to data-bound components.
onUrlChange: read URL query parameters and update internal state flags (such as focus and shipmentsView) without reloading data unnecessarily.
onSectionChange: when child components change active section or tab, update the URL query parameters using history APIs to keep state in sync.

components.functionality: Displays the main shortcuts for starting key logistics tasks: Create Shipment, Get a Quote, Track, Schedule Pickup, Find Locations, and Customs & Duties Estimator. It does not fetch data itself but relies on navigation.
data_binding: home-controller.state.focus (for emphasizing a primary action if focus is set via URL)
getHomeOverview.has_in_transit_shipments (to optionally adjust emphasis on the Track action)
event_handlers: onClickCreateShipment: navigate to 'ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment' (optionally appending query parameters derived from quick-shipment-starter-form if present).
onClickGetQuote: navigate to 'ship_rates.html?mode=quote' (optionally with parameters derived from quick-shipment-starter-form).
onClickTrack: scroll to or focus quick-track-form if present, or navigate directly to 'track.html' if a tracking number is already supplied via URL.

components.functionality: Provides a single-field form for entering a tracking number from the homepage and navigating to the detailed tracking page.
event_handlers: onInputChangeTrackingNumber: update internal form state with the current user-entered tracking number.
onSubmit: validate that a tracking number is present; if valid, redirect to 'track.html?tracking_number={enteredTrackingNumber}'.
onFocus: if home-controller.state.focus equals 'track', automatically focus this input when the page loads.

components.functionality: Collects minimal shipping details (such as origin, destination, and shipment type) to seed the shipping rates or shipment creation flows. It does not call any backend directly; it passes user input forward via URL parameters when the user chooses to get a quote or create a shipment.
data_binding: home-controller.state.quickShipmentFields (local state for form fields that may be initialized from URL parameters if provided)
event_handlers: onChangeOrigin: update quickShipmentFields with origin-related inputs (city, postal code, country).
onChangeDestination: update quickShipmentFields with destination-related inputs (city, postal code, country).
onChangeShipmentType: update quickShipmentFields with chosen shipment_type and package_type.
onClickUseForQuote: construct a URL pointing to 'ship_rates.html?mode=quote' and append query parameters representing the quickShipmentFields, then navigate.
onClickUseForCreateShipment: construct a URL 'ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment' with query parameters from quickShipmentFields, then navigate.

components.functionality: Shows an overview of recent shipments for the current user, including key details such as status and destination, derived solely from getHomeOverview.recent_shipments. Provides links for tracking individual shipments and a 'View all' control.
data_binding: home-controller.state.shipmentsView (to determine whether this list or the drafts list is the active view)
event_handlers: onClickShipmentRow: when a shipment has a tracking_number, navigate to 'track.html?tracking_number={shipment.tracking_number}'.

onClickViewAll: navigate to 'account_overview.html' with optional URL parameters specifying that the shipment overview section should be active (for example, 'account_overview.html?section=shipments').
onClickTabRecent: set home-controller.state.shipmentsView to 'recent' and update the URL parameter (e.g., shipmentsView=recent) using history.replaceState.

components.functionality: Displays shipments with status draft from getHomeOverview.draft_shipments, allowing users to resume incomplete shipment setups.
data_binding: home-controller.state.shipmentsView (to determine whether this list or the recent shipments list is active)
event_handlers: onClickDraftRow: navigate to 'ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment' and include URL parameters that identify the selected draft shipment if supported by the target page (for example, 'draft_id={shipment.id}') without hardcoding values.

onClickViewAllDrafts: navigate to 'account_overview.html' with optional URL parameters indicating that the draft shipments sub-section should be active (for example, 'account_overview.html?section=drafts').
onClickTabDrafts: set home-controller.state.shipmentsView to 'drafts' and update the URL parameter (e.g., shipmentsView=drafts) using history.replaceState.

components.functionality: Shows an overview of saved customs and duties estimates based on getHomeOverview.saved_customs_estimates so the user can quickly revisit previous estimates.
event_handlers: onClickEstimateRow: navigate to 'customs_estimator.html' and optionally attach URL parameters that allow loading or pre-populating the selected estimate (for example, a parameter referencing CustomsEstimate.id).
onClickViewAllEstimates: navigate to 'customs_estimator.html' without additional parameters, allowing that page to present a full list of saved estimates.

components.functionality: Presents compact, non-interactive or lightly interactive indicators from getHomeOverview, such as whether there are shipments in transit, how many locations are saved, and how many entries are in the address book, to help users understand their current account context.
event_handlers: onClickInTransitIndicator: if used, navigate to 'account_overview.html' with parameters indicating that shipments currently in transit should be emphasized.
onClickSavedLocationsIndicator: navigate to 'locations_search.html' with optional parameters that show the user's saved or favorite locations.

onClickAddressBookIndicator: navigate to 'account_overview.html' or another configured page responsible for managing address book entries, with URL parameters specifying that the address book section should be active.

components.functionality: Groups secondary but frequently used tools and resources such as the Customs & Duties Estimator link and indirect shortcuts to address book or location management. It relies entirely on navigation; it does not load data.
data_binding: getHomeOverview.saved_customs_estimates (for determining whether to highlight access to existing estimates)
event_handlers: onClickManageAddressesTool: navigate to 'account_overview.html' (with optional parameters to open the address book section).
onClickManageLocationsTool: navigate to 'locations_search.html' (with optional parameters to focus saved locations).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a slim utility bar and primary navigation at the very top (managed by the page controller), followed immediately by a dominant hero section that fills most of the first viewport. Within the hero, a left-aligned content block houses the main headline and the two key functional forms (quick track + quick shipment starter) grouped within a primary actions panel. The right side of the hero is reserved for full-bleed global/satellite imagery. A vertical tools/shortcuts panel floats along the right edge of the viewport, overlapping the hero but not consuming core content width. Below the hero, a full-width account summary strip acts as a bridge into a structured content area where shipment and customs summaries are arranged in a two-column layout inside the centered content container.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a slim utility bar and primary navigation at the very top (managed by the page controller), followed immediately by a dominant hero section that fills most of the first viewport. Within the hero, a left-aligned content block houses the main headline and the two key functional forms (quick track + quick shipment starter) grouped within a primary actions panel. The right side of the hero is reserved for full-bleed global/satellite imagery. A vertical tools/shortcuts panel floats along the right edge of the viewport, overlapping the hero but not consuming core content width. Below the hero, a full-width account summary strip acts as a bridge into a structured content area where shipment and customs summaries are arranged in a two-column layout inside the centered content container.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport height and the full browser width, with its interactive content constrained to a centered max-width container (~1140px). Within this container, the left content column takes about 40–45% of the width for the primary actions panel and forms, while the right 55–60% remains visually lighter to showcase imagery. The floating tools/shortcuts panel hugs the right edge, using about 5–7% of the viewport width. Below the hero, the account summary strip spans 100% width with a moderate vertical height (~120–160px), while the main data region beneath it uses a 12-column grid: a wider left column (~65% width) for shipment and drafts summaries stacked vertically, and a narrower right column (~35% width) for customs estimates. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → account strip → lists) uses generous 48–64px gaps; internal component padding follows 8px-based increments (16–32px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Above the fold, the layout is intentionally spacious and marketing-driven: large headline, clear subtext, and only two main interactive blocks (tracking and starting a shipment), each with simple, uncluttered form fields. The hero avoids dense text or tables, ensuring a clean first impression. Below the hero, content density gradually increases: list components display multiple rows of recent shipments, drafts, and customs estimates, but each is contained in a card with ample white space, clear headings, and 16–24px internal padding so it still feels breathable. The account summary strip is compact in height but simple—few key stats rather than complex charts. The right-side tools panel is visually minimal, relying on iconography and tooltips, preventing it from feeling heavy despite its persistent presence.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow intentionally mirrors the F/Z pattern from the mockup. Users first encounter the logo and primary navigation across the top, then their gaze moves diagonally down to the left into the hero’s primary actions panel and headline. From there, attention follows the vertical stack of the quick-track and quick-shipment forms. The strong orange CTA accents in these forms act as focal points. The eye then travels horizontally to the right, noticing the global imagery and the vertical tools/shortcuts panel hugging the right edge. As the user scrolls, the flow continues downward: first to the full-width account summary strip (acting as a visual divider and progress checkpoint), then down into the more utilitarian content area where the shipments summary card (top-left) becomes the next anchor, followed by drafts below it and customs estimates on the right, creating a zig-zag scanning pattern down the page.

layout.overall_description: The home page presents as a modern, full-width corporate hero built over a darkened satellite/earth background image in the top viewport, with all functional content sitting inside a centered, 1140px-wide container. A thin utility bar and a comfortably tall navigation bar sit stacked at the top, after which the hero dominates the screen. On the left half of this hero, a strong white headline and brief supporting copy introduce international shipping services, with a prominent primary actions panel containing two key blocks: a quick tracking form and a quick shipment starter form, each styled as clean, white cards with generous padding and orange CTAs. The right half of the hero is visually lighter on UI, showing the global imagery and subtle network motifs, while along the far right edge a vertical tools/shortcuts panel floats mid-viewport as a slim column of square icon buttons. As the user scrolls past this hero, the background transitions to a neutral light surface and a full-width account summary strip appears, offering key account stats in a low-profile band. Beneath that, the content resolves into a two-column arrangement within the same centered container: on the left, recent shipments and drafts are stacked as separate cards, and on the right, customs estimate summaries sit in a complementary card. The overall experience remains airy, with strong horizontal alignment, consistent 8px-based spacing, and a clear F/Z reading pattern from navigation to hero actions to the detailed lists below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-controller defines the global shell of the page and establishes the full-bleed hero structure. It positions a thin utility strip at the very top (spanning 100% width with a height around 40px), followed immediately by a primary navigation bar of moderate height (~70–80px), both centered within a max-width content container (~1140px) while allowing their backgrounds to span full width. Directly beneath the navigation, the controller sets up the hero section that fills roughly 70–80% of the viewport height, using a darkened satellite/earth background image that extends edge-to-edge. Within this hero, the controller places a centered content container aligned to the page grid; in this container, the primary-actions-panel and its nested forms are anchored to the left side, occupying about 40–45% of the container width, while the remaining right area is left clean for imagery and the overlapping tools-shortcuts-panel. Below the hero, the home-controller introduces a full-width account-summary-strip band, then a main content section with a 12-column grid that places shipments-summary-list and drafts-summary-list in the wider left portion and customs-estimates-summary in the narrower right portion. Overall, it maintains generous vertical spacing (48–64px) between these major sections and keeps them centered relative to the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the main scrollable document flow, controlling stacked sections (utility bar, nav, hero, summary bands, lists). The hero background image is full-bleed and visually fixed relative to the hero section (no parallax assumed), while all child components scroll normally within this structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-actions-panel is the dominant functional block within the hero, placed inside the centered hero container and aligned to the left grid edge. Horizontally, it occupies roughly 40–45% of the container width, starting with a left margin that lines up vertically with the logo and main navigation content. Vertically, it is centered within the hero height, with its top edge sitting approximately 96–128px below the bottom of the navigation bar to preserve the spacious, marketing feel. The panel itself appears as a vertical stack: at the top, a large, multi-line headline in white or near-white text; directly underneath, a compact subline briefly describing the service; then, beneath this text block with about 24–32px of spacing, two white card-like blocks that host the quick-track-form and quick-shipment-starter-form. The panel does not have a heavy card container around everything; instead, the headline and subcopy feel directly on the hero background, while the forms appear as separate elevated cards beneath. The total width of the panel is wide enough to comfortably contain multi-field forms without feeling cramped, but it intentionally stops short of the hero’s centerline, leaving clear negative space between the forms and the right imagery area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, participating in normal scrolling. On smaller viewports, it scales to occupy a higher percentage of the width (up to ~90–100%) and shifts closer to center alignment, but maintains vertical stacking of its internal elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-track-form is positioned as the topmost functional card within the primary-actions-panel’s lower area. It appears directly beneath the panel’s subheadline, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space to maintain visual distinction. The form is contained within a rectangular card with a white background, subtle shadow, and slightly rounded corners, matching the modern corporate aesthetic. It spans the full width of the primary-actions-panel (about 40–45% of the main container), with 16–24px padding on all sides. Internally, the form fields (e.g., tracking number input and a primary orange 'Track' button) are arranged horizontally on larger screens: the input occupying roughly two-thirds of the card’s width and the button taking the remaining third, aligned to the right. On narrower screens, these stack vertically with 8–16px spacing. The card’s top edge aligns visually with the hero’s left grid, while its right edge leaves a comfortable gap before the hero’s visual center, ensuring it feels anchored on the left. The orange CTAs in this card are one of the strongest color accents in the hero, pulling user attention immediately after the headline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the hero content. Within the primary-actions-panel, it remains fixed in order as the first functional block, with responsive behavior that adjusts its internal layout (horizontal → vertical fields) based on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-shipment-starter-form sits directly below the quick-track-form within the primary-actions-panel, separated by a vertical gap of about 16–24px to visually group them as a pair while still distinguishing the two tasks. It uses the same card treatment: white background, subtle elevation, and 16–24px internal padding. It spans the same width as the quick-track-form, aligning exactly on the left and right edges for a clean columnar look. Inside the card, the form typically includes origin, destination, and package details in a compact arrangement; on larger screens, key fields may appear in one or two horizontal rows (e.g., origin and destination side by side), with the primary orange 'Start shipment' or 'Get quote' button aligned to the right or placed as a full-width button at the bottom. This card is slightly taller than the quick-track-form due to more fields, but both share consistent spacing and typography. Together, they form a clear vertical pair of core actions at the left of the hero, with the shipment starter as the second focal action after tracking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the rest of the hero content. Responsively, fields may collapse from multi-column to single-column as the available width decreases, but the card retains its position as the second block directly under the quick-track-form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipments-summary-list appears in the main content area below the hero and the account-summary-strip. It is placed in the wider left column of the 12-column grid, starting approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the account-summary-strip. Horizontally, it occupies about 65% of the content container’s width, aligned to the same left grid line as the hero’s primary-actions-panel for strong vertical alignment. The component is presented as a card-like section with a white background and subtle shadow, stretching across this left column with 24–32px padding inside. At the top of the card, a section header (e.g., 'Recent Shipments') spans the full width; below it, a list of rows displays recent shipment entries. The rows are moderately dense but separated by 8–12px vertical spacing, with clear column labels and adequate padding left and right. The card’s bottom edge ends well above the fold on large screens but can extend vertically if there are many entries, pushing other content downward. There is a 24–32px gap between this card’s bottom edge and the drafts-summary-list below, maintaining visual separation between the two yet clearly keeping them in the same left column group.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal page scroll. On narrower viewports, it becomes the first main data card below the account-summary-strip and expands to nearly full width (100% of the container), stacking above drafts-summary-list and customs-estimates-summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The drafts-summary-list is positioned directly beneath the shipments-summary-list in the same left-hand column of the post-hero content area. It aligns perfectly with the shipments-summary-list on both left and right edges, preserving a clean vertical column that begins at the same alignment as the hero’s primary content. A vertical gap of around 24–32px separates it from the shipments-summary-list, creating a clear hierarchy while visually grouping both under 'work in progress' and 'recent activity'. This component is slightly shorter in height than the shipments summary under typical use, reflecting fewer draft entries. It uses the same card design: white background, light shadow, and 24px internal padding, with a concise header at the top (e.g., 'Drafts') and a list of draft shipments beneath. List rows are moderately spaced (8–12px vertical gaps) and optimized for scannability, but with less visual emphasis than the shipments list above. The bottom edge of this card sits comfortably above the page’s lower fold, leaving some whitespace before any subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content. On smaller screens, it stacks directly below shipments-summary-list with consistent vertical spacing and expands to full container width, remaining in sequence before customs-estimates-summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The customs-estimates-summary occupies the narrower right-hand column of the main content area below the hero and account-summary-strip. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the shipments-summary-list on the left, creating a balanced two-column layout. It spans approximately 35% of the content container’s width, sitting flush to the right grid edge while leaving comfortable inner margins. Presented as a single, vertically oriented card with the same white, elevated style and 24–32px padding, it features a clear header at the top (e.g., 'Customs & Duties Estimates') followed by a compact list or summary tiles. Because this component typically holds fewer items than the shipments list, its height often matches roughly the top 60–70% of the shipments-summary-list card, giving the overall section a visually stepped profile. As content grows, it may extend downward, but it remains visually balanced against the left column. The horizontal spacing between this card and the left column is generous (24–32px) to keep the layout airy and prevent crowding in the center.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the rest of the main content. On narrow or mobile viewports, it drops below the left-column cards and becomes a full-width card with the same padding and spacing, maintaining a clear vertical reading order after shipments and drafts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-summary-strip is positioned immediately below the hero section, acting as a bridge between the marketing-heavy hero and the more utilitarian data lists. It spans the full width of the page, but its content is constrained within the same 1140px centered container, aligning with the grid used above and below. The strip has a moderate height (~120–160px), with a slightly lighter or tinted background color (e.g., a soft blue/gray) that contrasts gently with both the dark hero and the white cards beneath. Inside, key account metrics—such as current balance, number of active shipments, and pending pickups—are arranged horizontally in 2–4 evenly spaced info blocks. Each block has an icon, a label, and a bold numeric value, using 16–24px padding and consistent spacing between them. There is 48–64px of vertical space between the hero’s bottom edge and this strip’s top, and another 48–64px between this strip’s bottom and the top of the shipments-summary-list and customs-estimates-summary section, making it feel like a clean transitional band rather than a crowded dashboard. The strip’s content aligns left with the main grid but visually extends across the screen width for a strong horizontal break.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in order beneath the hero, scrolling out of view as the user continues downward. On smaller screens, the horizontal info blocks wrap into two rows with vertical spacing (16–24px) while keeping the same full-width band structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-shortcuts-panel manifests as a slim, vertical toolbar floating along the right edge of the viewport, visually overlapping the hero section and partially extending into the area just below the hero as the user scrolls. Horizontally, it sits about one gutter (16–24px) inside the right edge of the browser window, independent of the central content container, and occupies roughly 5–7% of the viewport width. Vertically, its center aligns with the vertical center of the hero on initial load, so that it sits roughly mid-height of the screen. The panel is made up of a vertical stack of square or slightly rounded buttons, each about 40–48px in size with 8–12px internal padding, representing quick tools such as 'Schedule pickup', 'Find location', 'Support', or 'Chat'. The buttons sit on a slightly opaque dark or deep blue background strip with subtle rounding, ensuring they remain legible against the hero imagery. There is comfortable spacing (8–12px) between each button. As the page scrolls, the toolbar remains visible for a portion of the scroll, then either stays pinned within the right side of the main content zone or gently docks near the top-right of the viewport, always avoiding overlap with critical content like the primary navigation or any fixed support widgets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a floating/sticky element on larger screens: initially positioned over the hero, remaining fixed relative to the viewport as the user starts scrolling, then optionally transitioning to a pinned position within the bounds of the main content column to prevent it from obscuring footer or bottom content. On smaller viewports (e.g., mobile), it collapses into a single floating action button or a compact horizontal bar at the bottom or top to avoid covering central content.

name: Ship & Get Rates
title: Ship & Get Rates
description: Page to search international shipping rates, compare services, apply filters and sorting, and either keep a quote or start a shipment as a guest.

core_features: Mode selection between getting a quote only and creating a shipment as a guest, with a visible 'Continue as Guest' option and URL-driven mode state.

Shipment search form to capture origin and destination (city, postal code, country) and package details (package type, shipment type, weight, dimensions, shipment date) and request rate options via createShipmentQuote.
Optional use of saved addresses to prefill origin fields when an origin_address_id URL parameter is present or when the user chooses a saved address via getSavedAddresses.
Automatic loading and reloading of existing quotes and rate options using quoteId and getShipmentQuoteDetails, including restoration of selected service and filters.
Display of available ShippingRateOption entries for the current ShipmentQuote, including service name, level, estimated delivery information, price, and optional CO2 impact indicators.
Filter and sort controls for delivery time, price, and CO2 impact using presets and sort options from getShippingSearchOptions, and applying them via updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort.
Selection of a specific shipping rate option for a quote using selectShippingRateOption, updating the selected option without creating a shipment when in quote mode.

In create_shipment mode, ability to create a linked Shipment draft from a selected rate option via selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true and navigate to shipment_details.html with the returned shipmentId.
Capability to save a Shipment as a draft without continuing to payment by calling saveShipmentAsDraft after a Shipment has been created from a selected rate option.

Support for capturing basic shipment value and insurance preference fields (conceptually mapped to Shipment.declared_value, declared_value_currency, insurance_type, insurance_included) when the user is creating a shipment draft from a saved address.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Get rates only (quote mode): 1) User lands on ship_rates.html?mode=quote (no quoteId). 2) Page loads search presets via getShippingSearchOptions. 3) User enters origin/destination and package details in the search form. 4) User submits the form, triggering createShipmentQuote in mode='quote'. 5) Page stores returned ShipmentQuote and ShippingRateOption list, updates URL with ?mode=quote&quoteId=<quote.id>, and displays rate options. 6) User adjusts filters/sort, which calls updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort. 7) User may select a rate option via selectShippingRateOption (create_shipment=false) to mark it as preferred within the quote, remaining on this page without creating a shipment.

Workflow 2 – Create shipment as guest from scratch: 1) User lands on ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment (no quoteId). 2) Page loads search presets via getShippingSearchOptions. 3) User completes origin/destination and package details, and can optionally enter basic shipment value/insurance preferences. 4) User submits the form, which calls createShipmentQuote in mode='create_shipment'. 5) Page stores quote and rate options, updates URL with ?mode=create_shipment&quoteId=<quote.id>, and shows services. 6) User may refine via filters/sorting (updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort). 7) User selects a preferred service (selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=false). 8) User clicks 'Continue to Shipment Details', triggering selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true using the selected rate option. 9) When a Shipment is returned with shipment_created=true, page navigates to shipment_details.html?shipmentId=<shipment.id>.

Workflow 3 – Start from a saved origin address and create a shipment draft: 1) User clicks a 'Ship from this address' action elsewhere, arriving at ship_rates.html?mode=create_shipment&origin_address_id=<id>. 2) On load, the page calls getSavedAddresses (with a suitable address_type if needed) and finds the Address with the matching id, then prefills origin city/postal_code/country in the search form. 3) User confirms or adjusts destination and package data, optionally sets shipment value/insurance preferences. 4) User submits, calling createShipmentQuote in create_shipment mode. 5) User selects a service and clicks 'Save as Draft' instead of continuing. 6) If no Shipment exists yet for this quote, the page first calls selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true to obtain a Shipment. 7) The page then calls saveShipmentAsDraft with the returned shipmentId and shows the confirmation message, keeping the user on this page with the quote and selected option visible.

Workflow 4 – Reload an existing quote and continue as guest: 1) User returns from account_overview.html or another location via ship_rates.html?mode=quote&quoteId=<existingQuoteId>. 2) On load, the page calls getShipmentQuoteDetails to reconstruct the ShipmentQuote and rate options. 3) User reviews or changes filters and sorting via updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort. 4) If the user now wishes to create a shipment as a guest, they use the 'Continue as Guest' control to switch to create_shipment mode. 5) Page updates URL to mode=create_shipment&quoteId=<sameQuoteId> and may call selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true for the currently selected rate option to create a Shipment. 6) Once a Shipment is created, the user can either navigate to shipment_details.html?shipmentId=<shipment.id> or save the shipment as a draft via saveShipmentAsDraft.

Workflow 5 – Adjust filters and sort using URL-stable quoteId: 1) User is on ship_rates.html with an existing quoteId in the URL. 2) User changes delivery time, price, or CO2 filters or selects a sort option. 3) Page calls updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort with current quoteId and selected filter/sort values. 4) API returns the updated ShipmentQuote and filtered/sorted ShippingRateOption list. 5) Page replaces the rate list and keeps the same quoteId in the URL so the state is restored if the user refreshes or returns via browser navigation.
interactions: User toggles between 'Get a Quote' and 'Create Shipment as Guest' modes via a mode selector component; this updates in-page mode state, changes form labels as needed, and updates the mode URL parameter while ensuring subsequent createShipmentQuote calls use the selected mode.

User optionally clicks a 'Use saved origin address' control that triggers getSavedAddresses and displays selectable Address records; selecting one fills origin city/postal_code/country and, if the origin_address_id URL parameter is present, the matching address is auto-highlighted.

User fills in origin and destination city, postal code, and country, as well as package type, shipment type, weight, units, and optionally length/width/height and dimension units, then submits the search form; this triggers createShipmentQuote and disables the form until a response is received.

On successful quote creation or loading, the page displays a list of ShippingRateOption rows; clicking a row or its 'Select service' action calls selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=false to mark that option as selected on the ShipmentQuote and within the rate list.

User adjusts filters for maximum delivery time, maximum price, and maximum CO2 via controls that use presets and sort options provided by getShippingSearchOptions; clicking an 'Apply filters' or changing a sort option calls updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort with the current quoteId and selected criteria.

In create_shipment mode, after selecting a rate option, user clicks a 'Continue to Shipment Details' button that calls selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true; when a Shipment is returned, the page redirects to shipment_details.html?shipmentId=<shipment.id>.

User may enter basic shipment value and insurance preferences in a dedicated section that conceptually maps to Shipment.declared_value, declared_value_currency, insurance_type, and insurance_included; these fields are associated with the current in-progress shipment context when the user chooses to create a draft or continue.

To save without proceeding, user clicks 'Save Shipment as Draft'; if a Shipment already exists in the page state, the component calls saveShipmentAsDraft with shipmentId; if not, it first ensures a Shipment exists by calling selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true, then calls saveShipmentAsDraft.

User can click a 'Continue as Guest' control when viewing rates in quote mode; this updates mode to create_shipment in the URL and initiates the creation of a Shipment via selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true for the currently selected rate option, enabling the continuation to shipment_details.html.
User can navigate back to the home page via a breadcrumb or 'Back to Home' interaction, which performs a client-side navigation to index.html without additional parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and live API responses, without relying on localStorage. On initial load and on any URL change, the page parses mode, quoteId, and origin_address_id. If mode is not present, a default mode is determined in-page (e.g., treating it as 'quote') but is written back to the URL so state is explicit. If quoteId is present, the page uses getShipmentQuoteDetails to load the corresponding ShipmentQuote and rate options, then sets internal state (including selected_rate_option_id) based on the response. If quoteId is absent, the page shows an empty search form and only loads preset configuration via getShippingSearchOptions. Whenever the user submits the search form, the page calls createShipmentQuote with the current mode and form fields; on success, it updates the browser URL to include mode and quoteId=<quote.id>, so refreshing or returning restores the same quote via getShipmentQuoteDetails. Changes to filters and sorting never change the URL but always call updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort with the current quoteId; the quoteId in the URL remains stable while filter state is stored in the quote returned by the API. When the user switches modes (e.g., via 'Continue as Guest'), the page updates the mode parameter in the URL and may reuse the same quoteId; subsequent quote-related operations always use the mode stored in the ShipmentQuote, ensuring consistent behaviour. If origin_address_id is present, the page calls getSavedAddresses and searches for a matching Address.id to prefill origin fields; this does not alter quoteId or mode but does affect initial form values. Navigation to shipment_details.html includes shipmentId as a URL parameter derived from the Shipment returned by selectShippingRateOption when create_shipment=true. Navigation back to ship_rates.html with a quoteId parameter (e.g., from another page) always reloads that quote via getShipmentQuoteDetails, ensuring that the rate options and selected service are reconstructed solely from server data.

components.functionality: Initializes and coordinates page state based on URL parameters and API responses. On load or URL changes, it reads mode, quoteId, and origin_address_id, calls getShippingSearchOptions for configuration, getShipmentQuoteDetails when quoteId is present, and getSavedAddresses when origin_address_id needs resolution. It exposes the current ShipmentQuote, list of ShippingRateOption items, resolved origin Address (if any), and current mode to other components.

Allows the user to switch between 'quote' and 'create_shipment' modes, and exposes a clear 'Continue as Guest' action when in quote mode. Changing the mode updates internal state used by the shipment search form and triggers a URL update so that mode is reflected as a query parameter.

Manages origin prefill from saved addresses. When origin_address_id is present, it uses getSavedAddresses to load Address records and selects the one with matching id to populate origin city, postal_code, and country in the shipment search form. It also provides a user-triggered control to open a list of saved addresses and select one to prefill the origin.

Captures search criteria to create a ShipmentQuote. Fields include origin_city, origin_postal_code, origin_country, destination_city, destination_postal_code, destination_country, package_type, shipment_type, weight, weight_unit, optional length/width/height, dimension_unit, and shipment_date. On submit, it calls createShipmentQuote using the current mode from the mode-selector and updates the central state with the returned quote and rate options.

Displays and manages filter and sort options for rate results. It uses delivery_time_filter_presets and sort_options from getShippingSearchOptions to configure available controls. When the user changes maximum delivery time, price, CO2 limit, or sort option, it calls updateShipmentQuoteFiltersAndSort with the current quoteId and the selected values, and then updates the shared ShipmentQuote and ShippingRateOption list with the response.

Renders the list of available ShippingRateOption entries for the current quote, including service_name, service_level, delivery estimates, price, currency, CO2 impact, and any duties-related flags. Highlights the option whose id matches ShipmentQuote.selected_rate_option_id or ShippingRateOption.is_selected. Selecting an option triggers selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=false to update the quote and mark the option as selected.

Shows a concise summary of the current quote: origin and destination (city, postal_code, country), package_type, shipment_type, weight and dimensions, shipment_date, and the currently selected ShippingRateOption if any. It updates automatically when the ShipmentQuote or selected rate option changes, helping the user confirm details before continuing or saving as draft.

Captures basic shipment value and insurance preference fields for users intending to create a shipment or save a draft, particularly when starting from a saved address. It presents fields conceptually mapped to Shipment.declared_value, Shipment.declared_value_currency, Shipment.insurance_type, and Shipment.insurance_included. The values are kept in the in-page shipment context so they can be associated with the Shipment when it is created via selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true or handled on subsequent pages.

Provides primary actions based on mode and current selection: in create_shipment mode, a 'Continue to Shipment Details' action creates a Shipment via selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true for the selected rate option and then navigates to shipment_details.html?shipmentId=<shipment.id>. It also includes a 'Save Shipment as Draft' action that calls saveShipmentAsDraft with the current shipmentId after ensuring a Shipment exists for the quote. In quote mode, it exposes a 'Continue as Guest' action that updates the mode and, if a rate option is selected, initiates Shipment creation via selectShippingRateOption with create_shipment=true.

Provides minimal navigational links such as a breadcrumb to index.html and a self-link to reload the current quote (ship_rates.html?mode=quote&quoteId=<quote.id>) when a quote exists. The self-link allows the user to easily re-open the same quote in another tab or share the URL, relying on getShipmentQuoteDetails for reconstruction.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a top-to-bottom, marketing-style flow adapted to a functional rate-search experience. A shallow utility area (breadcrumbs) sits just below the global header, followed by a dominant, hero-like rate search section that fills most of the initial viewport. Within this hero, the main shipment search form and related controls are grouped in a left-aligned content column, while supplemental elements (quote/actions rail, background imagery, right floating actions toolbar) sit on the right. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a two-column results area: rate results on the left and a stacked set of supporting panels (quote summary and value/insurance) on the right, with a horizontal filter/sort bar bridging them at the top.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a top-to-bottom, marketing-style flow adapted to a functional rate-search experience. A shallow utility area (breadcrumbs) sits just below the global header, followed by a dominant, hero-like rate search section that fills most of the initial viewport. Within this hero, the main shipment search form and related controls are grouped in a left-aligned content column, while supplemental elements (quote/actions rail, background imagery, right floating actions toolbar) sit on the right. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a two-column results area: rate results on the left and a stacked set of supporting panels (quote summary and value/insurance) on the right, with a horizontal filter/sort bar bridging them at the top.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The primary content container is centered and constrained to roughly 1140px within a full-width background. The hero/rate-search section occupies about 70–80% of the initial viewport height. Within that, the shipment-search-form takes the left ~55–60% of the content width, while the right ~40–45% is visually lighter, showing global/satellite imagery plus the vertical shipment-actions-panel floating near the right edge. Below the hero, the results area also uses a 2-column split: rate-results-list spans roughly 65–70% of the content width on the left, quote-summary-panel and value-insurance-panel share the remaining 30–35% on the right. Vertically, large gaps of 48–64px separate major bands (breadcrumbs → hero, hero → filters, filters → list/footer), and inner elements use 16–32px spacing for open, breathable composition.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a spacious marketing feel despite its functional nature. The hero search form uses large labels, clear grouping, and generous vertical spacing (16–24px between rows), with one strong primary CTA. Interactive rows like the filter-sort-bar and rate result cards have ample padding (16–24px) and clear separation (16–24px gaps) to avoid a dense, tabular impression. The right column panels are compact but not cramped, with 16px interior padding and 24px gaps between sections. Overall density is low to medium: enough information for comparison, but expressed through cards and bands rather than tight tables.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern. The eye starts at the top left (breadcrumbs under the site header), moves horizontally across to any top-aligned mode-selector, then diagonally down to the left-focused hero block where the shipment-search-form and its headline/CTA form the primary focal point. From there, attention travels to the right, noticing the background imagery and the vertical shipment-actions-panel. As users scroll, the eye follows the horizontal filter-sort-bar, then drops down through the left-column rate-results-list while periodically checking the right-column quote-summary-panel and value-insurance-panel. The floating right-side actions panel remains a subtle beacon for key actions, encouraging forward movement (start shipment, save quote) as users explore rates.

layout.overall_description: The “Ship & Get Rates” page reads like a functional hero wrapped in a modern corporate marketing shell. Below the global header, a slim breadcrumb line anchors the experience to the left, echoing the thin utility strip from the mockup. Immediately beneath, a full-width hero band spans the viewport, with a darkened, tech-forward world map or satellite image bleeding edge to edge. Inside the centered content container, the left side is dominated by a large, card-like rate search form framed in white, with a bold page title and a single strong orange CTA at the bottom. To the right, the hero image remains more open, allowing visual breathing room; a slim, vertical shipment-actions-panel floats near the right edge of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s vertical contact bar. Scrolling down, the hero dissolves into a two-column results area: a filter/sort strip runs horizontally across the container, with the rate results stacking in clean, card-like rows on the left and a tidy, sticky right column of quote summary and value/insurance options on the right. The overall impression is of a structured, grid-driven page where a few key elements—the search form, primary CTA, and right-side actions rail—guide the user’s gaze and decisions while preserving the modern, spacious, technology-forward feel of the original design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-manager exists as an invisible, non-visual layer that underpins the entire layout. It should be conceptually anchored at the page root, above the main content container in the DOM hierarchy but not occupying any visual space. It spans the full width of the page and height of the document, but with no rendered UI, no padding, and no margins. All other components sit visually on top of it, while it manages modes (e.g., guest vs logged-in behaviors, search vs results emphasis) and synchronizes state across the hero search form, results list, and right-column panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered controller component; no visible footprint, but logically fixed relative to the page and persisting as users scroll or interact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mode-selector appears near the top of the main content area, just below the global header and aligned within the central 1140px content container. It sits above the hero search form but below the navigation-breadcrumbs, functioning like a subtle, segmented toggle bar. Horizontally, it is centered in the container and spans roughly 40–50% of the content width (e.g., 460–560px), presented as pill-shaped toggles (such as “Ship & Get Rates” and another mode). Vertically, it is given a comfortable 24–32px margin above and below, so it feels airy and not cramped against the hero form. On larger screens, the mode-selector and breadcrumbs align along the same left baseline, but the selector’s content remains visually centered, preserving the modern, balanced marketing look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; scrolls normally with the page. On smaller viewports, it can expand toward 80–100% width while remaining centered and stacking closer to the hero search form to preserve vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The origin-saved-address-selector is embedded as a prominent first-row control inside the shipment-search-form on the left side of the hero. Within the form’s white card, it occupies roughly half to two-thirds of the row width (about 50–66% of the inner form width), aligned to the left with a clear label above and a large, single-line dropdown or button group below. It sits near the top of the form, about 16–24px beneath the form title or rate search heading, providing an obvious way to prefill the origin. To its right, there may be another field or it may leave open space, reinforcing the spacious layout and making the prefill selector easy to find without visual clutter. The vertical spacing above and below this row follows the hero’s generous rhythm, with about 16–20px gap to the next row of form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully contained within the shipment-search-form; it does not move independently. On narrower screens, it stacks full-width, aligned left, occupying an entire row for clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipment-search-form is the primary hero element, positioned within the full-width hero band that occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height. Inside the centered 1140px content container, this form appears as a large, white card anchored to the left, spanning around 55–60% of the container width and rising to about 60–70% of the hero’s height. It begins 48–64px below the breadcrumbs/mode-selector band, giving room for the hero image to breathe around it. The form has generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and vertically stacked field groups with 16–24px gaps between rows, reflecting the spacious marketing style. At the top of the card, a clear page title like “Ship & Get Rates” and a short supporting line are left-aligned, followed by key input sections including the origin-saved-address-selector, destination, package details, and any advanced options. The primary orange CTA button spans most of the card width near the bottom (about 70–90% of the inner width), with a secondary link or subtle button to its right or below, separated by 8–16px. To the right of the card, the hero’s background imagery—global map, routes, or satellite—remains visible, reinforcing the corporate, technology-forward atmosphere. The edges of the card do not touch the container sides; instead, there is a 32–48px horizontal gap between the card’s right edge and the imaginary midpoint of the container, preserving the left-weighted layout seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; scrolls away as users move down. On large screens it maintains its left-anchored, 55–60% width; on medium and small viewports, it can grow toward 90–100% width and move toward center alignment while still sitting on top of a simplified or partially cropped background image.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sort-bar is a horizontal control strip that marks the transition between the hero search section and the results area. It is positioned directly below the hero band, aligned to the same centered 1140px container. Vertically, there is a clear 32–48px gap between the bottom of the shipment-search-form (or the point at which the hero ends) and the top of the filter-sort-bar, giving a sense of stepping from input to exploration. The bar spans nearly the full width of the content container (about 90–100%), but its active controls (sort dropdowns, filter toggles, pills) align primarily on the left two-thirds, leaving some open space on the right for a clean, modern look. It has a subtle background (slightly elevated or tinted) with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and is visually separated from the content below by a thin border or shadow. In the layout hierarchy, it sits directly above and flush with the left edge of the rate-results-list, while its right side aligns roughly with the right edge of the quote-summary column, acting as a visual bridge between the two columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky or semi-sticky band on larger screens: when users scroll past it, it can pin to the top of the viewport (just below the main site header) so filters and sorting remain accessible. On smaller screens, it may become a horizontally scrollable chip row or a collapsible panel while preserving its position at the top of the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rate-results-list occupies the main left column of the post-hero content area. It begins immediately below the filter-sort-bar with a modest 16–24px vertical gap, maintaining continuity. Within the centered 1140px container, the list column is left-aligned and spans about 65–70% of the width, mirroring the left dominance seen in the hero section. Each rate result appears as a clean, card-like row running nearly the full width of this column, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between cards. Information within each card is structured in horizontal bands and columns (carrier, transit time, price, options) but laid out loosely enough to avoid feeling tabular. The top of the list aligns with the left edge of the shipment-search-form above, creating a consistent vertical gutter. As the user scrolls, the cards form a long column that visually balances against the right-column quote-summary-panel and value-insurance-panel, with the right column taking up roughly the remaining 30–35% of the container width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main document flow. As the user scrolls, this column continues while the right-column panels may remain sticky to maintain context. On smaller screens, the list expands to nearly 100% width, and the right-column panels stack below the list, remaining clearly separated with generous 24–32px gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-summary-panel is the leading element in the right-hand supporting column that sits alongside the rate-results-list. It is positioned so that its top aligns roughly with the top of the first visible rate-results-card, immediately to the right of the list column, within the same 1140px container. This panel spans about 30–35% of the container width and appears as a compact, white card with 16–24px internal padding and a clear heading like “Quote Summary”. There is a 24–32px horizontal gap between the left edge of this panel and the right edge of the rate-results-list, maintaining the spacious two-column layout. Vertically, it is separated from the filter-sort-bar above by about 16–24px and from any panel below (such as the value-insurance-panel) by 16–24px. Its contents are stacked vertically (selected service, price, estimated delivery, key fees) with 8–12px gaps between rows, keeping a concise but not crowded feel. This panel acts as the user’s anchor for the currently selected rate, updating as they interact with the list on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within its column on larger viewports: as the user scrolls down the results, the quote-summary-panel remains fixed in view until the right column’s content reaches the page footer, ensuring the summary is always visible. On smaller screens, it loses its sticky behavior and appears as a full-width card below the rate-results-list, maintaining adequate spacing above and below (24–32px) for clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The value-insurance-panel is stacked directly beneath the quote-summary-panel within the right-hand column, sharing the same width (about 30–35% of the container) and right alignment. It is separated from the quote summary by a 16–24px vertical gap, creating a clear but connected grouping. This panel has a similar card treatment—white background, subtle border or shadow, and 16–24px internal padding. Its internal layout is vertically oriented, with an input for declared value, optional insurance toggles, and contextual info or price changes presented in concise rows with 8–12px spacing. While related, it is visually subordinate to the quote summary, perhaps using smaller typography or a lighter header. The left edge of this panel aligns perfectly with the left edge of the quote summary above, maintaining a clean, columnar rhythm that balances the taller stack of rate results on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Typically scrolls in tandem with the quote-summary-panel within the sticky right column. If the quote-summary-panel is sticky, the value-insurance-panel may scroll underneath it within the same column, becoming fully visible as the user nears the lower part of the results. On smaller screens, it appears as a separate full-width card directly following the quote-summary-panel, all underneath the rate-results-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipment-actions-panel borrows from the mockup’s vertical contact toolbar and is positioned as a slim, floating action bar along the far right side of the viewport. It sits outside the central content container, hugging the right edge of the screen at about mid-height of the hero section, overlapping both the hero and, as the user scrolls, the upper area of the results section. The panel occupies roughly 5–7% of the viewport width but is visually constrained to a vertical strip of fixed width (e.g., 56–72px), containing stacked circular or square icons/buttons for key actions like “Save Quote”, “Start Shipment”, or “Contact Support”. The icons are centered within the strip with 8–12px padding inside each button and 8–16px vertical gaps between them, and the strip itself has 12–16px padding from the viewport edge. It floats above the hero’s background imagery on the right, providing a visual counterweight to the left-aligned shipment-search-form and echoing the original design’s right vertical contact bar. As users scroll into the results, the bar remains visible, gently reinforcing the next-step actions without intruding into the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed or sticky relative to the viewport on larger screens: it remains in a consistent vertical position (roughly mid-viewport) and at a fixed offset from the right edge as the page scrolls. On smaller screens or touch devices, it can compress into a single floating action button anchored bottom-right, expanding into a mini menu when tapped, to preserve screen real estate while maintaining the same functional role.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipment-actions-panel borrows from the mockup’s vertical contact toolbar and is positioned as a slim, floating action bar along the far right side of the viewport. It sits outside the central content container, hugging the right edge of the screen at about mid-height of the hero section, overlapping both the hero and, as the user scrolls, the upper area of the results section. The panel occupies roughly 5–7% of the viewport width but is visually constrained to a vertical strip of fixed width (e.g., 56–72px), containing stacked circular or square icons/buttons for key actions like “Save Quote”, “Start Shipment”, or “Contact Support”. The icons are centered within the strip with 8–12px padding inside each button and 8–16px vertical gaps between them, and the strip itself has 12–16px padding from the viewport edge. It floats above the hero’s background imagery on the right, providing a visual counterweight to the left-aligned shipment-search-form and echoing the original design’s right vertical contact bar. As users scroll into the results, the bar remains visible, gently reinforcing the next-step actions without intruding into the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed or sticky relative to the viewport on larger screens: it remains in a consistent vertical position (roughly mid-viewport) and at a fixed offset from the right edge as the page scrolls. On smaller screens or touch devices, it can compress into a single floating action button anchored bottom-right, expanding into a mini menu when tapped, to preserve screen real estate while maintaining the same functional role.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-breadcrumbs appear as a thin utility strip just below the global site header, echoing the mockup’s top utility bar. They are left-aligned within the centered 1140px content container and span about 60–70% of that width, with the breadcrumb trail starting at the far left. Vertically, the breadcrumbs sit with 16–24px padding above and below, creating a low-profile but readable band. The text is modest in size and weight, ensuring it doesn’t compete with the hero title but still provides clear location context. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the breadcrumb strip and the top of the mode-selector or hero search form, maintaining the layered hierarchy: header → breadcrumbs → mode/hero. The strip itself has a transparent or slightly tinted background that blends into the page, reinforcing its role as a utility element rather than a primary feature.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow, scrolling off-screen as the user moves down the page. On very small screens, it may wrap or truncate elegantly and reduce vertical padding to preserve space while remaining at the top of the content stack.

name: Shipment Details
title: Shipment Details
description: Review and complete shipment information, including sender and recipient details, shipment value, insurance, and duties, before proceeding to payment.

core_features: Load the shipment, selected service, and cost summary for editing using getShipmentDetailsForEdit(shipmentId).
Display the selected shipping service and rate option details (price, delivery estimate, filters) in a read-only summary.
Collect and edit full sender (origin) and recipient (destination) address details, including name, company, street lines, city, postal code, country, phone, and email.
Allow selection of saved sender and recipient addresses from the Address Book using getSavedAddresses(address_type) and apply them to the shipment.
Capture or adjust shipment contents information (category and description) and declared value with currency.
Configure insurance (type and inclusion flag) and duties options (e.g., unpaid, prepaid, bill recipient) subject to capabilities of the selected service.
Update shipment details and recalculate cost summary (shipping, duties, insurance, total) using updateShipmentDetails.
Show a dynamic cost summary using values returned from getShipmentDetailsForEdit and updateShipmentDetails.
Save the shipment as a draft without paying via saveShipmentAsDraft and return to account overview.
Navigate to payment.html with the current shipmentId after validation and saving of shipment details.
Navigate back to ship_rates.html with the related quoteId and mode=create_shipment to change the selected service.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Complete shipment details after selecting a rate
1. User arrives from ship_rates.html with URL parameter shipmentId.
2. On page load, the system calls getShipmentDetailsForEdit(shipmentId).
3. The page displays the selected service summary, current origin and destination addresses, shipment content/value, insurance, duties option, and cost summary.
4. User reviews and, if needed, edits sender and recipient addresses in the respective forms.
5. If desired, user opens the Address Book for sender or recipient, triggers getSavedAddresses(address_type), selects an address, and the address form is populated with that data.
6. User fills or edits shipment contents, declared value, and currency, then selects insurance type and duties option.
7. User clicks a "Save changes" or equivalent action (explicit or triggered on "Continue to Payment").
8. The page validates required fields; if valid, it calls updateShipmentDetails with shipmentId, origin_address, destination_address, declared_value, declared_value_currency, insurance_type, insurance_included, and duties_option.
9. On success, the page updates bound shipment and cost_summary data.
10. User clicks "Continue to Payment", and the page navigates to payment.html?shipmentId=<current_shipmentId>.
Workflow 2: Edit an existing draft shipment from account overview
1. User clicks a shipment in account_overview.html that links to shipment_details.html?shipmentId=<id>.
2. Page calls getShipmentDetailsForEdit(shipmentId) and populates all fields as in Workflow 1.
3. User modifies addresses, insurance, or duties options.
4. User can click "Save as draft" at any time; the page calls saveShipmentAsDraft(shipmentId).
5. On success, the page may show a confirmation message and/or automatically navigate back to account_overview.html.
6. Alternatively, user can proceed to payment by clicking "Continue to Payment", triggering an updateShipmentDetails call (if there are unsaved changes) before navigating to payment.html.
Workflow 3: Change selected shipping service
1. User is on shipment_details.html with a loaded shipment and decides the service is not ideal.
2. User clicks "Change service" in the service summary component.
3. The component uses the currently loaded selected_rate_option.quote_id (from getShipmentDetailsForEdit) as relatedQuoteId.
4. The page navigates to ship_rates.html?quoteId=<relatedQuoteId>&mode=create_shipment to allow the user to pick a different rate.
5. After the new rate is selected and the shipment is updated on the backend, the user is redirected back to shipment_details.html?shipmentId=<id>, where getShipmentDetailsForEdit is called again to refresh the data.
Workflow 4: Auto-recalculate costs while editing
1. User modifies declared value, insurance, or duties option.
2. On appropriate events (e.g., field blur or clicking an explicit "Update costs" button), the page validates minimal input required for calculation.
3. The page calls updateShipmentDetails with the current form values.
4. The returned shipment and cost_summary are rebound to the UI so the user sees updated shipping charges, duties, insurance fee, and total before moving to payment.
interactions: On page load: When shipment_details.html is opened with a shipmentId query parameter, a page-level initialization handler calls getShipmentDetailsForEdit(shipmentId). While awaiting response, a loading state is shown. On success, components bind to the returned shipment, origin_address, destination_address, selected_rate_option, service, and cost_summary. On error, an error banner is displayed with retry capability.

Edit sender address: User types into sender address form fields (name, company, street_line1, street_line2, city, state_province, postal_code, country, phone, email). Each input updates a local form state bound to an origin_address object that mirrors the Address model.

Open sender Address Book: User clicks a 'Use saved address' action in the sender section. This opens an address-book modal and triggers getSavedAddresses with an appropriate address_type value (for example, 'personal' or 'business' depending on implementation). The resulting Address list is displayed as selectable items.

Select sender address from Address Book: User selects one Address entry in the modal. The modal closes and the sender address form fields are populated with the chosen Address data. The local origin_address form state is updated, but the backend is not called yet until an explicit update (e.g., on Save or Continue).

Edit recipient address: Same pattern as sender; user edits destination address form and optionally uses the Address Book (with address_type value such as 'recipient'). Selecting an address populates the recipient form.

Edit shipment contents and value: User selects a contents_category from allowed Shipment.contents_category values and enters a free-text contents_description. User enters declared_value and selects declared_value_currency from the supported currency options. These changes modify a local shipment details form state (mirroring Shipment fields) and are later sent via updateShipmentDetails.

Configure insurance: User chooses an insurance_type option (e.g., 'none', 'full_value', 'partial') and toggles insurance_included if applicable. Validation may ensure that if insurance_type is not 'none', a positive declared_value is provided. These fields map to Shipment.insurance_type and Shipment.insurance_included and are passed to updateShipmentDetails.

Configure duties option: User selects a duties_option (e.g., 'unpaid', 'prepaid', 'bill_recipient') via a radio group or select input. The set of available options may be conditionally constrained by service.supports_prepaid_duties or selected_rate_option.supports_prepaid_duties (for example, disabling or hiding 'prepaid' when not supported). The selected value is sent to updateShipmentDetails.

Update shipment and recalculate costs: User clicks an explicit 'Save changes' or 'Update costs' button (or this may occur immediately when clicking 'Continue to Payment').
