On the left half, a large, bold confirmation headline and brief summary text clearly state that the shipment is confirmed, with a prominent orange primary button for accessing the shipping label (and/or tracking) placed just beneath, surrounded by generous vertical spacing. On the right half of the hero, a compact but visually strong identifiers card displays the tracking number, shipment ID, and any quick status badges, framed as a clean, elevated white panel against the darker background. Below this hero band, the page transitions into a white, card-based content area with a 2/3–1/3 column layout: the left column continues the narrative of where the shipment is going and how it will get there (route, service level, delivery estimate, and post-purchase actions), while the right column provides financial and account-adjacent details (payment receipt and saving/sharing shipment info). A subtle right-edge floating contact/assistance toolbar aligns vertically with the hero content, reinforcing the brand’s global, responsive service persona. Throughout, large vertical gaps, clear card boundaries, and minimal clutter keep the page feeling calm and authoritative, while the strong orange CTA moments inject energy and guide the user to the next steps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay centered over the entire main content area, beginning just below the primary navigation bar and covering from the top of the hero confirmation section down past the fold. It spans 100% of the viewport width and height while active, dimming the underlying hero and content panels. At the center of the viewport, a compact loading indicator (spinner or progress element) and short status text are displayed within a small, rounded white card (approximately 320–400px wide) with 24–32px padding, ensuring legibility against the darker background. The overlay edges align with the browser edges rather than the 1140px content container, emphasizing that all content beneath is temporarily inactive. When loading completes, this overlay fades out to reveal the hero confirmation section already laid out beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed full-screen overlay; positioned above all other components while active with a high z-index, not scrolling with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the hero confirmation band, the confirmation status summary sits in the left portion of the centered content container, aligned to the left edge of the ~1140px grid. It occupies roughly 55–60% of the container width and is vertically positioned in the upper half of the hero, with a comfortable 48–64px margin from the top of the hero section. The content is stacked vertically: a small success icon or label at the top, followed by a large, bold headline (e.g., “Shipment Confirmed”) that is the most visually dominant text on the page, then a concise explanatory line. Directly beneath this, with about 24–32px of vertical spacing, the top area of the label-access panel (primary CTA) is visually integrated—either as part of this block or immediately adjacent below—creating a strong connection between the success message and the next step. This block sits over the darkened hero background, ensuring high contrast (white or light text on deep blue), and leaves generous space on its right side for the identifiers card, preserving the open, marketing-style hero feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; participates in normal page scroll, remaining at the top once the user scrolls past the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipment identifiers panel is positioned within the hero confirmation band on the right side of the 1140px content container, horizontally aligned with the confirmation-status-summary block but occupying the remaining 40–45% of the width. It appears as a raised, white card with subtle shadow and rounded corners, clearly separated from the dark background. Vertically, it is centered relative to the confirmation headline on the left, so that the eye can travel from the left headline across to this panel in a straight horizontal line, following the F-pattern. Inside, tracking IDs, barcode or QR code, and key shipment references are arranged with a comfortable 16–24px internal padding and stacked in 2–4 concise rows. The card maintains at least 32px of padding from the right edge of the content container and 24–32px from the top/bottom edges of the hero, ensuring it does not feel cramped. On wide screens, it appears as a clear right-side anchor; on narrower breakpoints, it would drop below the confirmation-status-summary, but still remain within the hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; scrolls with the page, with no sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Below the hero confirmation band, in the main white content area, the shipment route and service panel is the first card in the left column. The content container continues at ~1140px width, and the left column takes about 2/3 of that (roughly 8 of 12 grid columns), aligned with the left edge of the hero content. A 48–64px vertical gap separates the hero’s bottom edge from this panel, maintaining a clear section break. The panel itself spans the full width of the left column, with 24–32px internal padding, and presents origin-destination information, route visualization, and service-level details in a clean, horizontal layout (e.g., origin on the left, arrow or route graphic in the middle, destination on the right) with sufficient whitespace between each element. This card creates the first step in the narrative below the confirmation, inviting the eye down and slightly left in continuation of the F/Z-pattern. It maintains a 24–32px gap below for the delivery estimate panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main content flow; scrolls with the page within the left column of the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the shipment route and service panel in the left column, the delivery estimate panel is stacked with a consistent 24–32px vertical gap. It matches the width of the left column (approximately 2/3 of the content container) and uses a card style consistent with other panels—white background, subtle shadow or border, and rounded corners. Inside, the estimated delivery date/time and any time window or reliability indicators are displayed prominently near the top, possibly with a tertiary highlight color, with supporting text or notes below. The content density remains low, with the key date presented in larger type and secondary text spaced with 8–16px increments. This panel visually ties to the route panel above, forming a cohesive story about where and when the shipment will arrive. It keeps at least 24px of spacing beneath it before the next left-column panel, maintaining the clean, spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main content flow; scrolls naturally within the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The label access panel is tightly integrated with the hero’s left column, sitting directly below the confirmation-status-summary headline and subtext, within the same left-side hero column. Horizontally, it aligns with the left grid edge and spans roughly 60% of the content width, extending slightly farther to the right than the text above to emphasize its importance as the main CTA block. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of space between the bottom of the confirmation status text and the top of this panel, ensuring a clear but close connection. The panel appears either as a high-contrast pill/button row or as a shallow card with a bold orange primary button labeled “Download Label” or similar, accompanied by secondary links (e.g., “Print Label”, “Email Label”) arranged horizontally or as compact text links beneath. It is the most eye-catching interactive element in the hero, with sufficient vertical padding (12–16px) inside buttons and ample whitespace around them, anchoring the user’s next action. On scroll, the panel moves with the hero; below the hero, it is not repeated, so this hero-level placement makes label access feel immediate and prominent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; scrolls away with the hero content on page scroll, without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the main content area below the hero, the payment receipt panel occupies the top of the right column, directly opposite the shipment route and service panel. The right column takes about 1/3 of the content container width (roughly 4 of 12 columns) and is aligned flush with the right edge of the 1140px grid. A 48–64px vertical gap from the bottom of the hero mirrors the spacing on the left side. The payment receipt panel appears as a vertically oriented card with 24–32px internal padding, listing payment method, amount, date/time, and reference IDs in a tidy, low-density arrangement. Headings are aligned left within the card, with values aligned either in a simple vertical stack or in a two-column label/value layout. The panel’s height is visually balanced with the shipment route panel to its left, forming a coherent horizontal row across the page. There is a 24–32px gap below this panel before the shipment-save-info-panel that follows in the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with the page like other content cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Further down the left column, beneath the delivery estimate panel, the post-purchase actions panel is spaced with a 24–32px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the left column and serves as a structured call-to-action area for tasks like tracking the shipment, scheduling pickup, and finding drop-off locations. The panel is designed as a spacious, multi-action card: a short title aligned top-left, followed by 2–4 horizontally arranged action blocks or buttons (e.g., large orange primary button for tracking, smaller outlined buttons for scheduling and location search). Each action has sufficient padding (12–16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal) and at least 16–24px of horizontal spacing between them. This panel visually reintroduces the brand’s accent color to sustain energy below the hero and represents the main actionable area below the fold, pulling the eye diagonally from the left column further down and slightly towards the right as actions might extend in that direction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; scrolls normally with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Under the payment receipt panel in the right column, the shipment save info panel is stacked with a 24–32px vertical gap. It matches the width of the right column and uses the same card style as other panels. Positioned opposite or slightly lower than the post-purchase actions panel on the left, it provides options to save shipment details (e.g., save to account, export, or share) in a compact but clear layout. The top of the card contains a short title, with one or two rows of concise controls (checkbox to save shipment to profile, link/button to download receipt or share details) arranged in a vertical stack with 8–16px spacing. Because this is supportive rather than core to the shipment’s journey, the visual treatment is more subdued: fewer accent colors, smaller buttons or links, and more reliance on body text. This placement keeps account and documentation actions accessible without competing with the main CTAs on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with other content panels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notification appears as a slim, full-width message band directly below the main site navigation and above the hero confirmation section, aligning with the behavior of global alerts in the mockup. It spans the full browser width but its content is centered within the same ~1140px container, maintaining consistent alignment with the hero and subsequent panels. The band’s height is relatively shallow (around 40–56px), following the thin utility strip pattern, and uses a background color distinct from the hero (e.g., a muted red or amber) to indicate an issue while still fitting into the overall palette. Text is left-aligned within the container with a small icon on the far left and a close/dismiss control on the far right, both aligned vertically center. A 16–24px vertical gap separates this band from the top of the hero section, preventing the page from feeling cramped. When the notification is absent, this vertical space collapses, letting the hero align closer to the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top of the content area while visible, pushing the hero section down; remains fixed just under the main navigation on scroll until dismissed or until the user scrolls past it, then scrolls out of view.

name: Schedule Pickup
title: Schedule Pickup
description: Page for creating and confirming a courier pickup request for one or more packages, including pre-labeled shipments, by entering pickup address, package details, date, time slot, and contact information.

core_features: Allow users to choose the pickup type (pre-labeled, need labels, return pickup) and set whether they already have shipping labels, mapped to PickupRequest.pickup_type and PickupRequest.has_shipping_labels.

Capture and optionally prefill the pickup address (contact name, company, street, city, state/province, postal code, country) and bind it to the Address model that will be sent within createPickupRequest.pickup_address.
Collect package details including number of packages, total weight, and weight unit, mapped to PickupRequest.package_count, PickupRequest.total_weight, and PickupRequest.weight_unit.
Provide a date picker for selecting the pickup date, bound to PickupRequest.pickup_date and used as input to getAvailablePickupTimeSlots.

Call getAvailablePickupTimeSlots with postal_code, city, country, and pickup_date from the pickup address and date picker, then display the returned PickupTimeSlot entries and allow the user to select one available time slot (e.g., an afternoon window).
Capture contact information (contact_name, phone, email) for pickup notifications, mapped to PickupRequest.contact_name, PickupRequest.phone, and PickupRequest.email.
Allow entering optional special instructions for the courier, mapped to PickupRequest.special_instructions.

Call createPickupRequest with the collected pickup_type, has_shipping_labels, pickup_address, package_count, total_weight, weight_unit, pickup_date, selected timeSlotId, contact_phone, contact_email, and special_instructions to create and schedule the pickup.
Display an on-page confirmation summary using the PickupRequest returned from createPickupRequest, including date, time window, address, and status, without navigating away from the page.

Provide contextual navigation buttons: a link back to the related shipment page (shipment_confirmation.html with shipmentId) when the page was opened with a shipmentId URL parameter, and a link to return to the home page (index.html) after confirmation.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Schedule pickup from an existing shipment (with shipmentId in URL): User clicks "Schedule Pickup" from shipment_confirmation.html or account_overview.html -> Browser opens pickup_schedule.html?shipmentId={id}[&from=shipment_confirmation|account_overview] -> Page initialization reads shipmentId from URL and uses server-prefilled origin Address (not via a client interface) to pre-populate the pickup-address form (contact, company, street, city, postal code, country) -> User reviews/edits pickup type and address fields as needed -> User enters package_count, total_weight, and selects weight_unit -> User selects a pickup_date via date picker -> User clicks to load available time slots, which triggers getAvailablePickupTimeSlots(postal_code, city, country, pickup_date) -> Page displays only is_available PickupTimeSlot entries; user selects one time slot -> User verifies or edits contact_name, phone, and email, and optionally enters special instructions -> User submits the pickup form, which calls createPickupRequest with all collected data -> On success, the page displays a confirmation summary panel and switches to a confirmed state on the same URL (optionally updating the URL to include pickupRequestId) -> User can click "Back to shipment" to navigate to shipment_confirmation.html?shipmentId={id} or "Return to Home" to go to index.html.

Workflow 2 – Schedule pickup from header navigation (no shipmentId): User opens pickup_schedule.html from the main navigation without a shipmentId URL parameter -> Page loads with empty pickup-type, address, package details, date, and contact fields -> User selects a pickup type -> User fills in the pickup address fields (including city, postal_code, and country) -> User enters package_count, total_weight, and weight_unit -> User selects a pickup_date via date picker -> User triggers loading of time slots for that date, which calls getAvailablePickupTimeSlots with address (postal_code, city, country) and pickup_date -> Page lists available PickupTimeSlot options; user selects one -> User fills in contact_name, phone, and email, and optionally special instructions -> User submits the form, calling createPickupRequest with the collected data -> On successful response, the confirmation summary is shown on the same page, and a "Return to Home" link is available (no "Back to shipment" link because shipmentId is not present).

Workflow 3 – Change pickup date/time before submitting: User has already entered address, package details, contact info, selected a pickup_date, and selected a time slot -> User decides to change the date -> On changing the pickup_date, previous time slot selection is cleared and the time-slot list is reset -> User triggers a new getAvailablePickupTimeSlots call with updated pickup_date and the same postal_code, city, and country -> New PickupTimeSlot options are displayed; user selects a new time slot -> User reviews the updated summary and submits, calling createPickupRequest with the new pickup_date and timeSlotId.

Workflow 4 – Handle missing or invalid fields: User attempts to submit the pickup form without completing required fields (pickup_type, address, package_count, total_weight, weight_unit, pickup_date, timeSlotId, phone, email) -> Client-side validation prevents the createPickupRequest call and displays inline messages indicating which fields are required -> After the user corrects the fields, the submit action is retried, and only then is createPickupRequest invoked.

Workflow 5 – Handle time-slot API errors: User has filled address and date and triggers getAvailablePickupTimeSlots -> The interface responds with an error or an empty list -> The page shows an error or no-availability message in a non-blocking error component; the time-slot list remains empty and prevents form submission until a valid time slot is selected -> User can modify the date or address and retry loading time slots, which triggers a new getAvailablePickupTimeSlots call.

Workflow 6 – Handle pickup creation errors and retry: User submits the form -> createPickupRequest is called -> The interface responds with an error (for example, validation or service failure) -> The page shows a clear error banner at the top of the form with the message from the interface (if available) and keeps the user’s input in all fields -> User corrects any highlighted issues or simply retries and clicks submit again -> On a successful createPickupRequest response, the page transitions to show the confirmation summary.
interactions: On page load, a state-initialization process reads URL parameters (e.g., shipmentId and an optional from flag) to determine whether the pickup is related to a specific shipment and whether the "Back to shipment" button should be displayed.

Changing the pickup_type field updates the internal state for PickupRequest.pickup_type and the derived has_shipping_labels field (for example, pre_labeled sets has_shipping_labels to true), and may update contextual help text in the form.

Editing pickup address fields (name, company_name, street_line1, street_line2, city, state_province, postal_code, country) updates a local Address object that will be passed as pickup_address to createPickupRequest and used as input to getAvailablePickupTimeSlots.
Entering package_count or total_weight and selecting weight_unit updates the local package details state; validation ensures values are numeric and greater than zero before allowing form submission.
Selecting a pickup_date via the date picker updates the local pickup_date state and clears any previously selected time slot; it enables the control that triggers getAvailablePickupTimeSlots.

Clicking the control to fetch available time slots (for the current address and date) invokes getAvailablePickupTimeSlots with the current postal_code, city, country, and pickup_date; while the request is in progress, the time-slot list area shows a loading state and disables further related actions.

When getAvailablePickupTimeSlots returns, the time-slot list component binds the returned array of PickupTimeSlot objects, filters to is_available entries, and renders them as selectable options; selecting one updates a selectedTimeSlotId state and displays the time window (time_slot_start, time_slot_end) in the summary.

Entering or editing contact_name, phone, and email fields updates the respective state for PickupRequest.contact_name, PickupRequest.phone, and PickupRequest.email; basic format validation is performed for phone and email before submission.
Typing in the special_instructions field updates the value that will be sent as special_instructions to createPickupRequest and may be displayed in the confirmation summary.

Clicking the main "Schedule pickup" submit button triggers client-side validation; if all required fields are valid and a time slot is selected, the page calls createPickupRequest with the accumulated state; while waiting for the response, the button and key input fields are disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful createPickupRequest response, the page stores the returned PickupRequest data in state, optionally updates the URL query string with pickupRequestId and a view indicator, hides or disables inputs, and shows a read-only confirmation summary component with key details and the pickup status.
If createPickupRequest returns an error, the error message (if provided) is displayed in a global error banner; the form remains editable, and the user can adjust entries or retry submission.

Clicking the "Back to shipment" button (when shipmentId is present in the URL) navigates the browser to shipment_confirmation.html?shipmentId={shipmentId}, using the same shipmentId value read from the current page’s URL.
Clicking the "Return to Home" button navigates to index.html, without additional URL parameters.

If the user refreshes the page after a successful pickup creation and the URL contains a pickupRequestId parameter, the server can render the page directly in confirmation mode with the corresponding PickupRequest data, avoiding the need to re-call createPickupRequest on the client.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is controlled through a combination of URL parameters and in-memory form state, without relying on localStorage. On initial load, the page reads the shipmentId URL parameter (if present) from pickup_schedule.html?shipmentId={id}[&from={source}] to determine: (1) whether to expect server-prefilled shipment and origin Address data for the pickup-address form, and (2) whether to display the "Back to shipment" navigation button, which will link to shipment_confirmation.html?shipmentId={id}. An optional from URL parameter (such as from=shipment_confirmation or from=account_overview) can be used to fine-tune back-navigation context, but this is not required for core functionality. During interaction, the user’s selections (pickup_type, address fields, package details, pickup_date, selectedTimeSlotId, contact info, special instructions) live in client-side component state and are only sent to the backend through the assigned interfaces: getAvailablePickupTimeSlots and createPickupRequest. No new interfaces are invoked. When the user successfully schedules a pickup, the client can update the URL using the History API to append a pickupRequestId parameter and a view or mode indicator (for example, view=confirmation) without reloading the page. If the page is later reloaded with these parameters, the server can use pickupRequestId and view=confirmation to pre-render the confirmation panel instead of an empty form, ensuring the user still sees their scheduled pickup; this server-side behavior does not require any additional client-side interface beyond the assigned ones. All subsequent navigation (e.g., to shipment_confirmation.html or index.html) passes necessary context through URL parameters only, maintaining a stateless client that does not use localStorage.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (shipmentId and optional from) on page load, initializes page-level state, and connects any server-prefilled Shipment and Address data to the pickup-address form and navigation logic. Determines whether the page is in form mode or confirmation mode based on server-rendered state and URL query (e.g., pickupRequestId, view).

Allows the user to select the pickup type (pre_labeled, need_labels, return_pickup). Updates the internal pickup_type field and derived has_shipping_labels flag that will be sent to createPickupRequest. May display contextual information based on selected type.

Captures pickup address details: contact name, company name, street lines, city, state/province, postal code, and country. If a shipmentId is provided and server has prefetched origin address, the form initializes with that Address data but remains editable. The resulting Address object is used both as pickup_address in createPickupRequest and as the source for postal_code, city, and country parameters for getAvailablePickupTimeSlots.

Collects package_count, total_weight, and weight_unit for the pickup request. Ensures that numeric fields are valid and that a weight_unit is selected. These values are mapped directly to PickupRequest.package_count, PickupRequest.total_weight, and PickupRequest.weight_unit and will be passed into createPickupRequest.

Provides a control for selecting the pickup date. Updates the local pickup_date state that is used both by getAvailablePickupTimeSlots and createPickupRequest. Changing the date clears any previously selected time slot and triggers validation to ensure the date is acceptable (e.g., not in the past, subject to business rules handled on the server).

Given a valid pickup_date and address (postal_code, city, country), calls the getAvailablePickupTimeSlots interface to retrieve all available pickup slots for that date and area. Manages loading and error states and forwards the resulting PickupTimeSlot array to the time-slot list component.

Displays the list of PickupTimeSlot entries returned by getAvailablePickupTimeSlots, filtered to those with is_available set to true. Allows the user to select exactly one time slot, updating the selected timeSlotId used by createPickupRequest and exposing the time_slot_start and time_slot_end for display in the summary and confirmation components.

Collects contact_name, phone, and email for the pickup request. These fields map to PickupRequest.contact_name, PickupRequest.phone, and PickupRequest.email and are passed to createPickupRequest (as contact_phone and contact_email parameters, with contact_name included within pickup_address or as a separate contact_name field depending on the implementation). Includes basic format validation for phone and email before allowing submission.

Provides a text area for optional special_instructions to the courier (e.g., building access notes or preferred pickup location at the site). The content is mapped to PickupRequest.special_instructions and sent through createPickupRequest.

Shows a live read-only summary of the pickup request before submission, including pickup_type, key address fields (city, postal code, country), package_count, total_weight and weight_unit, pickup_date, selected time window (from the chosen PickupTimeSlot), contact phone and email, and any special instructions. Updates automatically whenever any bound state changes and is used as a final review before calling createPickupRequest.

Wraps all pickup-related form sections and orchestrates validation and submission. On submit, validates all required fields and, if valid, calls createPickupRequest using the assembled payload: pickup_type, has_shipping_labels, pickup_address (from Address), package_count, total_weight, weight_unit, pickup_date, selected timeSlotId, contact_phone, contact_email, and special_instructions. Handles loading states, disables the submit button during the request, and routes success or error responses to the relevant components.

Displays after a successful createPickupRequest call or when the server renders the page in confirmation mode. Shows confirmed pickup details from the PickupRequest object, including pickup_date, time_slot_start, time_slot_end, pickup address summary, contact details, special instructions, and status. Hides or disables the editable form sections when active and provides navigation actions to go back to the related shipment (if shipmentId is known) or to the home page.

Provides context-aware navigation controls. When shipmentId is present in the URL, shows a "Back to shipment" link that navigates to shipment_confirmation.html?shipmentId={shipmentId}. Always offers a "Return to Home" link that navigates to index.html. May also include a generic "Cancel" or "Start over" action that reloads pickup_schedule.html without confirmation-related URL parameters.

Displays non-field-specific error messages resulting from getAvailablePickupTimeSlots or createPickupRequest failures. Receives error messages from those calls and shows them persistently or until dismissed by the user, without clearing form data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page sits inside the same full-width shell as the marketing mockup: thin utility strip at the very top, then the primary navigation bar, then a dominant hero-like content stage. Within that stage, content is arranged in a two-column layout inside a centered 12-column grid (max ~1140px width): the left column (about 7/12 width) holds the main Schedule Pickup flow (forms and selectors), while the right column (about 5/12 width) holds a persistent pickup summary panel. A narrow, floating vertical action/navigation bar hugs the far right edge of the viewport, independent of the main content. Overlays (loaders, error banner) appear on top of this structure without breaking it.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside the same full-width shell as the marketing mockup: thin utility strip at the very top, then the primary navigation bar, then a dominant hero-like content stage. Within that stage, content is arranged in a two-column layout inside a centered 12-column grid (max ~1140px width): the left column (about 7/12 width) holds the main Schedule Pickup flow (forms and selectors), while the right column (about 5/12 width) holds a persistent pickup summary panel. A narrow, floating vertical action/navigation bar hugs the far right edge of the viewport, independent of the main content. Overlays (loaders, error banner) appear on top of this structure without breaking it.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero content stage occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height, with the full-bleed global logistics imagery as the background. The centered content container sits with generous left/right margins on large screens. Vertically, there is clear separation between sections in the main form—using 32–48px gaps—to create a calm, marketing-style feel even though it’s a functional form. The right summary column is visually compact but tall enough to summarize all entered information, and is allowed to scroll within the viewport if its content becomes longer. The floating right toolbar consumes about 5–7% of viewport width, aligned to the right margin, and does not compress the main 1140px grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Form controls are grouped into logical, well-labeled sections that read like individual cards, rather than a dense single form. Each section has a short title, 16–24px vertical padding inside, and 32–48px separation to the next section. Labels and fields use consistent vertical spacing (8–16px increments) and multi-column groupings where appropriate, but never exceed 2–3 fields per row to maintain openness. The right summary panel is more information dense than the hero background but still uses space generously, with clear grouping of address, date/time, and package details.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top left with the page title and brand (within the shared site shell), then moves right across the primary navigation. From there, the viewer’s gaze drops diagonally to the large, left-aligned Schedule Pickup form header and first section (pickup type). The primary submit CTA at the bottom of the left column forms a second strong focus. Meanwhile, peripheral attention is drawn to the right by the contrasting pickup summary card, then further to the vertical pickup navigation/actions bar on the far right. This preserves an F/Z pattern: top nav line, diagonal down to main form, then across to the summary and vertical toolbar, with secondary pulls toward the date/time slot area and any active error or confirmation states.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s utility bar and main navigation, the Schedule Pickup page opens onto a full-bleed hero-style background showing a darkened global logistics image (earth/satellite or routes), tinted deep blue with subtle overlays. Centered on top of this, an 1140px-wide content container is placed, aligned to the same grid as the rest of the site. Inside this container, a two-column layout runs from left to right: on the left, a vertically stacked, multi-section form guides the user through selecting pickup type, entering the pickup address, adding package details, choosing date and time slots, providing contact information, and adding special instructions, culminating in a clearly emphasized submit section. Each step feels like a distinct card with breathing room, set against the darkened hero backdrop with a light or slightly elevated panel style. On the right, a prominent but narrower summary panel sits aligned to the top of the form, visually floating above the background and remaining visible as the user scrolls, summarizing entered data in real time. To the far right edge of the viewport, a slim vertical navigation/actions bar floats, echoing the mockup’s contact toolbar, offering quick step navigation and help. Above everything, a global error banner can appear as a full-width strip beneath the navigation bar. When the pickup is confirmed, the left column’s content transitions into a confirmation view card, while the right summary updates to reflect the final, locked-in details. The entire experience preserves the modern corporate hero aesthetic: centered, spacious, with strong orange CTAs against deep blue and white, and a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom visual narrative.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-context-loader is a non-visual initializer anchored to the main hero content region. Functionally, it controls whether the primary two-column content (form on the left, summary on the right) is shown. When the page first loads or when context is refreshing, a subtle full-width, full-height translucent overlay covers the central content container (but not the global header/nav), dimming the left form area and right summary panel while a centered spinner or progress indicator appears roughly in the middle of the left column. This overlay aligns with the 1140px content bounds and respects the same padding from the top (starting just under the navigation bar and any global error banner).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay tied to the main content container: shown on top of all form and summary elements, hidden once loading completes; it does not scroll independently, instead it locks to the viewport-height portion of the content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-type-selector is the first visible section at the top of the left column inside the hero content container. It sits about 32–48px below the bottom edge of the main navigation bar (or 16–24px below any global error banner), aligned to the left boundary of the 1140px grid. It appears as a full-width card within the left 7/12 of the grid, with a section title at the top and 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Horizontally, it spans almost the entire left column, leaving a consistent 24–32px gutter between itself and the right summary column. Vertically, it sets the rhythm for subsequent sections, with about 32px space between its bottom edge and the start of the next form section. Its controls may be laid out in one or two columns but never exceed two fields per row to maintain the spacious, marketing-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, scrolling with the rest of the form; acts as the anchor section at the top of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-address-form is placed directly below the pickup-type-selector in the left column, maintaining a vertical gap of about 32–40px. It occupies the same width as the pickup-type-selector, aligning to the same left and right column boundaries within the 7/12 left grid. The section is structured like a card with a small section heading and grouped address fields (street, city, postal code, country) arranged in 1–2 columns but never feeling cramped, using 16px vertical spacing between rows. The top of this section remains above the midline of the initial viewport so that the user can still see part of the background image and the top of the summary panel to the right, reinforcing the hero aesthetic while they start filling in information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, participating in normal page scroll; no stickiness.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-details-form continues the vertical stack in the left column, sitting roughly 32–40px beneath the pickup-address-form. It mirrors the same card-like panel width, flush with the left column’s edges and keeping the constant gutter to the right column. Within this section, multiple package fields (dimensions, weight, content type, quantity) are likely arranged in a two-column grid on desktop, with ~24px gaps between columns and 16px vertical spacing between field rows. The section height may be slightly larger than the address section, but its visual weight is balanced by similar padding and generous whitespace, ensuring the left column still feels airy and not like a dense data-entry form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, scrolling with other form sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-date-selector anchors the start of the scheduling zone in the left column. It appears 32–40px beneath the package-details-form, using the same card treatment and width. At the top of this section, a clear label like “Pickup date” aligns to the left, while the date picker control itself sits immediately below, spanning roughly 60–80% of the left column width. On larger screens, the date picker may sit side-by-side horizontally with the initial portion of the time-slot section below (e.g., date on the left, earliest-available summary on the right), but the primary perception should remain a vertical flow: date selection first, then time slot selection. This section’s bottom edge visually leads into the time-slot area with minimal interruption (16–24px gap), making them feel like part of a unified scheduling module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column; the date picker itself may have an internal dropdown calendar overlay that opens above surrounding elements but still within the left column’s visual context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-time-slot-loader is positioned within the scheduling zone directly associated with the area where time slots are displayed, immediately beneath or beside the pickup-date-selector inside the left column. When active, it overlays just the time-slot list region, not the entire page. Visually, it appears as a semi-translucent layer covering the area where time slots will show, with a small spinner centered horizontally in that sub-section. Its boundaries align with the inner padding of the corresponding card, preserving the clean rectangular structure and ensuring the rest of the form remains visible and interactive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Local overlay confined to the time-slot subsection; it does not affect the scroll position and moves with the form as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-time-slot-list appears directly below the pickup-date-selector within the same broader scheduling section of the left column. It spans the full width of the scheduling card in the left column and consists of evenly spaced, pill-like or card-like time option rows (e.g., morning, afternoon, specific hour windows). Each time slot occupies the full width or splits into 2–3 columns depending on screen size, with about 8–16px vertical spacing between rows. This block sits roughly in the middle third of the initial viewport when the user scrolls to it, with the summary panel on the right remaining visible. The spacing above and below this list (16–24px to the date selector above and 32px to the next section below) keeps it visually distinct but clearly tied to the date picker.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, scrolling in sequence with form content; selection states are handled internally without changing the layout footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-contact-form sits beneath the scheduling section, with a 32–48px vertical gap to clearly mark the transition from scheduling to contact details. It occupies the same width as previous sections in the left column and is structured as a card with a concise heading and a small number of fields (name, phone, email) in 1–2 columns. The section height is moderate, keeping all key fields visible at once on most desktop viewports, while the summary panel on the right stays within view for context. The padding and spacing within this block match the established pattern to maintain consistency and an uncluttered appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column; no fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-special-instructions section appears after the contact form, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space. It occupies the same left column width, but its height may vary depending on the size of the instructions text area. To stay aligned with the marketing-like openness, the instructions field is surrounded by generous padding, with a single large multi-line input and clear label, rather than multiple scattered fields. On the initial viewport, this section often sits closer to the bottom edge, encouraging a natural downward flow toward the final submission area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column; the text area grows within constraints but the section maintains consistent margins relative to other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-summary panel occupies the right column of the central content container, aligned to the top edge of the first left-column section (pickup-type-selector). It spans roughly 5/12 of the available 12-column grid, starting about 32px to the right of the left column content. The panel appears as a vertically oriented card with subtle elevation or a contrasting background (white or very light surface) to stand out against the deep blue hero image. Inside, sections for pickup address, packages, date and time, and contact are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing and clear subheadings. The card has 24–32px internal padding and a slight top margin from the navigation to maintain breathing room. As the user scrolls down the left column, the summary panel remains mostly in view, either by being slightly shorter than the viewport or by using internal scroll if its content grows taller. Its height is visually balanced so it does not overwhelm but remains a consistent, reassuring reference of the user’s progress.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right column on desktop: the top of the summary panel becomes sticky after it reaches a comfortable offset (e.g., 96–120px below the nav), so it remains visible as the user scrolls the left column content. On smaller screens, it transitions to an inline section that stacks below the main form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-submit-form sits at the bottom of the left column as the final section in the flow, about 32–48px below the pickup-special-instructions. It spans the same width as other left-column sections but may be slightly shallower in height, focusing primarily on the primary CTA button (e.g., “Schedule pickup”) and any accompanying notes (e.g., terms or estimated arrival). The main CTA button is aligned to the left within this card, using bold orange fill to echo the marketing hero CTA from the mockup and draw the eye as the key action point. Around the button, there is generous padding (16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal within the section) and clear spacing from secondary links. When scrolled into view, this section typically sits in the lower half of the viewport with part of the summary panel still visible to the right, reinforcing confidence before submission.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column; the primary CTA is the main visual attractor at the end of the vertical flow, without sticky behavior. On mobile, it may be mirrored by a sticky bottom CTA bar for convenience, but the core layout keeps it inline.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful submission, the pickup-confirmation-panel replaces the entire sequence of left-column form sections. It occupies the same left 7/12 grid area as the original form, starting at the same top alignment just below the navigation and any banners. The panel appears as a single, visually elevated card with a confirmation icon, bold confirmation headline, key pickup details, and possibly next steps, all centered more prominently within the left column but still left-aligned text. It uses slightly larger heading typography and more vertical space between content blocks (24–32px) to echo the hero-like impact of a marketing success message. The right-side pickup-summary panel remains in place, now mirroring finalized details, giving a cohesive before-and-after feel within the same two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column, but it acts as a stateful replacement for the form. It does not scroll much if content is short; if confirmation content is longer, it scrolls together with the page while the summary panel on the right retains its sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-navigation-actions component is realized as a slim, vertical toolbar anchored near the right edge of the viewport, independent of the main 1140px content container. It runs from roughly mid-height of the viewport downward, starting about one-third of the way from the top, and occupies about 5–7% of the viewport width. The bar floats over the hero background and partially overlaps the right margin of the summary panel’s visual area without obstructing its core content. Inside the bar, 3–5 stacked icon-plus-label buttons (or step indicators) represent key steps: Details, Schedule, Contact, Review, plus a prominent help or contact action. Each item sits in a square or lightly rounded rectangle with 8–12px inner padding, separated vertically by 8px. The bar’s background contrasts slightly (semi-opaque dark or light) to remain legible against the global image but feels lighter than the main summary panel so it stays secondary. On scroll, it maintains its vertical position relative to the viewport, acting as a persistent, glanceable navigation aid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed or sticky relative to the viewport on desktop, floating over content along the right edge. On tablet and mobile, it collapses into a horizontal bar at the top or bottom of the content area to avoid obstructing the narrower layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-error-banner appears directly beneath the primary navigation bar and above the hero content container, spanning the full browser width from left to right edge. It is a horizontally oriented strip with a height of approximately 40–56px, using a high-contrast background color (e.g., deep red or a strong warning color) and white text to immediately draw attention. Within the central 1140px content alignment, the message is left-aligned, with an optional dismiss icon on the far right, all anchored to the same grid as the main content. When present, it pushes the hero content (left form and right summary) downward by its height, preserving consistent spacing below it so the topmost form section begins around 24–32px beneath the banner. This maintains the clean separation between global feedback and the main interactive area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to scroll position: it sits below the fixed site navigation. If the navigation header is sticky, the banner appears just under it and scrolls away with the content, not fixed to the viewport for the entire scroll.

name: Customs & Duties Estimator
title: Customs & Duties Estimator
description: Estimate customs duties and taxes for an international shipment, compare shipping options that support prepaid duties, choose whether to prepay, and save the estimate for later use.

core_features: Load selectable options for shipment contents categories, shipment purposes, and supported currencies using getCustomsEstimatorOptions.
Capture origin and destination location details (country, city, postal code) in a single estimation form.
Capture shipment contents details including contents category, free-text description, customs value, and customs value currency.
Capture shipment purpose from a dynamically loaded list of supported purposes.
Create a customs estimate and retrieve related prepaid-capable shipping rate options by calling createCustomsEstimateAndRates with the form data.
Display a duties and taxes breakdown using the CustomsEstimate response (duties, taxes, total estimated charges, currency).
Display a list of returned ShippingRateOption items, highlighting those that support prepaid duties and showing each option’s estimated duties amount.
Allow inline expansion of a shipping option to show detailed information and a control to include or exclude prepaid duties in shipping charges.

Allow the user to select a shipping option with prepaid duties when its estimated duties are at or below a configurable target threshold (the threshold is provided via configuration or URL/state, not hardcoded).
Apply the user’s selection and prepaid preference by calling selectCustomsPrepaidOption, updating the CustomsEstimate and selected ShippingRateOption in page state.
Allow the user to save the current customs estimate (including any selected prepaid option) for later use via saveCustomsEstimate.
After saving, provide navigation to view all saved estimates (account_overview.html) and to get shipping rates for the current route (ship_rates.html?mode=quote) using URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Create a new customs estimate:
1) User navigates to customs_estimator.html without a customsEstimateId URL parameter.
2) On page load, the page calls getCustomsEstimatorOptions to populate the contents category, shipment purpose, and currency dropdowns.
3) User fills in origin country, city, postal code and destination country, city, postal code.
4) User selects a contents category and enters a free-text contents description.
5) User enters the customs value and selects the customs value currency from the dynamically loaded list.
6) User selects a shipment purpose from the dynamically loaded list.
7) User submits the estimation form.
8) The page calls createCustomsEstimateAndRates with the form data.
9) On success, the page-state is updated with the returned CustomsEstimate and the array of ShippingRateOption results.
10) The duties breakdown panel and prepaid-capable rate options list become visible, reflecting the returned data.

Workflow 2 – Review duties breakdown and prepaid-eligible options:
1) After Workflow 1, the duties breakdown panel displays duties, taxes, and total estimated charges from CustomsEstimate.
2) The rate options list displays each ShippingRateOption, indicating whether it supports prepaid duties.
3) For each option, the UI shows its estimated duties using ShippingRateOption.estimated_duties and estimated_duties_currency.
4) The component determines whether each option’s estimated duties value is at or below a configurable target threshold (not hardcoded) and marks those options as eligible for prepaid selection.
5) User may expand any option row to see more detail, including the prepaid duties control for eligible options.

Workflow 3 – Select a prepaid duties option:
1) User expands a rate option row in the rate options list.
2) If the option supports prepaid duties and its estimated duties are at or below the configured threshold, the expanded panel shows a checkbox or similar control to include prepaid duties in shipping charges.
3) User sets the checkbox to the desired value (include or exclude prepaid duties).
4) User clicks a 'Select this option' control in the expanded panel.
5) The page calls selectCustomsPrepaidOption with the current CustomsEstimate.id, the selected ShippingRateOption.id, and the checkbox value as include_prepaid_duties.
6) On success, the page updates the CustomsEstimate (including selected_rate_option_id and include_prepaid_duties) and marks the corresponding ShippingRateOption as selected.
7) The list visually reflects which option is selected and whether prepaid duties are included.

Workflow 4 – Save an estimate for later use:
1) After creating an estimate (and optionally selecting a rate option and prepaid duties preference), the user clicks the 'Save Estimate' action.
2) The page calls saveCustomsEstimate with the current CustomsEstimate.id.
3) On success, the returned CustomsEstimate with saved=true is stored in page state.
4) The page may update the URL (e.g., via history APIs) to include customsEstimateId set to the current CustomsEstimate.id, without reloading the page.
5) A success message is shown along with a 'View all saved estimates' link.
6) If the user clicks 'View all saved estimates', the browser navigates to account_overview.html using a standard link with no additional parameters required by this page’s design.

Workflow 5 – Reopen an existing saved estimate:
1) User navigates to customs_estimator.html with a customsEstimateId URL parameter (e.g., from account_overview.html or index.html linking to this page).
2) On page load, the server is expected to pre-populate the page with the matching CustomsEstimate and associated ShippingRateOption list data (hydrated into the initial HTML/JSON state) based on the customsEstimateId; the client-side scripts read this state without calling additional interfaces.
3) The page-state controller sets the current CustomsEstimate and rate options from this preloaded data and marks the form fields as filled.
4) Duties breakdown and rate options list render immediately using the preloaded data.
5) The user can adjust prepaid duties selection using the rate options list, which calls selectCustomsPrepaidOption as in Workflow 3.
6) The user can re-save the estimate (for updates) using saveCustomsEstimate, which also keeps the customsEstimateId in the URL.

Workflow 6 – Navigate to shipping rates from the estimator:
1) After an estimate has been created (CustomsEstimate exists in page state), the user clicks 'Get shipping rates for this route'.
2) The page constructs a URL to ship_rates.html including mode=quote and any route-related query parameters needed by the shipping rates page (e.g., origin/destination data derived from CustomsEstimate).
3) Browser navigates to ship_rates.html with these URL parameters.
4) The shipping rates page, in quote mode, uses the provided route data to show detailed shipping rate options (outside the scope of this page).
interactions: On page load: read customsEstimateId from the URL query string; initialize page state accordingly and trigger getCustomsEstimatorOptions; if server-hydrated CustomsEstimate data exists for this ID, load it into state.
On getCustomsEstimatorOptions success: populate dropdown controls for contents_category, shipment_purpose, and customs_value_currency; mark the form as ready for input.
On change of any route field (origin/destination): update in-memory form state and clear any existing CustomsEstimate and rate options if the change invalidates the current estimate.

On change of any contents field (category, description, customs value, currency, purpose): update in-memory form state and clear any existing CustomsEstimate and rate options if the change invalidates the current estimate.

On estimation form submit: validate all required fields; if valid, call createCustomsEstimateAndRates; during the call, disable the submit button and show a loading state; on success, show duties breakdown and rate options; on error, show an error message and keep form values intact.

On expand/collapse of a rate option row: toggle visibility of the detailed panel for that specific ShippingRateOption without affecting other rows; the selected row remains highlighted even when collapsed.

On prepaid eligibility evaluation: for each ShippingRateOption, compare its estimated_duties against a configurable threshold value available in page configuration or query parameters; if not eligible, hide or disable the prepaid checkbox for that option while still allowing the option itself to be selected as a non-prepaid choice if desired by future business rules.

On toggle of 'Include prepaid duties' checkbox within an expanded rate option row: update a temporary UI state for that option; the change is persisted to the backend only when the user actually selects that option (via selectCustomsPrepaidOption).

On 'Select this option' click for a rate option: call selectCustomsPrepaidOption with the current CustomsEstimate.id, the clicked ShippingRateOption.id, and the checkbox state; disable selection controls while the call is in progress; on success, mark this option as selected and unmark others based on the updated data; on error, show an inline error and keep previous selection.

On 'Save Estimate' click: call saveCustomsEstimate with the current CustomsEstimate.id; disable the button while the call is pending; on success, update CustomsEstimate.saved and show confirmation; optionally update the URL query string to include or update customsEstimateId for this estimate; on error, show a non-blocking error message and keep the user’s estimate and selections intact.
On 'View all saved estimates' click: navigate to account_overview.html by updating window.location.

On 'Get shipping rates for this route' click: navigate to ship_rates.html while ensuring the URL includes mode=quote and any necessary route-related parameters derived from CustomsEstimate (such as origin and destination fields).

On any API error (for getCustomsEstimatorOptions, createCustomsEstimateAndRates, selectCustomsPrepaidOption, saveCustomsEstimate): show a clearly scoped error message related to the component that initiated the call; do not clear user input; allow retrying the action.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary explicit URL parameter is customsEstimateId. When the page loads, it parses the query string. If customsEstimateId is absent, the page initializes in 'new-estimate' mode: form fields are empty, a call to getCustomsEstimatorOptions is made, and no CustomsEstimate data is present. If customsEstimateId is present, the server is expected to render the page with a preloaded CustomsEstimate and associated ShippingRateOption list for that ID (embedded into the page as JSON). The client-side page-state controller reads this embedded data, sets it into in-memory state, and the page initializes in 'loaded-estimate' mode. In this mode, the form fields reflect the saved CustomsEstimate fields, the duties breakdown and rate options list are immediately shown, and controls like 'Save Estimate' may reflect the saved status. No additional interface is called solely to fetch by ID. Internal page state (not persisted in URL or localStorage) tracks: current form values, current CustomsEstimate object, current rate options array, loading/error states per interface call, and the currently expanded rate option row. A configurable duties-threshold value used to gate prepaid eligibility is injected via server-side configuration or through additional URL parameters (if implemented) and is stored in in-memory state; this threshold is not hardcoded in the page. When saveCustomsEstimate succeeds, if customsEstimateId was not present in the URL, the page can update the URL using history.pushState or history.replaceState to append or update customsEstimateId with the CustomsEstimate.id returned by the interface, without causing a full reload. Navigation to other pages (ship_rates.html and account_overview.html) uses standard URL-based navigation with the parameters defined in the navigation specification; no client-side storage such as localStorage is used to transfer state between pages.

components.functionality: Owns global page state for the customs estimator, including reading URL parameters, coordinating interface calls, and exposing derived state (current mode, current CustomsEstimate, rate options, loading and error flags) to other components.

On initialization, calls getCustomsEstimatorOptions to retrieve dynamic lists of contents categories, shipment purposes, and supported currencies, and exposes them to the customs-estimate-form component.

Collects all inputs required to create a customs estimate: origin/destination location information, contents category, contents description, customs value and currency, and shipment purpose. On submit, it triggers the creation of a new CustomsEstimate and associated prepaid-capable rate options.
Invokes createCustomsEstimateAndRates when the customs-estimate-form is submitted with valid data, then updates page state with the returned CustomsEstimate and ShippingRateOption array.

Displays the duties and taxes breakdown from the current CustomsEstimate, including estimated duties, estimated taxes, total estimated charges, and the currency. It is shown only when a CustomsEstimate is available.

Renders the list of ShippingRateOption items associated with the current CustomsEstimate, showing for each option basic info (service name, delivery estimate, price, currency), whether prepaid duties are supported, and the estimated duties. It determines prepaid eligibility per option based on a configurable duties threshold and passes this information to each row’s details panel.

For a single ShippingRateOption, displays detailed information inline when expanded, including additional pricing context and a control to include or exclude prepaid duties when the option is eligible. Provides a 'Select this option' action that applies the user’s prepaid preference.

Handles selection of a rate option with a specific prepaid duties preference by calling selectCustomsPrepaidOption and updating page state with the returned CustomsEstimate and selected ShippingRateOption.
Provides a 'Save Estimate' control and success/error feedback. It is enabled when there is a valid CustomsEstimate.id in state. On save, it also enables navigation to view all saved estimates.
Invokes saveCustomsEstimate with the current CustomsEstimate.id and updates the page state with the returned CustomsEstimate and any informational message.

Displays contextual navigation actions: a link to get shipping rates for the current route, and a link to view all saved estimates after a save. It constructs the ship_rates.html URL with mode=quote and relevant query parameters derived from the current CustomsEstimate.
Centralizes display of validation messages for the estimate form and error messages for all interface calls, scoped to the relevant component or action.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the marketing-hero structure from the mockup: a thin utility bar, then a primary navigation header, then a full-width hero section with a satellite/earth background. Within the hero, content is centered inside a max-width ~1140px container. The left 45–50% of this container holds the hero title, short description, local navigation, and the top, most essential portion of the customs-estimate-form. The right side of the hero remains visually open to showcase the global imagery, with only light overlay UI (e.g., a subtle 'Your estimate will appear here' placeholder aligned to the lower-right). Below the hero, the page transitions into a two-column tool layout: the left column (~60% width) continues the full estimator workflow (full form and prepaid options list), while the right column (~40% width) carries the duties breakdown summary. A vertical floating action bar on the far right uses the mockup’s right-edge toolbar pattern for saving and related actions, staying visually separated from the main content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the marketing-hero structure from the mockup: a thin utility bar, then a primary navigation header, then a full-width hero section with a satellite/earth background. Within the hero, content is centered inside a max-width ~1140px container. The left 45–50% of this container holds the hero title, short description, local navigation, and the top, most essential portion of the customs-estimate-form. The right side of the hero remains visually open to showcase the global imagery, with only light overlay UI (e.g., a subtle 'Your estimate will appear here' placeholder aligned to the lower-right). Below the hero, the page transitions into a two-column tool layout: the left column (~60% width) continues the full estimator workflow (full form and prepaid options list), while the right column (~40% width) carries the duties breakdown summary. A vertical floating action bar on the far right uses the mockup’s right-edge toolbar pattern for saving and related actions, staying visually separated from the main content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertical space follows the mockup: the utility bar is shallow (~40px), the primary navigation is medium (~70–80px), and the hero consumes ~70–80% of the initial viewport height, creating a strong first impression. Within the hero content container, the left text/form block occupies roughly 45–50% of the width, with generous padding (24–32px) around blocks and 16–24px between text elements. The right side of the hero is mostly occupied by the full-bleed background image, with minimal overlays so the imagery reads clearly. Below the hero, the main content area has a max width matching the hero container and is split 60/40 (left/right). Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → main form area, form → prepaid options list) uses 48–64px gaps, while spacing inside components (form groups, list items, panels) adheres to 8/16/24px increments for a clean, consistent rhythm. The right floating action bar occupies roughly 5–7% of the viewport width as a narrow, fixed column that overlays but does not reflow core content.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a spacious, marketing-style density even though it’s a tool: few fields per row, clear grouping into cards or sections, and large click targets for all interactive elements. The hero uses large typography and generous whitespace, with only the most critical inputs visible at first to avoid overwhelming the user. The form and lists are broken into logical sections with enough breathing room (24–32px between sections) to stay scannable. Dense data presentations (like breakdown numbers or shipping options) are simplified into card-based panels and stacked list items instead of tight tables, keeping the feel light and modern despite the complexity of the task.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z-pattern. Users start at the top left on the logo, then scan right across the primary navigation, then diagonally down to the large hero headline on the left. From there, their eye naturally drops to the key input fields and the bold orange 'Calculate estimate' CTA, then across to the right where the duties breakdown panel will appear once an estimate is generated and the floating save/action bar runs vertically along the right edge. As users scroll, the flow continues down the left column through the rest of the form and the prepaid options list, with secondary detail (rate-option-details-panel) expanding inline below the selected option. The sticky or semi-sticky duties breakdown on the right and the fixed vertical action bar maintain a persistent right-side anchor that keeps the page feeling dynamic and encourages interaction.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a thin dark utility strip and a clean, centered navigation bar against a deep blue backdrop, leading into a full-bleed hero showing a stylized earth and orbital network. Within this hero, a centered content container frames the left-aligned headline 'Customs & Duties Estimator', a concise explanation, and a local navigation strip, followed immediately by a streamlined set of top-level form fields. The right half of the hero remains mostly visual, allowing the satellite imagery to carry the global logistics theme while a subtle placeholder hints where the future estimate summary will live. Just beyond the hero fold, the layout settles into a two-column tool view: on the left, the full estimator form and prepaid options list stack vertically with ample whitespace; on the right, a prominent duties breakdown card summarises calculated costs and stays in view as users scroll. Along the extreme right edge of the viewport, a narrow, fixed vertical action bar floats above the background, housing save and related controls in stacked, icon-led buttons. The overall effect is a modern, corporate hero page that smoothly transitions into a functional, yet airy estimating workspace, with clear left-to-right and top-to-bottom flow guiding the user from input to decision and finally to saving their estimate.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-controller is a non-visual layer that sits at the top of the page’s layout hierarchy, conceptually positioned behind all visible elements. It does not occupy any explicit space in the grid but governs whether main regions (hero content, form, breakdown panel, lists) are shown, dimmed, or replaced by loaders. When the page is in a transitional state (e.g., initial load or recalculation), the controller can trigger soft overlays or skeleton states inside the existing content areas without changing their positions or proportions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered structural component; it affects visibility and state of other components but does not have its own box in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimator-options-loader appears inline wherever estimator-dependent content will load. In the hero and upper form area, it manifests as full-width skeleton bars and rectangular placeholders matching the shape of the main form fields and the duties breakdown card. In the prepaid options list and rate details area, it appears as stacked placeholder cards occupying the same width as the future list items. All loaders respect the surrounding grid: they sit within the left 60% and right 40% columns and maintain the same vertical spacing (16–24px) as the real content they stand in for.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within existing containers during loading phases, occupying the exact footprint of future content, then disappears when data is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The customs-estimate-form begins in the hero’s left content block and then continues directly below the hero within the main left column. In the hero, its top row presents 2–3 critical, large inputs (e.g., origin, destination, declared value) arranged horizontally across the left ~45–50% of the container, each with generous vertical padding (~12–16px) and 16–24px horizontal gaps, leaving the right side visually open. Below the hero fold, the form expands into the full left column (approximately 60% of the centered content width) with fields grouped into clearly labeled sections stacked vertically, separated by 24–32px of whitespace. The form aligns to the left with a consistent margin from the container edge, and its primary actions (like the main calculate button) appear just below the last key field group before the prepaid options section, keeping the left column as the dominant interactive zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page; the header area of the form (section title + main CTA) can optionally become sticky within the left column after the hero scrolls off, keeping the key action visible without changing the overall column widths.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-creator is embodied by the primary orange 'Calculate estimate' button positioned directly beneath the core input fields of the customs-estimate-form. In the hero, this button sits left-aligned under the top row of fields, taking up a fixed width of roughly 220–280px with ample vertical padding (~12–16px) and clear spacing (16–24px) above and below to make it a strong focal point. As the form continues below the hero, a repeated or persistently visible version of this CTA sits at the end of the main input section, still aligned to the left within the 60% column. The button stands out against the darker hero background and neutral form card backgrounds, using solid orange fill and white text to visually signal the key action on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form stack; does not fix to the viewport, but its closest section header can become sticky so that the call-to-action remains near the visible area while the user scrolls through fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The duties-breakdown-panel is a prominent summary card anchored in the right column of the main content area, starting just below the hero and slightly overlapping its bottom edge visually (e.g., with a subtle card that appears to rise out of the hero zone) to connect it to the hero imagery. It occupies roughly 40% of the centered content width, aligned to the right, with internal padding of 24–32px and 16–24px spacing between metric rows or tiles. At its default height, the panel shows a headline (e.g., 'Estimated Duties & Taxes'), 2–4 key numeric values presented as large figures with smaller labels, and possibly a small tag indicating whether duties are prepaid or paid on delivery. It maintains generous whitespace around its edges, with at least 32px separation from both the hero content above and any components below, making it a clean, easily scannable summary area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky element within the right column: once the user scrolls past the hero, the panel remains pinned near the top of the right column while the left column content scrolls, ensuring the summary is always in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prepaid-rate-options-list is positioned in the left column directly below the main portion of the customs-estimate-form and the primary calculate button, separated by a 48–64px vertical gap to signal a new decision phase. It spans the full width of the left 60% column, aligning with the form fields above and maintaining the same left margin from the container. The list consists of stacked, card-like rows for each prepaid shipping option: each card is full-width within this column, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical separation between cards. Each row shows a clear option title, key figures (total cost, delivery time, duties handling), and an easily clickable selection target, arranged so that the primary data runs horizontally across the upper half of the card and secondary info sits below. The styling echoes the clean marketing feel: light backgrounds, subtle dividers, and adequate whitespace, avoiding dense table layouts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls within the main left column, following the form. As options are added or removed, the list grows vertically but does not affect the right column’s width; on smaller screens, it stacks below the duties breakdown in a single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rate-option-details-panel appears as an expanding detail section directly below whichever row in the prepaid-rate-options-list is selected. It occupies the same width as the parent card (full width of the left column) and is indented or subtly inset (e.g., with a slight background tint and 16–24px padding) to visually separate it from the main row content. When collapsed, it is invisible and does not consume vertical space; when an option is expanded, the panel pushes subsequent list items downward, displaying finer-grain information such as duty/tax breakdowns, handling fees, and additional terms in a tidy, multi-row layout with 8–16px spacing between lines. This inline expansion keeps the user’s focus anchored in the left column while still providing depth on demand.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands and collapses inline within the list, dynamically adjusting the height of the surrounding content; does not affect the right column or global layout, and follows normal scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prepaid-option-selector is represented visually by the selection affordance inside each card of the prepaid-rate-options-list—typically a radio button or a highlighted card state. Positioned at the far left of each option card’s header row, it aligns vertically with the option title, ensuring quick scan-ability from top to bottom of the list. When an option is selected, the entire card subtly elevates or gains a border in the brand’s orange accent, and the corresponding rate-option-details-panel expands beneath it.
