This selector UI keeps the cards large and tap-friendly, with at least 16px padding around the selection control and ample space between adjacent options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each option card; selecting an option triggers the inline expansion of details in the same column and updates related summaries (like the duties breakdown) without changing the overall grid or scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-estimate-actions component appears as a vertical floating action bar anchored to the right edge of the viewport, spanning roughly 5–7% of the screen width. Vertically, it is centered around the mid-height of the hero on initial load and then remains in a consistent right-edge position as the user scrolls through the page. The bar is a tall, rounded rectangle with a deep blue background and subtle drop shadow, containing 3–4 stacked icon-buttons (e.g., Save, Export, Share, Help). Each button is a square or circular hit area with 8–12px internal padding and 8–12px spacing between buttons, with the primary 'Save estimate' control visually distinguished in orange. The bar floats above the main content without overlapping the left/right columns’ core information, maintaining a narrow footprint and leaving at least 24–32px margin between its left edge and the main content container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the right side of the viewport so it remains visible during scroll; on smaller screens, it can collapse into a single floating FAB or a bottom-aligned horizontal action bar while retaining its distinct, always-available position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-saver is realized through the primary 'Save estimate' control within the save-estimate-actions bar and any confirmatory button in the right column near the duties breakdown. In the vertical bar, this save control is placed near the top of the icon stack, slightly larger or accented in orange to emphasize its importance. It is positioned so that when the user is reviewing the duties-breakdown-panel and prepaid options, the save action is always within a short eye and mouse travel distance to the right. Any secondary save CTA near the breakdown panel appears as a compact, right-aligned button directly beneath the summary numbers, visually linked by color and label to the floating bar’s save control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the floating action bar and any local summary area; the main save control travels with the fixed bar, while secondary save buttons scroll with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-links-panel sits within the hero’s left content block, directly under the main page title and short description but above the top row of form fields. It spans roughly the same width as the hero text (about 45–50% of the container), arranged as a horizontal row of 3–5 text links or pill-style buttons (e.g., 'Estimator', 'Documentation', 'FAQ', 'Contact'). Each link has 12–16px horizontal padding and 8px vertical padding, with 16px spacing between items, and the currently active section (Estimator) is highlighted using the orange accent or a subtle underline. This panel creates a light secondary navigation area without overwhelming the hero, and the generous spacing around it—16–24px above and below—keeps the overall hero layout airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s left column; scrolls away with the hero as the user moves down the page and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation-and-error-messages panel appears within the left column, directly beneath the hero area and above the main body of the customs-estimate-form, spanning the full 60% width of that column. When active, it takes the form of a full-width alert bar or card with a light red or amber background, 16–24px of internal padding, and clear, concise text describing what needs to be corrected, optionally with short links or anchors to specific fields. Inline field-level validation messages appear immediately beneath the relevant input within the form, aligned to the left edge of the field and separated by 4–8px of vertical space. The main alert panel has at least 16–24px of vertical margin separating it from surrounding content, so it stands out without feeling cramped, and uses the brand’s typography and iconography to stay consistent with the clean corporate style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the left column content flow; when present, it pushes content below it downward but does not overlay or fix to the viewport. It may briefly scroll into view automatically after submission errors, but then behaves like normal content.

name: Track Shipment
title: Track Shipment
description: Allows users to enter a tracking number, retrieve live shipment tracking data, view shipment details and history, and access delivery management options when available.

core_features: Tracking number input and submission using the trackShipmentByNumber interface to retrieve shipment data.
Automatic tracking when the page is opened with a tracking_number URL parameter.
Display of current shipment status and estimated delivery date from the tracking response.
Display of shipment summary details based on the Shipment data (tracking number, basic origin/destination information, service type if provided).
Display of tracking history as a timeline based on TrackingEvent records returned by trackShipmentByNumber.
Conditional display of a "Manage delivery" action when can_manage_delivery is true in the tracking response, navigating to delivery_options.html with the shipmentId parameter.
Clear handling of invalid, not-found, or failed tracking lookups using error_message from the interface and client-side validation.
Navigation back to the home page via a "Back to Home" link to index.html.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Direct tracking on this page: (1) User opens track.html without URL parameters. (2) User enters a tracking number in the tracking input field. (3) User submits the form (Track button or Enter key). (4) Page validates that the input is non-empty and syntactically acceptable. (5) Page sets state to loading and calls trackShipmentByNumber with the entered tracking_number. (6) On success with a valid shipment, page updates state to result, populates shipment, events, current_status, estimated_delivery_date, and can_manage_delivery, and renders status header, shipment summary, history timeline, and any available delivery management actions. (7) If the interface returns error_message or the request fails, page enters an error state and shows an inline error message while preserving the entered tracking number.

Workflow 2 – Auto-tracking from another page: (1) User navigates from index.html, shipment_confirmation.html, or account_overview.html to track.html with tracking_number in the query string. (2) On initial load, the URL-state controller reads tracking_number from the URL and populates the tracking input field. (3) If tracking_number is present and non-empty, the page automatically calls trackShipmentByNumber with it and sets state to loading. (4) On response, page behaves as in Workflow 1: rendering status, summary, and history or showing an error if error_message is present. (5) The user can change the tracking number and resubmit; the URL is updated to reflect the new tracking_number.

Workflow 3 – Manage delivery from tracking result: (1) User retrieves a shipment successfully via trackShipmentByNumber. (2) The response includes shipment.id and can_manage_delivery. (3) If can_manage_delivery is true and the shipment is in a state where delivery changes are allowed (as implied by current_status and shipment.status), the "Manage delivery" button is shown. (4) User clicks "Manage delivery". (5) Page navigates to delivery_options.html with shipmentId set to the tracked Shipment.id in the query string, without additional API calls from this page.

Workflow 4 – Handling invalid or not-found tracking numbers: (1) User enters a tracking number and submits. (2) trackShipmentByNumber is called. (3) If the API returns error_message (e.g., for invalid format or not-found number) or no shipment data, the page transitions to an error/no-result state. (4) An inline error banner is displayed with the error_message from the response and the tracking input remains populated for correction. (5) The URL remains or is updated to reflect the attempted tracking_number so the error state is deep-linkable. (6) When the user corrects the number and resubmits, the error banner is cleared and the normal loading/result flow resumes.

Workflow 5 – Navigating away: (1) At any time, user can click the "Back to Home" link. (2) The page navigates to index.html without additional API calls. (3) Browser back/forward navigation is supported by syncing the tracking_number query parameter with the currently viewed tracking number so that revisiting the page with a prior URL restores the corresponding tracking lookup when available.
interactions: Tracking input change: On each change in the tracking number input field, the component updates the in-memory page state for activeTrackingNumber but does not trigger an API call until the form is submitted or the page is auto-triggering based on an initial URL parameter.

Tracking form submission: When the user submits the tracking form, the page prevents default form submission, validates the tracking number (non-empty and basic format checks), updates the tracking_number query parameter in the URL to match the submitted value, sets a loading state, and calls trackShipmentByNumber(tracking_number).

Automatic request on page load with URL parameter: On initial load, if tracking_number is present in the URL and passes basic validation, the page immediately calls trackShipmentByNumber with that value and shows a loading indicator until the response arrives.

Handling trackShipmentByNumber success: On a successful response without error_message, the page stores shipment, events, current_status, estimated_delivery_date, and can_manage_delivery in state. The tracking results components (status header, shipment summary, tracking timeline, and manage-delivery CTA) re-render based on this data.

Handling trackShipmentByNumber error: If trackShipmentByNumber returns error_message or fails (network or unexpected error), the page sets an error state including a user-visible message (using error_message when provided) and hides result-specific components. The tracking input remains editable, and the user can submit again.

Viewing tracking history: The user can scroll through the list of tracking events, which are sorted in a defined order (e.g., most recent first). Optional UI interactions such as expanding/collapsing event details do not trigger additional API calls and operate purely on the existing TrackingEvent data.

Clicking "Manage delivery": When visible (can_manage_delivery is true and a valid Shipment.id is present), clicking this control navigates the browser to delivery_options.html with the shipmentId query parameter set to the Shipment.id from the latest successful tracking response.
Clicking "Back to Home": Clicking this link navigates to index.html using normal navigation without modifying tracking-related state.

Browser back/forward navigation: When the user uses browser navigation, the page reads the tracking_number from the current URL and, if it differs from the in-memory state, updates the input field and optionally reissues trackShipmentByNumber for that number so that the view matches the URL history state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state driver is the tracking_number query parameter in the page URL. On initial page load, the URL-state controller parses tracking_number. If it is present and non-empty, the controller sets the page's activeTrackingNumber to this value, populates the tracking input field, sets a loading state, and calls trackShipmentByNumber(tracking_number). If the parameter is absent or empty, the page stays in an idle state with only the tracking input visible. When the user submits a tracking number via the form, the controller updates the tracking_number query parameter in the URL (using history API where available) to match the submitted value, then sets the page state to loading and calls trackShipmentByNumber with that tracking_number. The response from trackShipmentByNumber is used to transition the page into one of two main states: result state (when shipment and events are present and error_message is not set) or error state (when error_message is set or no valid shipment data is returned). In result state, the status header, shipment summary, tracking timeline, and conditional manage-delivery CTA are rendered based on the latest response. In error state, the error banner is rendered and result components are hidden or shown in an inactive form. At all times, the tracking input reflects the current activeTrackingNumber, which is kept in sync with the tracking_number query parameter; when the browser back/forward actions change the URL, a popstate handler re-reads tracking_number, updates activeTrackingNumber and the input, and may re-trigger trackShipmentByNumber to keep the UI consistent with the URL. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used; all restorable state is derived from the URL and fresh interface calls.

components.functionality: Provides a single tracking number input field and a submit action that calls trackShipmentByNumber, and keeps the tracking_number query parameter synchronized with the current input value on submission.
event_handlers: onInputChange: Update in-memory activeTrackingNumber state when the user edits the tracking number field.

onSubmit: Validate that the tracking number is non-empty and syntactically valid, update URL.query.tracking_number to the submitted value, set page state to loading, and invoke trackShipmentByNumber(tracking_number).

components.functionality: Displays a loading state while a trackShipmentByNumber request is in progress and hides itself when a result or error is available.
Displays error messages when tracking fails, using error_message from trackShipmentByNumber when available, and clears when a new tracking request is initiated or succeeds.
event_handlers: onDismiss: Optionally clear the visible error banner while retaining the current tracking number so the user can correct and resubmit.

components.functionality: Shows the current shipment status and estimated delivery information from the latest successful tracking response.

Displays a summary of the tracked shipment, including tracking number, high-level origin and destination information, and service type if available from the Shipment data included in the tracking response.
Renders the list of TrackingEvent items returned by trackShipmentByNumber as a chronological history of shipment scans and status changes, sorted by event_time.
event_handlers: onSortChange: Optionally toggle between most-recent-first and oldest-first ordering using existing TrackingEvent data without additional API calls.
onToggleEventDetails: Optionally expand or collapse detailed information for an individual TrackingEvent in the UI.

components.functionality: Provides a "Change delivery" / "Manage delivery" action that is conditionally displayed when the tracked shipment supports delivery management, and navigates to the delivery options page.
event_handlers: onClick: If can_manage_delivery is true and Shipment.id is available, navigate to delivery_options.html with URL parameter shipmentId set to Shipment.id.

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the home page without affecting tracking data or calling any interfaces.

Coordinates the rendering of result-related components (status header, shipment summary, tracking timeline, manage-delivery CTA) based on the current page state and the most recent trackShipmentByNumber response.

Synchronizes the tracking_number query parameter with the page's activeTrackingNumber state, triggers automatic tracking on initial load when tracking_number is present, and responds to browser history changes.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read URL.query.tracking_number; if present and valid, set PageState.activeTrackingNumber, populate the tracking input, set loading state, and call trackShipmentByNumber(tracking_number).
onTrackingNumberChange: When a new tracking number is submitted, update URL.query.tracking_number using the History API and ensure PageState.activeTrackingNumber matches.

onPopState: When the browser back/forward buttons change the URL, re-read URL.query.tracking_number, update PageState.activeTrackingNumber and the input field, and optionally re-call trackShipmentByNumber so that the displayed result matches the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the thin utility bar and primary navigation at the top, followed by a hero-style tracking section that mirrors the marketing hero from the mockup. The hero becomes the functional centerpiece: on the left, a vertically stacked block with page title, short explanatory line, and the tracking-number-input-form; on the right, a visually rich satellite/earth image with subtle network lines, preserving the technology-forward global theme. Beneath this hero, a structured results area flows top-to-bottom: tracking-status-header at the top of the results, then a two-column layout with shipment-summary-panel on the left and tracking-events-timeline on the right, with manage-delivery-cta integrated near the top of this block. back-to-home-link sits lower in the hierarchy, near the bottom-left of the results. tracking-loading-indicator and tracking-error-banner are layered contextually around the form and results without adding new sections. The url-state-sync-controller is non-visual and does not affect the visual arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the thin utility bar and primary navigation at the top, followed by a hero-style tracking section that mirrors the marketing hero from the mockup. The hero becomes the functional centerpiece: on the left, a vertically stacked block with page title, short explanatory line, and the tracking-number-input-form; on the right, a visually rich satellite/earth image with subtle network lines, preserving the technology-forward global theme. Beneath this hero, a structured results area flows top-to-bottom: tracking-status-header at the top of the results, then a two-column layout with shipment-summary-panel on the left and tracking-events-timeline on the right, with manage-delivery-cta integrated near the top of this block. back-to-home-link sits lower in the hierarchy, near the bottom-left of the results. tracking-loading-indicator and tracking-error-banner are layered contextually around the form and results without adding new sections. The url-state-sync-controller is non-visual and does not affect the visual arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero tracking section occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height, full-bleed with the global imagery, with the interactive content constrained to a centered max-width container (~1140px). Within this container, the left column (hero text + tracking-number-input-form) uses about 45% of the width, while the right visual area uses about 55%. The right-floating global contact/utility toolbar (part of the shared site shell) occupies a narrow strip of about 5–7% of the viewport width and sits above the hero and results, not altering the main grid. Below the hero, the tracking-results-container uses the same centered content width, with tracking-status-header spanning nearly 100% of the container, and a two-column layout where shipment-summary-panel occupies roughly 40% of the width and tracking-events-timeline takes about 60%. Vertical spacing between hero and results follows a generous 48–64px margin, and within sections components are separated with 16–32px gaps, adhering to the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area remains spacious and minimal, with a single primary interaction (enter tracking number) and large, clear typography. There is ample whitespace around the tracking-number-input-form and CTA, with about 24–32px gaps between the headline, description, and form elements. The results area becomes more information-dense but still clean and card-like: tracking-status-header is bold but not crowded; shipment-summary-panel presents key details in a compact but readable card with clear grouping and 16–24px internal padding; tracking-events-timeline is the densest part but uses vertical spacing between events to avoid clutter. manage-delivery-cta is visually prominent but not surrounded by many competing interactive elements. back-to-home-link is small and understated. Overall, density ramps up as you move down the page, but never reaches a table-like or cramped feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the same F/Z-pattern as the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left with the logo in the header, then moves to the right across the primary navigation. From there, the gaze drops diagonally down to the left side of the hero where the main page title and tracking-number-input-form sit. The bright orange submit button within the form becomes a key focal point. Once a tracking number is submitted, attention flows downward into the tracking-results-container, first to the tracking-status-header at the top, then horizontally across to any status badge and manage-delivery-cta near the upper-right of the results. From there, the eye naturally scans down the left to the shipment-summary-panel and then sweeps right to the tracking-events-timeline. As users scroll further, the visual weight lightens toward the bottom where the back-to-home-link resides as a secondary option. The right-floating contact/utility toolbar and any slider controls on the hero’s right side act as tertiary anchors on the far-right edge, echoing the mockup’s pattern without overpowering the central flow.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a slim utility bar and a comfortably tall navigation bar across the top, both spanning the full width of the viewport and sitting over a full-bleed darkened satellite/earth image. Centered beneath the nav, a content container (~1140px wide) holds a hero-style tracking section that fills about three-quarters of the viewport height. On the left half of this hero, a bold white headline like “Track Your Shipment” and a brief supporting line sit above a clean, single-field tracking-number-input-form with a strong orange action button, all stacked vertically with generous breathing room. The right half is mostly dedicated to the global imagery, subtly overlaid with network lines and perhaps faint UI markers, while a slim vertical contact/utility bar floats along the far-right edge of the screen. When errors occur, a slim, colored tracking-error-banner appears just under the form, and when loading, a discreet tracking-loading-indicator sits centrally in the results area, slightly dimming surrounding content. Scrolling (or after submitting a valid tracking number) reveals the tracking-results-container below the hero, separated by about 56px of whitespace. This results block feels like a cohesive card laid on a lighter background: the tracking-status-header stretches across the top, summarizing the shipment status with a prominent status pill and basic identifiers. Immediately below, the content splits into two columns—on the left, the shipment-summary-panel card presents key origin, destination, dates, and service info; on the right, the tracking-events-timeline runs vertically from the most recent event at the top down to the earliest, aligned with the summary card’s top edge. The manage-delivery-cta sits near the top-right of this results region (often aligned with the status header or just above the timeline), in brand orange, signaling the next primary action. At the bottom-left of the results area, the back-to-home-link appears as a subtle text link, offering a quiet exit without competing with the main CTAs. Everything is aligned to the same central grid, with consistent 8px-based spacing and a clear, left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading order that mirrors the original marketing hero’s visual rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tracking-number-input-form is placed within the hero section on the left side of the central content container, aligned flush with the container’s left edge. It sits roughly one-third down from the top of the hero, directly under the hero page heading and a brief descriptive line, forming a tight vertical stack. The form itself spans about 60–70% of the container’s width on desktop, which corresponds to roughly 40–45% of the full viewport width, keeping it firmly in the left visual column while leaving plenty of empty space around it. The input field and its associated submit button are arranged in a single horizontal row on desktop (input on the left, orange button on the right) with 8–16px between them; on smaller screens they can collapse to a stacked vertical arrangement. Vertical padding within the form (top/bottom around the input and button) uses 16–20px, with at least 24px of space above it (to the heading) and 24–32px below it (to the error banner or the bottom of the hero) to preserve the spacious, marketing-driven feel. The form is clearly the main interactive focal point in the hero and is visually pulled forward against the dark image using a subtle dark-blue tinted panel or soft drop shadow, while still respecting the clean, minimally ornamented style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form is positioned within the normal document flow, anchored to the hero’s left column and responsive with the 12-column grid. It remains centered vertically within the hero region on taller screens but shifts slightly upward on shorter screens to ensure it stays fully visible without scrolling. On smaller viewports, it expands closer to full width while maintaining internal padding based on the 8px spacing system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tracking-loading-indicator appears within the tracking-results-container region, centered both horizontally and vertically relative to the visible area of the results block. When active, it overlays the shipment-summary-panel and tracking-events-timeline with a semi-transparent dark-blue or charcoal veil, while a spinner or progress indicator sits in the middle of the results area, ensuring it is immediately visible. The overlay is confined to the width of the central content container (matching the results layout) and does not cover the global navigation or hero section. The vertical thickness of the overlay matches the height of the results content currently in view, creating the impression that the underlying cards are temporarily paused. There is at least 24px of padding around the spinner and any “Loading your shipment details” text to maintain the clean, open feel even in this transient state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loading indicator behaves as an absolutely positioned overlay within the tracking-results-container, stacking above the status header, summary panel, and timeline with a higher z-index while active. It does not affect the layout or spacing of those components; when hidden, the underlying components retain their original positions without any reflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tracking-error-banner is a full-width, slim alert bar that appears directly beneath the tracking-number-input-form within the hero’s left column. It aligns to the same left and right boundaries as the form, spanning about 60–70% of the content container width, and maintains a consistent left margin with the hero text. The banner height is relatively shallow—approximately 40–56px—using 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. It uses a high-contrast background (e.g., a warm, slightly desaturated red or orange variant) with clear error text and, optionally, a compact icon at the left. There is about 16px of vertical space between the bottom of the form and the top of the banner, and an additional 24–32px of space below the banner before the bottom edge of the hero content, so the area still feels airy even when an error is present. On smaller screens, the banner continues to sit immediately under the form, stretching to near full width with consistent padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The banner is part of the normal vertical flow of the hero’s left column. It only occupies space when an error exists, pushing any content that might appear below it downward. When dismissed or absent, the vertical spacing closes, returning the hero to its more minimal state. It is not sticky or floating and scrolls away with the hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tracking-status-header is the topmost visible element inside the tracking-results-container, positioned immediately below the hero section with a generous gap of about 48–64px to separate marketing content from data content. It spans nearly the full width of the central content container, aligning with the same left and right margins as the hero’s text block. The header has a height of approximately 80–100px, with 16–24px padding on all sides. Internally, it adopts a horizontal layout: on the left, prominent shipment title text and the tracking number are stacked; on the right, a rounded status pill (e.g., “In Transit,” “Delivered”) and possibly a small secondary line like “Last updated X min ago” align to the right edge. The header background may be a slightly lighter deep-blue or a subtle card color to distinguish it from the page background while keeping it visually tied to the brand. Below it, there is about 24px of vertical space before the two-column area with the summary and timeline begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The status header sits in the normal flow at the top of the tracking-results-container, acting as the anchor for the data section. On very small screens, its internal layout stacks vertically (status pill moving below or above the title), but it remains full-width and scrolls naturally with the rest of the content. It is not fixed or sticky by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipment-summary-panel is positioned directly beneath the tracking-status-header, aligned to the left side of the central content container and forming the left column of the results grid. On desktop, it occupies about 40% of the container width, leaving 60% to the tracking-events-timeline on the right. The top of the summary panel aligns horizontally with the top of the timeline, creating a clean row. The panel is styled as a card with a light background (white or very light gray) against a slightly darker page background, featuring 24px internal padding and a soft shadow or subtle border. Key shipment details (origin, destination, service type, key dates, recipient, etc.) are laid out in small grouped sections with 8–16px spacing between lines and 16–24px between groups, maintaining clarity without feeling crowded. There is roughly 24px of vertical space between the bottom of this panel and the elements below it (e.g., bottom of the timeline row or any subsequent info blocks), aligning with the overall spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The summary panel behaves as a standard card within the grid layout, flowing in the left column. On wider screens, it stays side-by-side with the timeline; on tablet and mobile, it naturally stacks above the tracking-events-timeline, taking full width while keeping its internal padding. It scrolls with the rest of the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tracking-events-timeline sits to the right of the shipment-summary-panel within the same horizontal row in the tracking-results-container. It occupies about 60% of the content container width on desktop and aligns its top edge with the top of the summary panel. The left edge of the timeline column is separated from the summary panel by a 24–32px gutter, following the 12-column grid. Inside, a vertical timeline runs from top to bottom, with the most recent event at the top. Each event block has 16–24px of vertical spacing between them and uses concise typography, ensuring the column can hold many events without feeling chaotic. The vertical line and event markers (dots) are center-aligned within the timeline column, with event details offset to the right, creating a clear visual path. The bottom of the timeline column ends roughly aligned with or just below the bottom of the summary panel, with 24–32px of space to the next section or the bottom of the results container, so the entire results region reads as one coherent card cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The timeline is a standard, scrollable column within the results layout. It grows vertically as more events are loaded and pushes the page height accordingly. On smaller screens, it stacks beneath the shipment-summary-panel, taking full width while keeping sufficient padding and event spacing. It does not stick or float; the user scrolls down the page to see older events.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The manage-delivery-cta is an orange primary button placed within the tracking-results-container near the top-right area of the results, making it a clear next step after reading the status. On desktop, it typically sits aligned horizontally with the tracking-status-header’s right side or slightly below it, visually attached to the header or the top of the timeline column. For example, it may appear under the status pill, right-aligned to the container’s right edge, with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the bottom of the header. The button is medium-large, with approximately 12–16px of vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, ensuring a strong yet non-overbearing presence. There is at least 16px of space between the button and any adjacent text or controls so it stands cleanly on its own. On mobile, the button can stretch closer to full width and may appear directly beneath the status header text, still near the top of the results content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The CTA is part of the normal flow inside the tracking-results-container. It remains anchored near the top of the results so users encounter it early, but it is not fixed or sticky; it scrolls out of view as the user navigates further down the timeline. On smaller viewports, it reflows into a centered or full-width block, while preserving its vertical spacing relative to the status header and subsequent content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is positioned in the lower-left area of the tracking-results-container, after the primary content (status header, summary panel, timeline, and manage-delivery-cta). It aligns with the same left margin as the shipment-summary-panel and overall content container. Vertically, it appears with about 32–40px of space above it to clearly separate it from the main data card cluster, giving it a quieter, secondary feel. The link itself is styled as a simple text link with perhaps a left-pointing arrow icon, with minimal decoration and no heavy background, so it doesn’t compete with the orange manage-delivery-cta. It occupies only as much horizontal space as needed for its text, leaving ample whitespace to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The link sits in the normal vertical flow at the bottom of the results section, scrolling away as the user moves back up. It is not fixed or sticky and does not alter the layout of other components. On smaller screens, it remains left-aligned but may be centered if the design system calls for it, while preserving clear separation from the primary CTAs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tracking-results-container is a dedicated section positioned directly below the hero tracking section, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to signal a transition from marketing content to functional data. It is centered on the page, constrained to the same max-width (~1140px) as the hero content, and uses a stacked structure: tracking-status-header at the top; beneath it, a two-column row with shipment-summary-panel on the left (about 40% width) and tracking-events-timeline on the right (about 60% width); the manage-delivery-cta near the top-right of this region; and finally, the back-to-home-link near the bottom-left. The results container may be visually grouped with a subtle top and bottom margin, and on some breakpoints it might appear as a unified card set against a slightly tinted background to distinguish it from the rest of the page while still feeling integrated. Internal vertical spacing between each major element (header, row, bottom link) follows the 24–32px increments to maintain readability and hierarchy. The tracking-loading-indicator overlay appears within this container when active, and error messages specific to retrieved data can also appear near the top of this block if needed, without altering the fundamental layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results container is a standard section in the main page flow, appearing only after a tracking number is submitted or when a valid tracking id is present in the URL. It expands vertically as content (especially within the timeline) grows. On smaller screens, its internal layout collapses gracefully into a single column: status header at top, manage-delivery-cta beneath, then the summary panel, followed by the timeline, and finally the back-to-home-link, all full-width and aligned to a single central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync-controller is non-visual and does not manifest as a visible element in the layout. Conceptually, it lives at the page level and governs whether the tracking-results-container is shown and which tracking data is loaded, based on the tracking number present in the URL or user input. It ensures that reloading the page or sharing a link returns users directly to the appropriate state (e.g., with the results displayed for a specific tracking number) without any additional visual controls or footprints on the UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This controller occupies no physical space in the layout. It does not impact spacing, alignment, or flow of any visible components. Its behavior is purely functional, controlling visibility and state of the tracking-number-input-form and tracking-results-container in response to URL changes or interactions, while leaving the visual structure unchanged.

name: Delivery Options
title: Delivery Options
description: Manage delivery options for an in-transit shipment, including rescheduling the delivery date and time window and updating delivery instructions.

core_features: Load the shipment’s current delivery details and available rescheduling options using getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(shipmentId).
Display the shipment’s current delivery date and time window alongside any existing delivery instructions.
List all available future delivery dates derived from available_time_slots and allow the user to select one.
For the selected date, display all available DeliveryTimeSlot options and allow the user to choose a single time window.
Provide a text input area to create or update delivery instructions bound to Shipment.delivery_instructions.
Validate that a delivery time slot is selected before allowing submission.
Submit the reschedule request via submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest(shipmentId, deliveryTimeSlotId, delivery_instructions).
Show a confirmation state with details from DeliveryRescheduleRequest and the updated Shipment returned by submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest.
Handle and display error states when the shipment cannot be rescheduled or when API calls fail.
Allow navigation back to the tracking page (track.html) with the shipment’s tracking_number as a URL parameter after a reschedule attempt.
user_workflows:
View and understand current delivery details:
1. User navigates from track.html to delivery_options.html?shipmentId={id}.
2. On page load, the page reads shipmentId from the URL.
3. The page calls getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(shipmentId).
4. When the response is received, the page displays the shipment’s current_delivery_date, current_time_window_start, current_time_window_end, and any existing Shipment.delivery_instructions from the shipment object.
5. If getDeliveryRescheduleOptions fails or returns data that indicates no rescheduling is possible (e.g., no available_time_slots), the page shows a clear message and disables rescheduling actions.

Reschedule delivery to a new date and time:
1. After current details load successfully, the page derives a list of distinct future dates from available_time_slots where is_available is true.
2. The user selects a desired date from this list.
3. The page filters available_time_slots to those matching the selected date and shows their start_time and end_time values.
4. The user selects one available DeliveryTimeSlot.
5. The user can review or modify delivery instructions in the text area.
6. The user clicks a primary action to submit the new delivery schedule.
7. The page validates that a DeliveryTimeSlot has been selected and that shipmentId is present in the URL.
8. If valid, the page calls submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest(shipmentId, deliveryTimeSlotId, delivery_instructions).
9. While waiting for the response, the page shows a submitting state and disables the submit action.
10. When the response returns, the page displays a confirmation using reschedule_request and the updated shipment, and updates the URL state to reflect that a reschedule attempt was made.

Update delivery instructions without changing the address:
1. User arrives at the page and current instructions (if any) from Shipment.delivery_instructions are prefilled in the instructions input.
2. The user edits the text as needed.
3. The user optionally selects a new date and time slot; if they choose to keep the same date/time (and if that same slot is still available), they may reselect that slot from available_time_slots.
4. The user submits the form, which calls submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest with the selected deliveryTimeSlotId and updated instructions.
5. The confirmation panel shows the new instructions as part of the updated shipment object if returned.

Return to tracking after rescheduling:
1. After submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest returns successfully, the page reads shipment.tracking_number from the response.
2. The page enables a "Back to tracking" interaction.
3. When the user activates this, the browser navigates to track.html?tracking_number={shipment.tracking_number}.
4. The tracking page can then show updated delivery information based on its own logic.

Handle errors and retry:
1. If getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(shipmentId) fails (for example, network error or invalid shipmentId), the page shows an error message and an option to retry.
2. When the user clicks retry, getDeliveryRescheduleOptions is called again with the same shipmentId from the URL.
3. If submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest fails, the page displays an error, keeps the user’s date/time slot selection and instructions in the form, and allows the user to correct or retry submission without losing entered data.
interactions:
Page load / initialization:
- Trigger: Browser loads delivery_options.html with URL parameter shipmentId.
- Behavior: The page-state controller validates the presence of shipmentId. If valid, it calls getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(shipmentId). If missing, it shows an error state indicating the page cannot function without a shipmentId.

Date selection:
- Trigger: User clicks or taps on one of the available delivery dates derived from available_time_slots.
- Behavior: The selected date is stored in the page state and mirrored into the URL as selectedDate (e.g., ?shipmentId={id}&selectedDate={dateValue}). The time-slot selector component updates to show only slots whose date matches selectedDate and is_available is true.

Time slot selection:
- Trigger: User selects a specific DeliveryTimeSlot for the current selectedDate.
- Behavior: The selected DeliveryTimeSlot.id is stored in page state and mirrored into the URL as deliveryTimeSlotId. Only one slot can be selected at a time. If the user selects a different slot, the previous selection is cleared.

Delivery instructions input:
- Trigger: User types into the instructions text area.
- Behavior: The component updates a local delivery_instructions value in page state on every change. This value can be initialized from shipment.delivery_instructions returned by getDeliveryRescheduleOptions. The instructions are not sent to the server until submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest is called.

Submit reschedule request:
- Trigger: User clicks the primary submission button in the reschedule form.
- Behavior:
  1. The form validates that shipmentId exists and that a deliveryTimeSlotId is selected.
  2. If validation fails, the form shows inline validation errors and prevents submission.
  3. If validation succeeds, the form calls submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest(shipmentId, deliveryTimeSlotId, delivery_instructions).
  4. While waiting for the response, the submit button is disabled and the form shows a loading indicator.
  5. On success, the form passes reschedule_request and updated shipment to the confirmation component and updates URL state (for example, adding rescheduleStatus and rescheduleRequestId parameters).
  6. On error, the form shows an error message and re-enables the submit button.

View confirmation details:
- Trigger: submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest returns successfully.
- Behavior: The confirmation component displays values from reschedule_request (such as previous_delivery_date, previous_time_window_start, previous_time_window_end, new_delivery_date, new_time_window_start, new_time_window_end, and status) and may use the updated shipment to show updated delivery_instructions. The state indicating that a confirmation is visible can be represented in the URL with a parameter (e.g., confirmationVisible=true).

Back to tracking navigation:
- Trigger: User activates the "Back to tracking" interaction after a reschedule attempt.
- Behavior: The page reads shipment.tracking_number from the most recent shipment object in state (either from getDeliveryRescheduleOptions or from submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest response). It then changes location to track.html?tracking_number={shipment.tracking_number}.

Retry loading options:
- Trigger: User activates a retry control when loading reschedule options fails.
- Behavior: The page calls getDeliveryRescheduleOptions again with the shipmentId from the URL and updates loading/error states accordingly.

Change selection via URL (deep linking / refresh):
- Trigger: User reloads the page or manually modifies selectedDate or deliveryTimeSlotId in the URL query string.
- Behavior: On initialization, after getDeliveryRescheduleOptions completes, the state controller checks if selectedDate or deliveryTimeSlotId are present in the URL and, if they correspond to available_time_slots, preselects those values in the date and time slot selectors.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL query string, specifically the shipmentId parameter. On every page load, the page reads shipmentId from delivery_options.html?shipmentId={value}. If shipmentId is missing, the page shows a non-functional error state because no interfaces can be called. If shipmentId is present, the page calls getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(shipmentId) once on initialization. The response populates in-memory state for shipment, current_delivery_date, current_time_window_start, current_time_window_end, and available_time_slots. Starting from this data, the page exposes additional state via URL parameters to make the user’s selections and outcomes shareable within the session: selectedDate is set when the user chooses a date derived from available_time_slots; deliveryTimeSlotId is set when a specific DeliveryTimeSlot is selected; confirmationVisible and optionally rescheduleStatus and rescheduleRequestId are set after submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest returns, reflecting that a reschedule attempt occurred and its status. These parameters are read after every getDeliveryRescheduleOptions call to restore selections if they match currently available_time_slots. No persistent client storage such as localStorage is used; all state beyond memory is represented by URL parameters and can be reconstructed only from the current getDeliveryRescheduleOptions response and the immediate submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest response present in memory during that page session.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, initial data loading with getDeliveryRescheduleOptions, and propagation of shipment and available_time_slots to child components. Manages derived state for selectedDate, selectedDeliveryTimeSlotId, and confirmation visibility, while keeping these in sync with the URL.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads shipmentId from URL; if present, invokes getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(shipmentId). If absent, sets a fatal error state.

onGetDeliveryRescheduleOptionsSuccess: Stores shipment and available_time_slots, then reads selectedDate and deliveryTimeSlotId from URL and validates them against available_time_slots to initialize selections.
onGetDeliveryRescheduleOptionsError: Sets a load-error state used by the feedback component.
onDateChange: Updates internal selectedDate state, updates URL selectedDate, clears invalid deliveryTimeSlotId if it no longer matches available_time_slots for the new date.
onTimeSlotChange: Updates internal selectedDeliveryTimeSlotId state and URL deliveryTimeSlotId to the selected DeliveryTimeSlot.id.

onSubmitRescheduleSuccess: Receives reschedule_request and updated shipment from submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest, stores them, and updates URL parameters (e.g., confirmationVisible, rescheduleStatus, rescheduleRequestId).
onSubmitRescheduleError: Sets a submission-error state while preserving current selections and instructions.
onUrlChange: When the browser URL changes (e.g., manual edit, navigation actions), re-reads selection parameters and aligns internal state with any valid values.

components.functionality: Displays the current delivery plan for the shipment, including current_delivery_date, current_time_window_start, current_time_window_end, and any existing delivery instructions. It helps the user understand what will change when rescheduling.
event_handlers: onShipmentDataChange: When shipment or current_* fields update from getDeliveryRescheduleOptions or from submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest, re-renders the summary to reflect the latest delivery details.

components.functionality: Presents a list or calendar of available delivery dates derived from available_time_slots where is_available is true. Allows the user to select one date to see and choose time windows for that date.
event_handlers: onOptionsChange: When available_time_slots are loaded or updated, recomputes the set of distinct available dates and updates the UI accordingly.
onDateClick: When the user selects a date, emits the selected date value to page-state-manager.onDateChange for state and URL synchronization.

components.functionality: Shows all available DeliveryTimeSlot entries for the currently selectedDate and allows the user to choose a single time window for rescheduled delivery.
event_handlers: onSelectedDateChange: Filters available_time_slots to those matching selectedDate and is_available = true.

onTimeSlotClick: When the user selects a time slot, emits the corresponding DeliveryTimeSlot.id to page-state-manager.onTimeSlotChange so it becomes the selectedDeliveryTimeSlotId and updates the URL.
onOptionsChange: If available_time_slots change (e.g., after a refresh), validates the currently selectedDeliveryTimeSlotId and clears it if it is no longer available for selectedDate.

components.functionality: Provides a multi-line text input where the user can enter or update delivery instructions, initialized from Shipment.delivery_instructions if available.
data_binding: State: editedDeliveryInstructions (local to this component, propagated to page-state-manager and submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest)
event_handlers: onInit: Initializes editedDeliveryInstructions from shipment.delivery_instructions when the shipment data is first loaded.
onChange: Updates editedDeliveryInstructions as the user types and notifies the page-state-manager to keep the latest value ready for submission.

onShipmentUpdate: If shipment is updated after a successful reschedule (from submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest.shipment), reloads editedDeliveryInstructions from the updated shipment.delivery_instructions so the displayed text matches server state.

components.functionality: Orchestrates rescheduling submission by validating the chosen date and time slot and sending updated instructions via submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest. It does not change the delivery address; it only reschedules date/time and instructions.
event_handlers: onSubmitClick: Validates that shipmentId exists and selectedDeliveryTimeSlotId is set. If validation passes, calls submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest(shipmentId, selectedDeliveryTimeSlotId, editedDeliveryInstructions). If validation fails, triggers inline validation messages.
onSubmittingStateChange: When a submission is in progress, disables the submit button and propagates a loading state to the feedback component.
onSubmitSuccess: Receives reschedule_request and updated shipment, and calls page-state-manager.onSubmitRescheduleSuccess to update global state and URL parameters.
onSubmitError: Calls page-state-manager.onSubmitRescheduleError, displays an error message, and re-enables the submit button so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Shows confirmation or failure details after a reschedule attempt, based on DeliveryRescheduleRequest.status and the updated Shipment. It highlights the previous and new delivery date/time and confirms any updated instructions.
event_handlers: onSubmitRescheduleSuccess: When called by page-state-manager, updates the confirmation panel with reschedule_request and shipment details and sets confirmationVisible in state and URL.
onVisibilityChange: If confirmationVisible is toggled via URL or internal state, shows or hides the panel accordingly.

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the shipment’s tracking page using the tracking_number from the shipment object.
event_handlers: onClick: Reads the most recent available shipment.tracking_number (preferring the updated shipment from submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest if present; otherwise using the shipment from getDeliveryRescheduleOptions) and navigates the browser to track.html?tracking_number={tracking_number}.

components.functionality: Displays loading indicators, success messages, and error messages for both data loading and submission processes. It centralizes user feedback about the status of getDeliveryRescheduleOptions and submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest.
data_binding: State: isLoadingOptions (from page-state-manager during getDeliveryRescheduleOptions calls)
State: optionsLoadError (from page-state-manager on getDeliveryRescheduleOptions errors)
State: isSubmittingReschedule (from delivery-reschedule-form during submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest calls)
State: rescheduleSubmitError (from page-state-manager or delivery-reschedule-form on submitDeliveryRescheduleRequest errors)
event_handlers: onGetDeliveryRescheduleOptionsStart: Shows a loading indicator while initial options are being fetched.
onGetDeliveryRescheduleOptionsSuccess: Hides the loading indicator and clears any previous load error.
onGetDeliveryRescheduleOptionsError: Shows an error message explaining that delivery options could not be loaded and offers a retry action.
onRetryClick: Invokes getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(shipmentId) again via page-state-manager when the user chooses to retry loading.
onSubmitStart: Shows a submitting indicator and clears prior submission errors.
onSubmitError: Displays a submission error message and optionally suggests that the user try again later.
onSubmitSuccess: Shows a brief confirmation message that the reschedule request has been submitted, in addition to the details shown in the confirmation panel.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same top-to-bottom marketing flow as the mockup: a full-width hero-like main section centered in a fixed-width container (~1140px) sitting over a full-bleed background. Within this main section, the current-delivery-summary acts as the hero text block on the left, with the reschedule interaction (delivery-reschedule-form and its child selectors/inputs) stacked directly beneath it in a clean, linear column. The right half of the hero area is kept visually lighter, allowing the global logistics imagery and brand color fields to show through without crowding the functional controls. The back-to-tracking-link is a slim utility element above the hero content, and feedback-and-error-messages appear just below the hero summary and above the form so they are immediately noticed but do not dominate the layout. The reschedule-confirmation-panel reuses the same hero content area as the form, swapping into place when needed, controlled by the page-state-manager.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same top-to-bottom marketing flow as the mockup: a full-width hero-like main section centered in a fixed-width container (~1140px) sitting over a full-bleed background. Within this main section, the current-delivery-summary acts as the hero text block on the left, with the reschedule interaction (delivery-reschedule-form and its child selectors/inputs) stacked directly beneath it in a clean, linear column. The right half of the hero area is kept visually lighter, allowing the global logistics imagery and brand color fields to show through without crowding the functional controls. The back-to-tracking-link is a slim utility element above the hero content, and feedback-and-error-messages appear just below the hero summary and above the form so they are immediately noticed but do not dominate the layout. The reschedule-confirmation-panel reuses the same hero content area as the form, swapping into place when needed, controlled by the page-state-manager.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content area sits within a centered content column approximately 1140px wide on desktop, aligned to a 12-column grid. The hero band (containing current-delivery-summary plus either the form or the confirmation panel) occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height, with generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) to mirror the mockup’s airy hero. Within this band, the left 6–7 columns (about 55–60% of the width) are reserved for the summary and form elements, while the right 5–6 columns (40–45%) remain mostly open for imagery or background, reinforcing the left-aligned focus. Individually, form fields and panels use padding and gaps in multiples of 8px (e.g., 24–32px inner padding, 16–24px vertical spacing) to maintain rhythm. The right edge of the viewport remains visually clear for the site’s global floating toolbar (from the original design), which does not collide with these components.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a spacious, marketing-oriented density even though it supports a functional flow. Only one primary task is visible at a time (either filling in the reschedule form or viewing the confirmation). Each major block (summary, form, confirmation) sits within its own visually separated area with ample whitespace: wide margins inside cards, clear vertical breathing room between the headline, details, and controls, and comfortable spacing between form fields. Inputs are not packed tightly; instead, each selector or text area has room around it to feel approachable and easy to scan, echoing the low-density hero in the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a similar F/Z-pattern to the mockup but applied to a functional page. The eye starts at the top left with the back-to-tracking-link, then drops slightly to the bold current-delivery-summary headline, which acts as the first major focal point. From there, the gaze moves diagonally down and slightly right into the delivery-reschedule-form fields (date, then time, then instructions), anchored by a primary orange action button at the bottom of the form. The surrounding right-hand whitespace and subtle background imagery draw a secondary diagonal toward the right edge, softly echoing the original hero’s right-side emphasis without competing with the form. Feedback-and-error-messages appear near the top of this flow, just under the summary, so they’re read before a user continues down into the form inputs.

layout.overall_description: The Delivery Options page presents a hero-style main section over a full-bleed dark blue, technology-forward background with faint global or satellite imagery concentrated toward the right side. Centered on the page is a wide content container that aligns with the site’s overall grid. At the very top of this container, a slim back-to-tracking-link sits left-aligned as a light utility item. Just below, the current-delivery-summary occupies the upper-left of the hero band, with a large, confident headline and key shipment details stacked vertically and aligned to the left, leaving the right half of the hero band mostly open to the background image. Directly beneath the summary and still within the left 60% of the container, a card-like reschedule area either shows the delivery-reschedule-form or, after a successful change, the reschedule-confirmation-panel. The form arranges the date and time selectors side by side on desktop, with the instructions input stretching full width beneath them, all generously spaced and anchored by a strong primary CTA in brand orange. Feedback-and-error-messages appear as a full-width bar or compact card just under the summary and above the form, ensuring they sit in the main vertical reading path without crowding the hero heading. The overall effect is a clean, modern corporate interface: a bold left-aligned functional hero that matches the original marketing hero’s proportions and flow while providing a focused, single-task experience for managing delivery options, with the right side visually lighter and reserved for brand imagery and breathable whitespace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-manager exists as an invisible logic layer tied to the main hero content area rather than occupying visible space. Conceptually, it is anchored to the main content container that starts just below the global navigation and utility bar, spanning the full content width (~1140px). It governs whether the central hero body area shows the delivery-reschedule-form or the reschedule-confirmation-panel, but does not render any UI itself. From a layout perspective, it simply reserves a single, consistent region below the current-delivery-summary where one of these two panels is mounted at any given time, maintaining identical dimensions and alignment for both states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, logical controller only; no direct spatial footprint. It ensures that either the form or the confirmation panel is mounted in the same left-aligned hero region beneath the summary, with smooth state transitions but no independent scrolling or positioning behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current-delivery-summary is positioned as the hero text block of the page, anchored at the top-left of the main content container. It sits approximately 64px below the global navigation/utility strip, spanning about 6–7 columns of the 12-column grid (roughly 55–60% of the container width). The block is left-aligned, with its headline in large white or light text against the dark blue hero background, and shipment details stacked vertically underneath with 8–16px spacing between lines. There is generous padding above and below this block (about 32–48px vertical) to clearly separate it from both the back-to-tracking-link above and the reschedule area below. The right side of the hero area, horizontally aligned with this summary, is left mostly open, allowing the global imagery and background gradients to show, creating an asymmetrical layout that visually echoes the original hero design. On smaller viewports, this summary remains at the top and continues to span nearly the full width, but maintains more vertical spacing to preserve hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; it forms the top portion of a hero section that visually occupies 70–80% of the initial viewport height. It does not scroll independently, but as the user scrolls, the entire hero (summary plus form/confirmation) slides off-screen together.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-date-selector is a key field within the delivery-reschedule-form and is positioned in the upper-left of the form card. The form card itself is centered within the main container but aligned to the same left margin as the current-delivery-summary. Inside the card, the date selector occupies roughly the left half of the content area (about 5–6 columns of the grid, or ~50% width), with a clear label above it and the input control below. There is approximately 24px vertical space separating the bottom of the current-delivery-summary from the top of the form card, and 24–32px padding inside the card. Horizontally, the date selector aligns to the left edge of the card and sits in parallel with the delivery-time-slot-selector on its right, creating a two-column row that matches the mockup’s balanced but spacious horizontal layout style. On smaller screens, the date selector stacks above the time slot selector, each taking 100% of the card width with 16–24px vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form card. On desktop, it participates in a two-column layout; on tablet/mobile breakpoints, it reflows to a single-column stack, maintaining full-width usability while preserving generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-time-slot-selector is placed directly to the right of the delivery-date-selector within the top row of the delivery-reschedule-form card. It occupies the right half of the card’s content area, mirroring the date selector’s width (about 5–6 grid columns, or ~50% of the card width), and is aligned so that their top edges form a clean horizontal line. A label sits above the time slot control with 4–8px spacing, and the control itself is positioned with 16–24px spacing from the date selector horizontally, ensuring a clear separation while still reading as a paired choice. The combined width of the date and time selectors does not exceed the left 60% of the overall page container, keeping them aligned under the hero summary and leaving the right hero side visually lighter. On narrower viewports, the time-slot selector drops below the date selector, matching its left alignment and width, with ample padding inside the form card so that each control still feels distinct and easy to tap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; part of a responsive two-column row that collapses into a single column on smaller screens. It maintains alignment with the date selector to preserve a clear, scannable horizontal grouping on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-instructions-input is positioned directly below the row containing the date and time selectors, inside the same delivery-reschedule-form card. It spans nearly the full inner width of the card, aligning to the same left margin as the date selector and extending to the right margin, effectively occupying the full 10–12 grid columns available inside the card’s left-aligned region. A label sits above the multi-line input with about 4–8px spacing, and there is a generous vertical gap (16–24px) separating it from the fields above to clearly delineate the second section of the form. The instructions area itself is taller than standard inputs, visually balancing the blocky hero feel and adding perceived weight to the lower part of the form. Beneath this input, additional vertical spacing (24–32px) leads into the primary CTA button row, ensuring a clear, stepwise flow down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and full-width within the form card. On all breakpoints it maintains its position as a single, broad block beneath the date/time row, simply adjusting width with the card while preserving comfortable vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-reschedule-form appears as a prominent, card-like panel directly beneath the current-delivery-summary, aligned to the same left margin within the central content container. Vertically, it starts approximately 24–32px below the summary, and horizontally it spans about 6–7 columns of the grid (roughly 55–60% of the container width), matching the width of the summary to create a unified hero column. The form background is slightly elevated from the dark hero backdrop (either via a lighter tint or subtle surface) with 24–32px inner padding, rounded corners, and a subtle shadow consistent with the design DNA. Inside the form, content is arranged vertically from top to bottom: optional inline feedback-and-error-messages at the very top (if present), then the date and time selectors in a two-column row, followed by the full-width delivery-instructions-input, and finally a horizontally aligned action row at the bottom with a strong primary orange button on the left and any secondary actions (like cancel) to its right. Generous vertical spacing (16–24px) separates each row. The right side of the hero band remains mostly empty beyond the form’s right edge, preserving the mockup’s negative space and background imagery. On smaller screens, the form expands to use nearly the full width of the container, but still maintains top alignment with the summary and preserves the vertical hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static primary panel within the hero section, occupying the same spatial slot as the reschedule-confirmation-panel. The page-state-manager swaps this form in or out without changing its position or width; there is no independent scrolling inside the form—the page scroll handles overflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reschedule-confirmation-panel occupies the exact same spatial region as the delivery-reschedule-form, acting as its visual replacement after a successful submission. It is aligned to the left within the main content container, beginning about 24–32px below the current-delivery-summary and spanning the same 6–7 grid columns (~55–60% width). The panel shares the same card-like treatment—rounded corners, subtle elevation, and 24–32px internal padding—but its internal layout is simpler and more hero-like: a prominent success icon or checkmark aligned near the top left, a bold confirmation headline immediately to the right or just below, a concise summary of the new delivery date/time/instructions presented in a short, vertically stacked list, and a primary action row at the bottom with a bright orange “Back to tracking” or similar CTA, supplemented by more subtle secondary options if needed. Vertical spacing remains generous (16–24px between sections), and the right half of the hero band continues to be open, maintaining the same balance as when the form is visible. This consistency ensures the page feels stable even as the content state changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero panel that is mutually exclusive with the delivery-reschedule-form. Controlled by the page-state-manager, it occupies the same coordinates and width, with smooth transitions but no separate scrolling or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-tracking-link is positioned at the very top-left of the main content container, just below the global navigation/utility bar and above the current-delivery-summary. It appears as a slim, understated text link (optionally with a left-facing chevron icon) and occupies only a small horizontal footprint (1–2 grid columns), leaving ample whitespace around it. Vertically, there is approximately 16–24px of spacing between this link and the top edge of the hero background area, and another 16–24px between the link and the top of the current-delivery-summary. This placement echoes the thin utility strip concept from the mockup: it is clearly visible for users needing to return to tracking, but visually subordinate to the bold hero summary and form. On smaller screens, it remains pinned to the top-left of the content column, preserving its role as the first interactive item in the F-pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static utility link positioned at the top of the main content flow. It scrolls away with the page and has no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback-and-error-messages area is positioned so it is encountered early in the user’s vertical scanning path without overshadowing the hero summary. When related to the delivery-reschedule-form, it appears as a full-width bar or compact card embedded at the top of the form card, immediately below the lower edge of the current-delivery-summary and just inside the form’s top padding. In this case, it spans the full internal width of the form (matching the 6–7 column width of the hero column), with 16px vertical padding and 16–24px separation from the fields below, ensuring messages are prominent yet not cramped. For more global messages that apply to the entire page state, the same component can instead appear directly beneath the current-delivery-summary and above the form card, still left-aligned and matching the hero column width, with 16–24px vertical spacing above and below. The styling should make it clearly noticeable (using brand accent colors for errors or success) but thin enough in height so that the hero layout remains visually dominant. On smaller screens, this area continues to span the full width of the content column, stacking above the form fields with consistent spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, but its exact vertical position can shift between being embedded at the top of the form or sitting just under the summary, depending on whether the message is form-specific or page-level. It does not float or stick; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the hero content.

name: Find Locations
title: Find Locations
description: Search for nearby logistics locations by address or postal code, refine results by distance, type, international capabilities, and opening hours, and view details for a selected location.

core_features: Location search form that accepts a free-text address or postal code and an optional radius, and executes the searchLocations interface.
Filter controls for location types, international shipment acceptance, and weekday opening time, mapped to searchLocations parameters.
Sorting control for ordering results (e.g., by distance) via the searchLocations sort_option parameter.
Results list that displays locations returned from searchLocations with key details such as address, distance, and indicative weekday opening hours.
Optional synchronized map view that plots the same searchLocations results using their coordinates.
Clickable result items that navigate to the Location Detail page with the selected location ID in the URL.
Navigation link to the Saved Locations page.
URL-driven state: all search inputs, filters, and sorting options are reflected in URL parameters and used to rehydrate the page and query searchLocations on load or when the URL changes.
user_workflows:
1) Basic search from scratch:
   - User navigates to locations_search.html (e.g., via header navigation) without URL parameters.
   - URL-state manager initializes with empty or system-default values for query, radius_km, location_types, accepts_international_shipments, weekday_open_after_time, and sort_option.
   - User enters an address or postal code in the search field and optionally adjusts radius.
   - User submits the form.
   - The page updates the URL parameters with the current form and filter values.
   - searchLocations is called with parameters read from the updated URL.
   - Results list and map (if visible) render locations from the searchLocations response.
   - User reviews locations and optionally clicks a result to open location_detail.html with the selected locationId.

2) Deep-linked search with prefilled filters:
   - User arrives on locations_search.html with some or all of the following URL parameters predefined: query, radius_km, location_types, accepts_international_shipments, weekday_open_after_time, sort_option.
   - URL-state manager parses the URL and initializes the search form, filters, and sort control to match these parameter values.
   - Without any user action, searchLocations is called with the parsed parameters.
   - Results list and map display the matching locations.
   - User may adjust any filter or sort option; the page updates the URL accordingly and re-calls searchLocations, refreshing the results.
   - User can bookmark or share the URL for the exact same search configuration to be reloaded later.

3) Refining results by type, international capability, and opening hours:
   - After an initial search has run, searchLocations has returned a set of locations.
   - User selects one or more location types (e.g., drop_off, service_center) in the filter panel.
   - User optionally toggles the "Accepts international shipments" filter and/or sets a specific weekday_open_after_time using a time input control.
   - On each filter change, the filter panel updates the corresponding URL parameters (location_types, accepts_international_shipments, weekday_open_after_time) and triggers a search refresh.
   - URL-state manager reads the updated parameters and calls searchLocations with all current search criteria.
   - Results list and map render the new filtered set of locations, which may also indicate which locations satisfy the time filter via is_open_after_filter_time.

4) Sorting and viewing details:
   - With search results displayed, user adjusts the sort option (e.g., selects a distance-based sort).
   - Sort control updates the sort_option URL parameter and triggers a search refresh.
   - searchLocations executes with the new sort_option and returns the results in the requested order.
   - User clicks a location list item (or a map marker action) representing a specific Location.
   - The click handler reads the location.id from the selected result and navigates to location_detail.html?locationId=<location.id>.
   - Alternatively, user clicks the "View Saved Locations" navigation link to open saved_locations.html (no additional parameters).
