interactions: On page load (onInit): parse URL parameters (query, radius_km, location_types, accepts_international_shipments, weekday_open_after_time, sort_option), initialize the search form and filters, then call searchLocations using these values.

On search form submit (onSubmit): prevent default form submission, update URL parameters with current query and radius input values (preserving any existing filter/sort parameters), and call searchLocations again based on the updated URL.
On search query input change (onChange): optionally enable or disable the submit button depending on validation (e.g., whether query is non-empty); no API call until submit.
On radius input change (onChange): update the in-page form state; when the form is submitted, write radius_km to the URL and trigger the search.
On location type filter change (onChange): transform selected types into a comma-separated string, update the location_types URL parameter, and re-run searchLocations.

On "Accepts international shipments" filter toggle (onChange): convert the checkbox state into a string value ("true" or "false"), update accepts_international_shipments in the URL, and re-run searchLocations.
On opening hours time filter change (onChange): validate the input format (HH:MM) if necessary, update weekday_open_after_time in the URL, and re-run searchLocations.
On sort option change (onChange): update sort_option in the URL and re-run searchLocations so the backend can return results in the requested order.

On receiving searchLocations results (onSuccess): bind the returned array of result objects to the results list and map components; each object exposes location (Location entity), distance_km, and is_open_after_filter_time.
On click of a result list item (onClickLocationItem): read the selected location.id from the bound data, construct the URL location_detail.html?locationId=<id>, and navigate to it.
On click of a map marker (onClickMapMarker): synchronize selection with the corresponding list item and optionally navigate to the location detail using the same logic as the list item.
On click of the "View Saved Locations" link (onClick): navigate to saved_locations.html without altering search state.
On URL change via browser navigation (onPopState or equivalent): re-parse query parameters into page state and re-execute searchLocations to keep the UI consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page is fully driven by URL query parameters, which represent the current search state. On initialization, the URL-state manager reads the following parameters: query (string), radius_km (number), location_types (comma-separated string of Location.location_type values), accepts_international_shipments ("true"/"false" string), weekday_open_after_time (HH:MM string), and sort_option (string). If some parameters are absent, the page uses internal system defaults but does not hardcode any specific numeric or textual values in the specification. These parsed values are used both to pre-populate the search form, filters, and sort controls, and to call searchLocations. Any interaction that changes search criteria or sorting updates the corresponding URL parameters before calling searchLocations again. For example, submitting the search form updates query and radius_km; adjusting filters rewrites location_types, accepts_international_shipments, and weekday_open_after_time; changing the sort selection rewrites sort_option. The searchLocations interface always receives its parameters from the current URL state, ensuring that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same results. When navigating away (e.g., to location_detail.html or saved_locations.html), the current search URL remains bookmarkable. Browser back/forward navigation triggers a re-parse of the URL and a fresh searchLocations call, keeping the UI synchronized with URL history.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading from and writing to URL query parameters that represent the search state, and orchestrates calls to searchLocations when the URL changes.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse URL parameters into an internal search state model and trigger the initial call to searchLocations with those values.
onUrlChange: When the browser history changes (e.g., back/forward), re-parse the URL, update the internal search state, and re-call searchLocations.

onSearchStateUpdate: When child components request a state change (e.g., form submit, filter change), merge the changes into the current state, write them into the URL as query parameters, and trigger searchLocations with the updated state.
onSearchLocationsResponse: Receive results from searchLocations and dispatch them to the results list and map components.

components.functionality: Collects the core search inputs: free-text location query (address or postal code) and an optional distance radius in kilometers, then triggers a search based on these values.
data_binding: URL: radius_km (for initial radius input value)
event_handlers: onInit: Read initial values for query and radius_km from the URL-state manager and populate the form inputs.
onChangeQuery: Update internal form state with the new query value and optionally perform inline validation; does not call searchLocations until submission.
onChangeRadius: Update internal form state with the new radius_km value and perform validation (e.g., numeric); actual search occurs on submission.
onSubmit: Prevent default page reload, send the form's query and radius_km values to the url-state-manager as a state update request, which updates the URL and invokes searchLocations.

components.functionality: Provides controls to filter search results by location type, international shipment acceptance, and weekday opening time, translating user selections into URL parameters understood by searchLocations.
data_binding: Location.location_type (for filter options, aligned with enum values: drop_off, pickup, service_center, locker, partner_store)
URL: accepts_international_shipments (for initial international toggle state)
URL: weekday_open_after_time (for initial time filter value)
event_handlers: onInit: Read current location_types, accepts_international_shipments, and weekday_open_after_time from the URL-state manager and set the initial state of checkboxes/multi-select and time input.

onChangeLocationTypes: Convert the current set of selected location types into a comma-separated string, request a search state update from the url-state-manager (which writes location_types to the URL) and trigger searchLocations.

onToggleAcceptsInternational: Convert the checkbox state to a string ("true" or "false"), request a search state update (accepts_international_shipments) from the url-state-manager, and trigger searchLocations.

onChangeWeekdayOpenTime: Validate and normalize the time input as an HH:MM string if needed, request a search state update (weekday_open_after_time) from the url-state-manager, and trigger searchLocations.
onClearFilters: Reset filter controls to their default states and request removal or reset of related URL parameters through the url-state-manager, which then re-calls searchLocations.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose how the search results are ordered, by setting the sort_option parameter used by searchLocations.
data_binding: URL: sort_option (for initial selected sort option)
event_handlers: onInit: Read the current sort_option from the URL-state manager and set the sort dropdown or selection control accordingly.
onChangeSortOption: When the user picks a new sort mode, request a search state update from the url-state-manager that updates sort_option in the URL and triggers a new call to searchLocations.

components.functionality: Displays the list of locations returned from searchLocations with essential details and provides navigation to the Location Detail page when a location is selected.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When url-state-manager receives new results from searchLocations, render the list based on the response data, showing each location's name, address fields, distance_km, and indicative weekday opening info using the Location fields.
onClickLocationItem: Capture the selected item's location.id from the bound data and navigate to location_detail.html?locationId=<location.id>.
onHoverLocationItem: Optionally notify the map component to highlight the corresponding marker for better spatial context.

onEmptyResults: If searchLocations returns an empty array, display a functional empty-state message controlled by configuration (without hardcoded example locations) and surface options to adjust filters or broaden the search.

components.functionality: Optionally renders a map view of the locations returned by searchLocations, synchronized with the results list for spatial exploration.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When new results arrive from searchLocations, update map markers using each Location's coordinates and basic details.

onClickMapMarker: Identify the corresponding result item by location.id, optionally request the results list component to scroll/highlight that item, and provide an action (e.g., in a marker popup) to navigate to location_detail.html?locationId=<location.id>.
onHoverMapMarker: Optionally inform the results list to highlight the related list entry for improved linkage between map and list.

onMapViewportChange: Optionally, emit the current map bounds or center to other components for informational purposes; this does not directly change searchLocations parameters unless explicitly wired through url-state-manager in future extensions.

components.functionality: Shows loading, success, and error states for the searchLocations requests so users understand the status of their search.
data_binding: Interface: searchLocations (request status and any error metadata derived from the interface call)
event_handlers: onSearchStart: When url-state-manager starts a searchLocations call, display a loading indicator and optionally disable inputs that should not change mid-request.
onSearchSuccess: When results are successfully returned, hide loading indicators and ensure the results list and map components render the new data.

onSearchError: When searchLocations fails, hide loading indicators and display an error message controlled by configuration, without embedding static example results. Provide an action to retry the search by re-invoking searchLocations with the current URL-based state.

components.functionality: Provides access to the user's Saved Locations page from the Find Locations page.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate directly to saved_locations.html. This interaction does not modify or depend on the searchLocations interface or current URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s top-heavy hero structure, but adapt the hero to be a functional search header. The page flows from top to bottom: (1) a thin utility strip and primary navigation (inherited from the site, not part of this spec), (2) a dominant, full-width hero band used for the Find Locations search experience, (3) a main results area directly beneath, split horizontally into a left content column (filters + list) and a right visual column (map), and (4) a floating vertical action element on the far right edge of the viewport. The search form and its status feedback sit on the left side of the hero, while secondary, more exploratory content (map, saved link) is pulled toward the right edge, mirroring the original hero’s text-left, controls-right pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s top-heavy hero structure, but adapt the hero to be a functional search header. The page flows from top to bottom: (1) a thin utility strip and primary navigation (inherited from the site, not part of this spec), (2) a dominant, full-width hero band used for the Find Locations search experience, (3) a main results area directly beneath, split horizontally into a left content column (filters + list) and a right visual column (map), and (4) a floating vertical action element on the far right edge of the viewport. The search form and its status feedback sit on the left side of the hero, while secondary, more exploratory content (map, saved link) is pulled toward the right edge, mirroring the original hero’s text-left, controls-right pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The search hero occupies roughly 65–75% of the initial viewport height with a full-bleed, darkened world/satellite background. Within this hero, the central content container is constrained to about 1140px and centered; the search block takes the left ~55% of this container, leaving the remaining ~45% visually lighter to let the background image breathe. Below the hero, the main results section fills the remaining page height with a two-column layout: the left column occupies roughly 40–45% of the content width (housing filters and the list), and the right column roughly 55–60% (housing the map, which may bleed slightly to the right to feel expansive). A narrow, floating vertical saved-link element occupies about 5–7% of the viewport width on the far right, stacked above the content and not affecting layout flow.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a marketing-style spaciousness despite this being a utility page. The hero search area uses large typography and generous gaps (24–48px) between the headline, search inputs, and feedback text. Form controls have ample padding and breathing room horizontally. The results section below is denser but still airy: filters are grouped with clear 16–24px vertical separation, list items present as roomy cards with 16–24px internal padding and 16px gaps between cards, and the map has clean margins with minimal UI chrome. Overall, vertical section spacing between hero and results uses 48–64px to echo the mockup’s clear section breaks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z pattern: users start at the top left with the page title and search controls in the hero, scan right across the hero band to notice the world-map background, then move diagonally down-left into the filters and results list. From the list, the eye is naturally drawn horizontally across to the right-side map. The primary action (search/submit) is rendered as a bold orange button within the search form, acting as a key focal point. The floating vertical saved-link element on the far right edge echoes the mockup’s right contact toolbar, becoming a subtle secondary attractor once the main hero and map have anchored attention.

layout.overall_description: The Find Locations page opens with a tall, full-width hero section set against a darkened global/satellite image that fills roughly two-thirds of the first viewport. Inside a centered 1140px content container, the left half is dominated by a bold white headline like “Find a nearby location” and a large, multi-field search form framed in deep blue and accented with an orange primary button. The right half of the hero remains visually lighter, allowing the global imagery to show through and suggesting the international reach of the service. Directly below, after a generous 48–64px of vertical breathing space, the layout transitions into a functional split-screen: on the left, a vertical filter panel runs down the page with a sorted list of locations beneath it; on the right, a large, map canvas stretches from just under the hero down toward the footer, subtly bleeding toward the right edge for impact. The map remains visually dominant and often sticky as users scroll the list. A slim, vertical saved-locations link floats along the far right edge of the viewport, midway down the screen, echoing the mockup’s right-hand contact toolbar without interrupting the main content. The result is a modern, spacious, and purpose-driven layout that retains the original hero’s marketing drama while supporting a highly practical search and browse flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it lives at the top level of the page structure, spanning the full width and height of the document, orchestrating URL parameters for the search form, filters, sort order, and selected map location. It should not create any padding, margins, or visual container; instead, it passively tracks and updates state behind the scenes while the visible components (hero, filters, list, map) define the actual spatial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered, document-level behavior; no box model footprint and no impact on surrounding components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits within the hero section as the primary focal element, positioned in the left half of the centered content container. It is vertically centered within the hero band (which occupies about 65–75% of the viewport height), with at least 64px of padding to the top and bottom edges of the hero. Horizontally, the form spans roughly 55–60% of the container width, aligned to the left, leaving a clear right-hand area for the background imagery to be visible. The form appears as a slightly elevated, deep-blue card with rounded corners and generous internal padding (24–32px). At the top of this card sits the hero page title and short supporting copy; below, the main input row arranges the address/postal code field, distance selector, and type selector in a single horizontal line where space allows, with 16–24px gaps between them. The primary orange search button is positioned at the far right of this row, aligned to the baseline of the inputs, and sized generously (about 48px tall, with 24–32px horizontal padding) to echo the bold CTA button in the mockup. On narrower viewports, these fields stack vertically while maintaining 16–24px vertical separation. The left edge of the form aligns with the left margin of the grid (e.g., starting around column 2 of the 12-column layout), maintaining a clear 24–32px inset from the hero’s left boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; it moves with page scroll but remains visually dominant while the hero is in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel is positioned directly below the hero in the main results section, aligned to the left side of the central content container. It forms the upper portion of the left column of the page, occupying roughly 30–35% of the content width (approximately 3–4 columns out of 12), with its right edge visually aligned to the left edge of the results list. There is a generous vertical gap (48–64px) between the bottom edge of the hero and the top edge of this panel, reinforcing the transition between the marketing-like hero and the more functional results area. The panel itself is a tall, scrollable column with a subtle card treatment: a light background or faint border, 24px internal padding, and 16–24px vertical spacing between filter groups (distance, type, international capabilities, opening hours). Its top aligns roughly with the top of the results list’s sort bar, creating a cohesive horizontal baseline across the left column. On wider screens, the filter panel is sticky: as the user scrolls the results list, the panel remains fixed relative to the viewport starting just below the primary navigation, so filters are always accessible while the list and map scroll beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the results section on desktop/tablet (fixed offset from top after scroll), falling back to normal static flow on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits at the top of the results list area within the left column of the results section, just to the right of the filter panel’s vertical boundary. It occupies a horizontal strip that spans the full width of the list (not the entire page), aligned left with the list items below and right with the map’s inner content gutter. Vertically, it sits in the first 80–96px of the results list area, forming a compact toolbar. On the left side of this strip, a small label like “Locations near [query]” or the total count can appear; on the right, the sort dropdown (e.g., “Sort by: Distance”) is placed, creating a subtle left-to-right reading line before the eye drops down into the list. The strip has 16–24px of vertical padding and is visually lighter than the hero—typically a plain background with subtle divider at its bottom to separate it from the first result card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling away with the list; optionally can become a mini sticky bar at the top of the list column on taller pages, but does not overlay the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the central portion of the page below the hero, forming the main content of the left column next to the map. It begins directly beneath the sort control and extends downward toward the footer. Horizontally, it uses roughly 40–45% of the page’s content width (about 5–6 grid columns), starting immediately to the right of the filter panel with a 24–32px gutter between panel and list to avoid crowding. Each result is presented as a card spanning the full width of this column, with 16–24px top and bottom padding and 16px vertical gaps between cards. In the initial viewport after the hero, 3–4 result cards are visible alongside a substantial vertical slice of the map to the right, enabling quick comparison. The list scrolls normally within the page; on larger screens, the map remains visible on the right as the user scrolls through the list, reinforcing the spatial link between list items and their locations. When no results are present, the list area shows a spacious empty state centered within this column, preserving the same left alignment and width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrolling content within the left results column; no independent scroll container on desktop to avoid nested scrollbars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map is the dominant visual element of the results section and is positioned to the right of the filter panel and results list, occupying roughly 55–60% of the content width on desktop. Its left edge aligns vertically with the right edge of the results list, maintaining a 24–32px horizontal gap. The map begins slightly higher than the top of the results list—ideally aligning with the top of the filter panel—to create a strong horizontal band across the page and reinforce its importance. It extends downward toward the footer and, on taller viewports, often fills most of the visible height, giving the impression of a large, continuous canvas. The right edge of the map can align almost flush with the page’s right edge or extend slightly beyond the content container to create a subtle full-bleed effect, echoing the full-width imagery of the hero. Minimal UI elements (zoom controls, current location indicator) sit inside the map with generous margins (~16–24px from edges) to maintain the clean, modern aesthetic. On scroll, the map behaves as a sticky element: once scrolled into place beneath the hero, it remains fixed vertically while the results list on the left continues to scroll, preserving a constant visual reference to locations.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the main results area on larger screens, fixed relative to the viewport after it reaches a defined top offset; falls back to stacking beneath the list on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search feedback appears closely associated with the search form in the hero, positioned immediately below the form card’s bottom edge and aligned to the left margin of the form content. It occupies a single line or compact block of text that spans no more than 60–70% of the hero content width, ensuring it does not visually compete with the headline or button. There is a modest vertical gap of 8–12px between the bottom of the form and this feedback text, consistent with the mockup’s subtle secondary messaging spacing. The text is smaller and lighter in weight than the primary hero copy, likely in a light gray or soft white on the dark background, displaying information such as “Showing 24 locations within 25 km” or error/no-results messages. When the user scrolls past the hero into the results section, the feedback’s functional information is effectively re-expressed via the results count near the sort control, so this element remains confined to the hero zone and does not need to be sticky.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; it scrolls away with the hero and does not remain persistent once the user is in the results section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-link component is treated as a slim, floating vertical action element along the far right edge of the viewport, inspired directly by the mockup’s vertical contact toolbar. It appears as a tall, rounded-rectangle pill or a short vertical stack of icon + text, approximately 48–56px wide and 160–200px tall, positioned about midway down the viewport height. The element is anchored visually to the right side of the map and results area but floats above them, with a small offset (8–16px) from the viewport’s right edge so it never overlaps the browser chrome. Its background uses the brand’s deep blue or a slightly translucent dark overlay, with the active icon or label (e.g., a bookmark icon and “Saved”) highlighted in the brand orange. This element does not push or reshape the main content; it simply sits on top of the layout as a fixed overlay, remaining visible as the user scrolls the results and map. On hover or focus, it may expand slightly (e.g., reveal a short horizontal tooltip) into the map area without interfering with core interactions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport’s right edge at mid-height, overlaying the main content without affecting the page’s flow; remains visible while scrolling.

name: Location Detail
title: Location Detail
description: Displays full details for a specific logistics location, including address, opening hours, available services, international shipping capability, and options to copy the address or save the location.

core_features: Load and display full details for a specific location using the getLocationDetails interface, based on the locationId URL parameter.
Show the complete postal address and contact information for the selected location.
Display opening hours for weekdays, Saturday, and Sunday, highlighting weekday closing times that meet the late-closing threshold.
Indicate whether the location accepts international shipments and what services are offered (drop-off, pickup, service center, locker, partner store, etc.).
Provide a 'Copy address' action to copy the formatted address to the clipboard for reuse in other tools or forms.
Provide a 'Save this location' action, allowing the user to optionally add a label and favorite flag, using the saveLocation interface.
Offer navigation back to the location search results and forward to the Saved Locations page after saving.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View location details from search results
1. User clicks a location item in locations_search.html which navigates to location_detail.html?locationId={selectedLocationId}.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the locationId URL parameter.
3. The page calls getLocationDetails(locationId).
4. While data is loading, a loading state is shown in all detail sections.
5. Once getLocationDetails returns a Location object, all bound components (header, address, hours, services, contact, save form) render using the returned fields.
6. The user can review opening hours, services, and international shipping capability to decide whether this location is suitable.

Workflow: Copy the address for reuse
1. With location details loaded, the user clicks the 'Copy address' button in the address section.
2. The component formats a full address string from the loaded Location fields (street lines, city, state/province, postal code, country) and triggers a clipboard copy action.
3. On success, the component sets an internal success state and displays a brief confirmation message.
4. If the clipboard operation fails, the component sets an error state and shows guidance that the user may need to manually select and copy the address text.

Workflow: Save a location and manage saved locations
1. With location details loaded, the user can enter an optional label (e.g., a nickname for this location) and optionally mark the location as a favorite in the 'Save this location' section.
2. The user clicks the 'Save this location' button, which triggers validation that a Location object is present and then calls saveLocation with parameters: locationId (from the loaded Location), label (input value or empty), and is_favorite (checkbox state).
3. While saveLocation is in progress, the component shows a saving state and disables the save button to prevent duplicate submissions.
4. On success, the component receives a SavedLocation object, updates its internal state to reflect that this location is saved, and displays a success confirmation.
5. After a successful save, the component reveals or enables a 'Manage saved locations' link that navigates to saved_locations.html (without additional URL parameters, unless provided by higher-level navigation rules).
6. If saveLocation returns an error, the component displays an error message and re-enables the save button so the user can retry.

Workflow: Return to search results
1. The user wants to return to the previous list of locations.
2. The user clicks the 'Back to search results' link in the header/navigation area.
3. The page navigates to locations_search.html, without modifying or relying on local storage.
4. Any search context restoration is handled by locations_search.html, not by this page.
interactions: On page load, read the locationId URL parameter and, if present, call getLocationDetails(locationId) to retrieve the Location data.
If no locationId parameter is present, display an error state indicating that no location could be loaded and disable actions that depend on location data.
When getLocationDetails succeeds, propagate the Location object to all components that display name, address, hours, services, international capability, and contact info.
When getLocationDetails fails (e.g., invalid or unknown locationId), show an error message and disable the 'Save this location' and 'Copy address' actions.
When the user clicks 'Copy address', construct a formatted address string from the Location fields and attempt to copy it to the clipboard, updating the component state to reflect success or failure.
When the user edits the label field in the save-location form, update the component's local form state without calling any interface until submission.
When the user toggles the favorite checkbox in the save-location form, update the form state for the is_favorite parameter.
When the user clicks 'Save this location', validate that location data is loaded, then call saveLocation with the current form state; handle loading, success, and error sub-states.
After a successful saveLocation call, enable or display a 'Manage saved locations' link that navigates to saved_locations.html.
When the user clicks 'Back to search results', navigate to locations_search.html.
When the user clicks 'Manage saved locations', navigate to saved_locations.html.

In the opening hours component, interpret missing or empty open/close fields as closed for that day, and visually highlight weekday closing times that meet or exceed a predefined late-closing threshold.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state driver is the locationId URL parameter. On initialization of location_detail.html, the page reads locationId from the query string. If locationId is provided, the page calls getLocationDetails(locationId) and stores the returned Location object in page-level state that is shared with all components. If locationId is missing or empty, the page sets an error state that prevents dependent actions (copy address, save location) and informs the user that no location was specified. If locationId is changed by navigation (e.g., the user follows another link that leads to location_detail.html with a different locationId), the page detects the change, resets its loading and error states, and calls getLocationDetails again for the new ID.
The save-location component maintains its own transient form state (label text and is_favorite checkbox) and a submission state (idle, saving, success, error). On submit, it calls saveLocation using the current Location.id and form values. A successful response with a SavedLocation object is stored in the component's internal state so that the UI can indicate that the location is already saved and show the 'Manage saved locations' link. This state is not persisted in URL parameters or localStorage; if the user reloads the page, the component starts in an unsaved visual state unless the application passes additional context via the URL (which is beyond this page's responsibility).
Opening-hours state is derived from the Location's weekday_open_time, weekday_close_time, saturday_open_time, saturday_close_time, sunday_open_time, and sunday_close_time fields. A threshold for 'late closing' (for example, a specific time such as 20:00) is used purely as application logic to determine whether to highlight weekday closing times. Missing values are treated as closed. All logic relies directly on the dynamic Location data returned by getLocationDetails and does not use any hardcoded sample content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom structure as the mockup: a thin utility bar at the very top, a primary navigation header below it, and then a dominant hero section that introduces the specific location. The hero remains full‑width with a background image (satellite/earth or map‑like imagery) and layered foreground content. In the hero, the core location information (location name, country/region, key status badges, primary CTA) forms a left‑aligned vertical content block inside the central 12‑column container. The right side of the hero is reserved for more visual, contextual content (e.g., a stylized map or global network visualization tied to this specific location). A floating vertical contact/action toolbar sits on the far right edge of the viewport, aligned with the hero’s vertical center and not displacing the main content. Below the hero, the location’s detailed information flows in clearly separated sections (address & basics, hours, services, international capability, additional actions) in a 12‑column content container, maintaining a left‑dominant content block with optional secondary right column panels where needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom structure as the mockup: a thin utility bar at the very top, a primary navigation header below it, and then a dominant hero section that introduces the specific location. The hero remains full‑width with a background image (satellite/earth or map‑like imagery) and layered foreground content. In the hero, the core location information (location name, country/region, key status badges, primary CTA) forms a left‑aligned vertical content block inside the central 12‑column container. The right side of the hero is reserved for more visual, contextual content (e.g., a stylized map or global network visualization tied to this specific location). A floating vertical contact/action toolbar sits on the far right edge of the viewport, aligned with the hero’s vertical center and not displacing the main content. Below the hero, the location’s detailed information flows in clearly separated sections (address & basics, hours, services, international capability, additional actions) in a 12‑column content container, maintaining a left‑dominant content block with optional secondary right column panels where needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The utility bar occupies a shallow strip across the full width at the top (~40px height). The primary navigation bar directly under it is moderately tall (~70–80px), giving generous vertical spacing around logo and menu items. The hero section takes up roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height, making the location headline and imagery the dominant focal point. Within the hero’s central content container (max width ~1140px centered), the left text block uses about 5–6 of the 12 columns (around 45–50% of the container width), while the right visual area (map/imagery) uses the remaining 6–7 columns, visually more open and less text‑heavy. The floating right toolbar occupies a narrow vertical strip on the far right (about 5–7% of the viewport width) with its own internal spacing. Below the hero, the main details area uses the same centered content width, often in a 2‑column layout: a primary content column on the left (~2/3 width) for rich text details and data (address, hours, services list), and a secondary column on the right (~1/3 width) for compact action blocks (copy address, save location, quick contact). Vertical spacing between sections follows the 48–64px rhythm, ensuring sections feel distinct but related.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a marketing‑style, spacious density. The hero uses a large, bold location title with a generous line height and ample space around it. The distance between the headline, any subheading (e.g., city, country, hub type), and the primary CTA button follows 16–32px increments, maintaining a visually calm layout. Below the hero, the information is more structured but still not visually dense: address blocks, hours, and service lists are grouped with clear headings, card‑like surfaces, and comfortable padding (16–24px inside each block). Icons, badges, and action buttons maintain large click/ tap targets and are not crowded—expect 16–24px gaps between distinct controls. Even though this is more informational than the marketing homepage, dense tables are avoided; information is chunked into cards and clearly separated lines to preserve a modern, premium brand feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z‑pattern from the mockup. The user’s eye starts at the top left with the logo, moves right across the primary navigation items, and may catch a subtle accent CTA at the top right (e.g., 'Track shipment'). From there, the eye moves diagonally down to the left side of the hero where a large, high‑contrast location name and key attributes are presented. Immediately below that, an orange primary CTA button (e.g., 'Book a shipment' or 'Get a quote') serves as the second strong focal point. The eye then moves horizontally toward the right side of the hero, where a stylized global/map image and possibly a subtle location pin draw attention and reinforce the global logistics context. The floating vertical toolbar on the right edge (with icons for contact, chat, or emergency support) becomes a tertiary focal element guiding attention downwards, hinting at interactive options. As the user scrolls, the flow becomes more linear: left‑aligned section headings lead into supporting content on the left, while right‑aligned secondary cards and actions are discovered slightly later in the scan, maintaining the left‑to‑right reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The Location Detail page appears as a refined, corporate logistics interface that inherits the home hero’s visual architecture but focuses on a single location. At the very top, a slim, full‑width utility bar in deep blue spans the page, carrying compact items like language, region selection, and a small phone or support icon, all with restrained 8–16px horizontal spacing. Directly beneath, a taller navigation bar sits against either a solid deep blue or subtly transparent background over the hero, containing the logo on the far left, a centered or left‑aligned primary navigation menu with evenly spaced items, and a right‑aligned minor CTA or profile area; the nav height and padding give it a calm, premium presence. Below this, the hero occupies roughly three quarters of the initial viewport, full‑bleed across the screen. A darkened global satellite or stylized map background fills the hero, with a subtle gradient overlay making white text pop. Centered in the page, a max‑width content container adheres to a 12‑column grid. On the left 5–6 columns, a vertical stack presents the location name in large white type, followed by the city and country, a row of compact orange or outlined badges showing attributes like 'International Hub' or '24/7 Operations', and then one or two CTAs stacked or aligned horizontally, led by a bold orange button. On the right 6–7 columns, a lighter‑content area shows a dynamic map or network of lines converging on this location, with ample negative space, maintaining the airy, tech‑forward feel. Along the far right edge of the viewport, floating above the hero and anchored mid‑height, a narrow vertical toolbar hosts 3–4 square or rounded‑corner icons (contact, chat, help, maybe 'Find nearby'), spaced with 8–12px internal padding and 12–16px between icons. As the user scrolls beyond the hero, the main content area emerges as a stack of clean sections within the same centered container: first an 'At a glance' band with the address, opening hours today, and a compact status indicator in a 2‑column layout (details on the left, a slim action card on the right with 'Copy address' and 'Save location'); then a services section with a bold heading left and a responsive grid of service tags or cards below; and further down, an international shipping capability block and any supporting information. Each section is separated vertically by 48–64px, with clear headings and card‑like groupings, creating a rhythm where each cluster is easy to scan yet visually connected to the brand. The overall impression is a focused, location‑specific experience that retains the original hero’s drama while presenting operational details in a calm, structured, and clearly prioritized layout.

name: My Account Overview
title: My Account Overview
description: Central page that summarizes the user’s shipments, drafts, saved customs estimates, address book usage, and saved locations, and provides fast navigation to related tools and details.

core_features: Fetch account summary data using getAccountOverview and initialize all overview sections (recent shipments, draft shipments, saved customs estimates, saved locations, address book summary).
Display recent_shipments with key shipment information and direct tracking access by navigating to track.html with the shipment’s tracking_number as a URL parameter.
Display draft_shipments with a way to resume shipment creation by navigating to shipment_details.html with the draft Shipment.id as a URL parameter.
Highlight shipments that are awaiting payment (status = pending_payment) within recent_shipments and provide direct access to payment.html with the Shipment.id as a URL parameter.
List saved_customs_estimates and allow users to reopen a selected estimate by navigating to customs_estimator.html with the CustomsEstimate.id as a URL parameter.
Summarize the user’s address book using address_book_count and provide a link to manage full details in address_book.html.
Summarize saved_locations (using SavedLocation + Location data) and provide a link to manage them in saved_locations.html.
Provide a quick tools area with shortcuts to the Customs & Duties Estimator and the Ship & Get Rates flow (using standard application routing with URL parameters where needed).
Use the optional section URL parameter to focus the page on a specific section (e.g., drafts, estimates) by controlling which section is emphasized and scrolled into view.
user_workflows:
View and track a recent shipment:
1. User navigates to account_overview.html (optionally with no section parameter).
2. Page calls getAccountOverview and receives recent_shipments.
3. Recent shipments section renders a list of Shipment items from recent_shipments.
4. User clicks a shipment’s tracking number in the list.
5. Browser navigates to track.html?tracking_number={Shipment.tracking_number}.
6. User views detailed tracking on the tracking page.

Resume a draft shipment:
1. User opens account_overview.html or is redirected there from another page.
2. Page calls getAccountOverview and receives draft_shipments.
3. Draft shipments section lists all Shipment items from draft_shipments with status = draft.
4. User clicks the "Continue" action on a selected draft shipment card.
5. Browser navigates to shipment_details.html?shipmentId={Shipment.id}.
6. The shipment details page uses shipmentId to restore the draft shipment flow.

Pay for a shipment awaiting payment:
1. User opens account_overview.html.
2. Page calls getAccountOverview and receives recent_shipments.
3. Recent shipments section highlights shipments where status = pending_payment and displays a "Pay now" action.
4. User clicks "Pay now" on a pending payment shipment.
5. Browser navigates to payment.html?shipmentId={Shipment.id}.
6. Payment page loads the shipment by shipmentId and processes payment.

Reopen a saved customs & duties estimate:
1. User opens account_overview.html (optionally with section=estimates in the URL).
2. Page calls getAccountOverview and receives saved_customs_estimates.
3. Saved customs estimates section displays each CustomsEstimate entry.
4. User clicks a specific estimate.
5. Browser navigates to customs_estimator.html?customsEstimateId={CustomsEstimate.id}.
6. Customs estimator page reloads the estimate using customsEstimateId so the user can review or adjust it.

Manage address book entries:
1. User opens account_overview.html.
2. Page calls getAccountOverview and reads address_book_count.
3. Address summary component displays the count and a "Manage addresses" link.
4. User clicks "Manage addresses".
5. Browser navigates to address_book.html.
6. User manages stored addresses on the address book page.

Manage saved locations:
1. User opens account_overview.html.
2. Page calls getAccountOverview and receives saved_locations entries, each containing SavedLocation and Location data.
3. Saved locations summary displays a count and a subset or summary of saved locations.
4. User clicks "Manage saved locations".
5. Browser navigates to saved_locations.html.
6. User edits, adds, or removes saved locations there.

Use quick tools from the overview:
1. User opens account_overview.html.
2. Page calls getAccountOverview and renders a quick tools panel.
3. User clicks the shortcut to the Customs & Duties Estimator tool.
4. Browser navigates to customs_estimator.html (optionally with parameters defined by that tool, not by this page).
5. Alternatively, user clicks the shortcut to the Ship & Get Rates flow.
6. Browser navigates to the configured Ship & Get Rates URL (e.g., a shipment creation or rate search page) where further inputs are collected.

Deep-linking into a specific section using URL parameter:
1. Another page links to account_overview.html?section=drafts.
2. User lands on account_overview.html with section=drafts in the URL.
3. On initialization, the page reads the section URL parameter.
4. Section handling logic sets the drafts section as active, expands it (if sections are collapsible), and scrolls it into view.
5. User interacts with the drafts section (e.g., clicking a draft’s "Continue" action).
6. If the user later clicks to another section from the overview’s internal navigation, the page updates the section parameter in the URL to match the selected section.
interactions: On page load, call getAccountOverview with no parameters and bind the response data to the overview components.
When the URL parameter section changes (via direct navigation or internal navigation), update the active section state and scroll to the corresponding section container.
Clicking a tracking number in the recent shipments list navigates to track.html?tracking_number={Shipment.tracking_number}.
Clicking "Pay now" on a recent shipment with status = pending_payment navigates to payment.html?shipmentId={Shipment.id}.
Clicking "Continue" on a draft shipment card navigates to shipment_details.html?shipmentId={Shipment.id}.
Clicking on a saved customs estimate entry navigates to customs_estimator.html?customsEstimateId={CustomsEstimate.id}.
Clicking the "Manage addresses" link in the address summary navigates to address_book.html.
Clicking the "Manage saved locations" link in the saved locations summary navigates to saved_locations.html.
Clicking the customs estimator shortcut button in the quick tools panel navigates to customs_estimator.html (without a customsEstimateId parameter, starting a new estimate).

Clicking the Ship & Get Rates shortcut button in the quick tools panel navigates to the pre-configured Ship & Get Rates page URL (for example a shipment creation or quote page) using standard URL navigation.

If recent_shipments, draft_shipments, saved_customs_estimates, or saved_locations arrays are empty, the respective components display an empty-state message or call-to-action appropriate to that section (text and localization are handled outside this functional spec).

Internal section navigation (e.g., clicking a tab or sidebar link for drafts/estimates/shipments) updates the browser URL to account_overview.html?section={targetSection} and updates the active section state without requiring a new getAccountOverview call in a single-page context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the optional `section` URL parameter. On initial load, the page reads `section` from the query string (e.g., account_overview.html?section=drafts). If `section` matches one of the known section identifiers (such as `recent_shipments`, `drafts`, `estimates`, `saved_locations`, `addresses`), the corresponding section is treated as active: it is scrolled into view and, if sections are collapsible or tabbed, its panel is opened and highlighted. If `section` is missing or does not match a known value, a default section (for example, recent shipments) is set as active. When the user interacts with internal navigation (e.g., clicking a section link), the page updates the `section` query parameter (e.g., account_overview.html?section=estimates) via standard navigation or history API, and the section handling logic re-runs to focus the appropriate section. The data from `getAccountOverview` is considered valid for the entire lifespan of the page view; it is fetched once per full page load and is not stored in localStorage. Any subsequent refresh of the page or direct navigation to a different `section` value causes a new HTTP request to `getAccountOverview` and full re-render. Other UI states such as loading, error, or empty-section indicators are purely in-memory (component-level) states derived from the `getAccountOverview` Promise lifecycle and the lengths of the returned arrays.

components.functionality: Top-level container for the My Account Overview page that orchestrates data loading, interprets the `section` URL parameter, and composes all other functional components.

Invokes getAccountOverview on page initialization, manages loading and error states, and exposes the response object (recent_shipments, draft_shipments, saved_customs_estimates, saved_locations, address_book_count) to child components.
Reads and writes the `section` URL parameter and maps it to an internal activeSection state used by all section components to determine visibility, emphasis, and scroll behavior.

Provides internal navigation (e.g., tabs or sidebar links) for jumping between sections such as recent shipments, drafts, estimates, saved locations, and addresses. Updates the `section` URL parameter and notifies section-state-manager.

Displays recent shipments from recent_shipments, including key details (such as status, route, dates) and actions to track shipments or complete pending payments. Integrates with tracking and payment pages via URL navigation.
Displays draft shipments from draft_shipments and provides a "Continue" action on each draft shipment to resume its setup via shipment_details.html with the Shipment.id in the URL.

Lists saved customs and duties estimates from saved_customs_estimates and allows the user to reopen a specific estimate in the Customs Estimator tool using customs_estimator.html with customsEstimateId as a URL parameter.
Shows aggregate information about the user’s address book, using address_book_count from getAccountOverview, and provides a "Manage addresses" button that navigates to address_book.html.

Summarizes the user’s saved locations using the saved_locations array (SavedLocation + related Location) to show count and selected details, and offers a "Manage saved locations" link to saved_locations.html.

Provides quick access buttons to frequently used tools related to the account, including a shortcut to the Customs & Duties Estimator (customs_estimator.html) and a shortcut to the Ship & Get Rates page (URL defined in application routing).
Wraps the recent-shipments-list with a section anchor identifier (e.g., `recent_shipments`) so that section-state-manager can scroll to and highlight it when `section=recent_shipments`.
Wraps the draft-shipments-list with a section anchor identifier (e.g., `drafts`) so that section-state-manager can scroll to and highlight it when `section=drafts`.
Wraps the saved-customs-estimates-list with a section anchor identifier (e.g., `estimates`) so that section-state-manager can scroll to and highlight it when `section=estimates`.
Wraps the saved-locations-summary with a section anchor identifier (e.g., `saved_locations`) so that section-state-manager can focus it when `section=saved_locations`.
Wraps the address-book-summary with a section anchor identifier (e.g., `addresses`) so that section-state-manager can focus it when `section=addresses`.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, hero-first structure as the marketing mockup: global header (outside this scope) at the very top, then a full-width account hero band within the account-overview-page, and beneath it a structured content area with a left-dominant column of detailed lists and a right supporting column of summaries. A floating quick-tools panel on the far right mirrors the vertical contact toolbar from the mockup. Section navigation sits just below the hero content, aligned with the main content column, acting as a secondary navigation rail for jumping between account sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, hero-first structure as the marketing mockup: global header (outside this scope) at the very top, then a full-width account hero band within the account-overview-page, and beneath it a structured content area with a left-dominant column of detailed lists and a right supporting column of summaries. A floating quick-tools panel on the far right mirrors the vertical contact toolbar from the mockup. Section navigation sits just below the hero content, aligned with the main content column, acting as a secondary navigation rail for jumping between account sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The account hero within the page-container occupies roughly 60–70% of the initial viewport height, with a full-bleed satellite/earth themed background and a left-aligned text block constrained to a central 1140px-wide content container. The left content column below the hero (recent shipments, drafts, estimates) takes about 60–65% of the content width, while the right column (address book summary, saved locations summary) uses 35–40%. The floating quick-tools panel consumes a narrow 5–7% strip of viewport width on the far right and does not push other content. Vertical gaps between major sections use 32–64px increments, giving generous breathing room similar to the marketing hero layout.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density stays marketing-like and spacious: a large account overview headline, wide spacing between heading, subtext, and calls to action in the hero; lists show only the most recent or most relevant 3–5 items with card-like rows, not dense tables; summary cards on the right emphasize high-level stats and shortcuts instead of detailed data. Interactive targets (rows, cards, quick tools) have generous padding (12–16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal) to maintain a clean, touch-friendly corporate feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z pattern from the mockup: starting at the top left of the hero with the page title and account greeting, moving horizontally to the right toward the upper-right area where secondary actions and the floating quick-tools panel live, then diagonally down to the left where the 'Recent Shipments' section heading anchors the main content. From there, the eye travels across to the right-hand summary cards, then vertically down the left column through additional sections (Drafts, Estimates), with the section-navigation providing quick jumps. The persistent vertical quick-tools panel on the right acts as a recurring visual anchor that encourages action without overwhelming the primary left-column content.

layout.overall_description: Within the global site frame, the My Account Overview page opens with a tall, full-width hero band that visually echoes the marketing homepage: a deep blue-tinted satellite or earth background spans edge-to-edge, while a centered content container (around 1140px wide) holds a left-aligned block with the account title, a short welcome line, and a concise summary of shipment activity plus a primary orange action button. This hero occupies around two-thirds of the initial viewport height, so it feels like a powerful account snapshot rather than a cramped dashboard. Overlaid near the right edge of the viewport, a slim vertical quick-tools panel floats independently of the hero, offering icon-based shortcuts and aligning visually with the rightmost edge of the hero imagery. Just below the hero content, the section navigation appears anchored to the top of the main content area, aligned with the left side of the central container. As the user scrolls, the content resolves into a two-column layout: a wider left column for detailed, scrollable lists (recent shipments, drafts, saved estimates) stacked vertically, and a narrower right column with card-style summaries for the address book and saved locations. Generous whitespace separates these blocks, and each section is clearly delineated with a heading and card-like container while still feeling part of a single, continuous page. Throughout, the eye is led from the strong hero heading down into the recent shipments list, then across to the right-hand summaries and down again, while the fixed quick-tools panel on the right remains a constant, subtle invitation for quick actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-overview-page component defines the main content region below the global site header. It stretches full-width across the viewport but constrains its primary content to a centered max-width container approximately 1140px wide, aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. At the top of this page container is a full-width hero band styled as an account snapshot: it occupies roughly 60–70% of the initial viewport height, with a satellite/earth themed background image tinted in deep blue and a subtle dark overlay for contrast. Inside the hero, a left-aligned content block uses about 40–45% of the central container width, starting around 15–20% from the top of the hero. This block contains the My Account Overview title, a brief description, and key account stats stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps, plus a prominent orange CTA button aligned under the text with about 24px spacing. The remaining central container width to the right is visually lighter, allowing the background imagery and any subtle network graphics to show, supporting the global logistics theme. Beneath the hero, the page container transitions into the main content area, where the section-navigation aligns with the left edge of the central container, followed by the left/right column structure for the section containers. Overall vertical padding at the top and bottom of the page uses 48–64px multiples to maintain the spacious corporate marketing feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, acting as the root layout frame for all account content. It supports a full-bleed background in the hero region while constraining interactive content to a centered fixed-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-data-loader is an invisible structural layer that covers the entire interactive content area when data is being fetched. Visually, its main expression is a centered loading indicator that appears both within the hero and main content region: when active, a semi-transparent dark-blue overlay covers the hero and main content container, dimming underlying sections (including lists and summary cards) while leaving the global navigation and floating quick-tools panel subtly visible in the background. The loading spinner or message is centered horizontally within the 1140px content container and placed roughly at the vertical center of the viewport, so it appears over the middle of the hero or the upper main content area depending on scroll position. This preserves the perception of a single, unified page while clearly indicating that data for all sections (recent shipments, drafts, estimates, address summaries) is loading simultaneously.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-page overlay positioned above the account-overview-page content but below any global header; it uses fixed positioning while active so that the dimming effect and loader stay centered relative to the viewport as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-state-manager is non-visual and resides logically alongside the section-navigation and section-container components. It tracks which section is active or in focus and synchronizes that state with scroll position and interactions with the navigation. Spatially, it does not take up visible space, but conceptually it anchors to the top of the main content region just below the hero so that section-navigation highlighting and smooth scrolling between section-container anchors feel cohesive and responsive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-rendered in the visual layer; it only influences the behavior of scroll and navigation states without affecting layout dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation appears immediately below the hero's main content area, aligned to the left edge of the 1140px central container. It spans roughly 60–70% of that container width, presenting a horizontal row of large, well-spaced pill-like navigation items labeled for each major section (Recent Shipments, Drafts, Estimates, Addresses, Saved Locations). The nav sits on a subtle card-like strip with a light background and a soft shadow, about 56–64px high, giving it clear separation from both the hero above and the section content below. There is about 32px of vertical space between the bottom of the hero content block and the top of this navigation strip. Each nav item has around 24–32px horizontal padding and is separated from neighbors by 16–24px gaps, echoing the spacious marketing navigation. On wider screens, the navigation aligns left, leaving breathing room to the right where the floating quick-tools panel is visible. As the user scrolls down past the hero, this navigation remains pinned just below the global header, forming a secondary sticky bar that keeps section access always within view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned in the normal flow directly beneath the hero, then becomes sticky at the top of the main content region (just beneath the global header) when the page is scrolled, maintaining its horizontal position within the centered content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-shipments-list lives inside section-container-recent and is the first major content block in the main area. It occupies the left column of the grid, approximately 60–65% of the 1140px container width, starting about 24–32px below the section-navigation. A section heading 'Recent Shipments' sits at the top of this left column with generous top padding (24px within its container) and a strong typographic hierarchy similar to the hero subheading. Below the heading, a short descriptive line or filter summary sits with a 8–16px gap, followed by the list itself. Each shipment in the list is rendered as a horizontally stretched card row, full width within the left column, with 16px vertical and 24px horizontal padding and 8–16px gaps between rows. The list shows a manageable number of items (e.g., 3–5) by default to preserve the clean, marketing-inspired feel; if more items exist, a link or button to 'View all shipments' appears aligned to the bottom right of the list block with about 16px top margin. Overall vertical space from the last list row to the next section (Drafts) is around 32–40px, keeping the recent-shipments-list visually dominant as the primary data focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the main content grid, scrolling naturally with the page. Its height grows with content but is capped with internal scroll or pagination if the list becomes overly long, to preserve the spacious layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The draft-shipments-list is placed within section-container-drafts directly beneath the recent shipments section in the same left column, maintaining alignment with the grid. It starts after a vertical gap of about 40–48px from the end of the recent-shipments-list to clearly delineate the section. The section heading 'Draft Shipments' is left-aligned, matching the style and size of the 'Recent Shipments' heading, with 24px top padding inside its container. Below it, the list displays draft items in a similar card-row format but may use slightly lighter emphasis (e.g., less saturated accents) to indicate that these are pending rather than active. Each row uses the same spacing and card dimensions for consistency: approximately 16px vertical padding, 24px horizontal padding, and 8–16px vertical spacing between rows. A subtle divider or muted subheading at the bottom, such as 'View all drafts', aligns to the right edge of the left column with about 16px margin above. The drafts list visually reads as the second-tier area after recent shipments but still occupies the full width of the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column under the recent shipments section, scrolling in sequence. It extends vertically as needed, but is expected to show a limited number of rows before offering navigation to a dedicated drafts page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-customs-estimates-list is contained within section-container-estimates and sits below the draft-shipments-list, continuing vertically down the same left column. It begins after a 40–48px vertical gap, again with a left-aligned section heading 'Saved Customs Estimates' and a short descriptive line indicating what the user will find here. The list is formatted in a card-row style similar to shipments, but with fields and icons tailored to estimates (e.g., origin, destination, duty amounts). Each row spans the full width of the left column, with consistent padding (around 16px vertical and 24px horizontal) and generous spacing between rows (8–16px). To preserve the marketing-style spaciousness, only a limited set of estimates is visible by default; a link or button to 'View all estimates' sits aligned to the right under the last row with a 16px top margin. The overall block height is slightly shorter or visually lighter than the recent-shipments-list, signaling its supporting role in the overview.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the left column, positioned beneath the drafts section. It scrolls with the rest of the page and may use lazy-loading or pagination if needed to prevent the list from becoming overly long.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The address-book-summary appears in the right column within section-container-addresses, aligned to the top of the main content area so that its vertical position roughly lines up with the top of the 'Recent Shipments' heading to the left. The right column occupies around 35–40% of the 1140px width, starting with a slight gap (24–32px) from the left column to maintain separation. The address-book-summary itself is a compact card with a light surface, rounded corners, and a subtle shadow. It includes a small section title 'Address Book' aligned left at the top, followed by key stats such as 'Total saved addresses' and 'Most used address' arranged in one or two columns inside the card. Internal padding is generous, around 24px on all sides, and vertical spacing between text elements is 8–16px. A primary or secondary action (e.g., 'Manage addresses') is placed at the bottom right inside the card. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of space between this card and the one below it (saved locations), creating a neat stacked composition on the right side that balances the taller lists on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, aligning its top edge approximately with the first major left-column section header. It scrolls naturally with the page and resizes fluidly within the right column at different breakpoints, eventually stacking under the left column on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-locations-summary resides directly beneath the address-book-summary within section-container-saved-locations, following the same right-column alignment and card style. It is a slightly shorter or equivalent-height card that focuses on saved pickup and drop-off locations. The card width matches the address-book-summary, and it uses the same padding (around 24px) and vertical spacing to maintain visual consistency. A section title 'Saved Locations' appears at the top left inside the card, with key metrics such as number of saved locations, recently used location, or regional distribution displayed underneath in a compact layout. A link or button like 'View locations' appears at the bottom right inside the card with about 16px margin above. A vertical gap of 24–32px separates this card from any additional right-column content below, keeping the right column visually balanced against the taller, data-rich left column sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, stacked directly under the address-book-summary. On narrower screens, it follows address-book-summary in the vertical flow when the layout collapses to a single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-tools-panel is a vertical, floating toolbar positioned along the far right edge of the viewport, visually echoing the contact toolbar from the marketing mockup. It occupies a narrow strip approximately 5–7% of the viewport width or a fixed width around 56–64px, and is centered vertically within the viewport so that its midpoint aligns roughly with the mid-height of the hero area on initial load. The panel consists of stacked square or rounded-rectangle icon buttons (e.g., 'Create shipment', 'Track shipment', 'Support'), each with internal padding of 8–12px and 8–12px spacing between them. The toolbar background is a solid or translucent deep blue, helping it stand out against the hero imagery and the white main content sections. There is a slight offset (8–16px) from the right edge of the main 1140px content container to ensure it floats visually above content rather than feeling attached to the grid. The toolbar casts a subtle shadow to reinforce its floating nature. It does not push any content; instead, it overlays the page, maintaining consistent position as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the right side of the viewport, independent of page scroll. It remains visible as the user scrolls through hero and content sections, adjusting its vertical position responsively on smaller screens or hiding when space is insufficient.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-container-recent wraps the recent-shipments-list and its heading, defining it as the first major section in the main content area. It starts directly below the section-navigation with around 24–32px vertical spacing. In terms of width, it spans the left column of the grid (around 60–65% of the 1140px container) and extends from the left content margin to the column gutter between left and right columns. The container appears as a soft card-like block or simply a clearly demarcated section with a subtle bottom border and ample internal padding at the top (24px) and bottom (24–32px). Inside, the section heading, optional description, and recent-shipments-list are stacked vertically. The right column content begins aligned to this container's top edge but is not part of this container, reinforcing the notion of distinct columns. Vertically, the bottom of this section is separated from the next container (Drafts) by 40–48px of whitespace, making it visually clear that this is the primary section of the overview.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, serving as an anchor for the 'Recent' item in section-navigation. It may expose an anchor ID for smooth scrolling and highlight synchronization managed by the section-state-manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-container-drafts encloses the draft-shipments-list and its heading, positioned directly under the recent section within the same left column. It begins after a 40–48px vertical gap from section-container-recent, providing a clear visual break. The container width matches the recent section, maintaining alignment with the left content margin and the column gutter. Like the recent section, it may use a light card background or a defined top heading with a subtle bottom divider, but with slightly less visual weight (e.g., lighter shadow or border) to reflect its secondary importance. Internal top and bottom padding are around 24px, and the list rows sit comfortably inside with 16–24px vertical spacing around them. This section's bottom edge is separated from the estimates section by another 40–48px, preserving the stacked, spacious layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column and acts as the scroll anchor for the 'Drafts' item in section-navigation. It participates in smooth scroll behavior and active state highlighting via the section-state-manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-container-estimates wraps the saved-customs-estimates-list, continuing the vertical stack in the left column below the drafts section. It begins after a generous 40–48px vertical spacing from section-container-drafts, retaining the rhythm of clearly separated sections. It has the same width as the other left-column containers and aligns perfectly with them on the grid. Top padding of approximately 24px allows the 'Saved Customs Estimates' heading to sit comfortably, followed by the list and any supporting actions. The bottom of the container has 24–32px padding and is separated from any following content by another 40–48px margin. Its card or section styling is consistent with the drafts container, making it visually clear they are sibling sections under the same navigation structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the left column and acts as an anchor for the 'Estimates' item in section-navigation. Its position informs scroll-based highlighting through the section-state-manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-container-saved-locations envelops the saved-locations-summary card in the right column. It is aligned with the right column’s grid and stacked under the section-container-addresses. The top of this container starts about 24–32px beneath the bottom of the address-book-summary card, keeping a clean vertical rhythm in the right column. Its width matches that of the address container, and it adheres to the same right margin and internal padding conventions. While visually it appears as a single card, the container conceptually defines the saved locations section as a destination for the 'Saved Locations' navigation item. Its height is determined by the content of the summary card, typically shorter than the left-column sections to maintain a lighter, complementary presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column. It can act as an anchor for the 'Saved Locations' section-navigation item, potentially scrolling the page so this card aligns near the top of the viewport when selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-container-addresses wraps the address-book-summary in the right column and is aligned so that its top roughly matches the vertical start of section-container-recent on the left. It sits flush to the right column boundaries, with consistent side padding and a 24–32px top padding that frames the address-book-summary card or heading. This container establishes the addresses section as the right-column counterpart to the main left-column sections, emphasizing its importance as a frequent destination. There is 24–32px of vertical spacing between this container and section-container-saved-locations below it, preserving a clean stacked composition on the right side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column and acts as an anchor target for the 'Addresses' item in section-navigation. It scrolls in sync with the main content while maintaining its column alignment at various responsive breakpoints.

name: Address Book
title: Address Book
description: Manage saved sender and recipient addresses, including creating, editing, deleting, and starting shipments from selected addresses.

core_features: Load and display saved addresses using getSavedAddresses, optionally filtered by address_type from the URL.
Filter visible addresses by type (personal, business, recipient, pickup, other) and reflect the selected filter in the URL address_type parameter.
Create new address entries with createOrUpdateAddress, including all relevant contact and address fields, default sender/recipient flags, and saved_in_address_book set for this page.
Edit existing address entries by loading their current data into a form and saving changes via createOrUpdateAddress.
Delete an address from the address book using deleteAddress and refresh the list with the current filter.
Trigger shipment creation from a selected address by navigating to ship_rates.html with mode=create_shipment and origin_address_id for sender/business addresses via "Ship from this address".
