interactions: On page load, the help-content-loader component triggers a call to getInformationalPageContent with a Help & Support–specific page_slug, and the help-topic-router component reads the topic URL parameter to determine which topic tab is initially active.

When a user clicks a topic tab (e.g., FAQs, Guides, Contact), the help-topic-tabs component updates the active topic state and notifies the help-topic-router to update the URL topic parameter (via pushState/replaceState) so the URL reflects the selected topic without reloading the page.

When the topic URL parameter changes (either via direct navigation or via tab clicks), the help-topic-router component broadcasts the current topic to other components so that the appropriate section is shown (e.g., focusing on the contact form when topic=contact).

As the user types into the FAQ search field, the faq-search-form component updates an internal search query state and, on submit or debounced input change, filters the in-memory FAQ items from the loaded InformationalPageContent. The faq-list component re-renders to show only matching FAQs or a no-results message when there are no matches.
When the user clicks a FAQ item header, the faq-list component toggles an expanded/collapsed state for that item and shows or hides its answer content accordingly, without additional API calls.

When the user clicks inline self-service links within any help content (FAQs or guides), the quick-links-panel or inline content triggers navigation to:
  - track.html (no additional parameters) for tracking shipments.
  - ship_rates.html with the URL parameter mode=create_shipment for creating a shipment.
  - customs_estimator.html for estimating duties and taxes.
Navigation is performed using standard link navigation so that these pages can read their own parameters.

When the user clicks on a phone or email contact method displayed within the contact-options-block (values provided via InformationalPageContent), the browser handles the appropriate action (e.g., tel: or mailto: link) without invoking additional interfaces.

When the user submits the contact-support-form, the form validates required fields (name, email, topic, message). If valid, it calls submitSupportRequest with the entered values, disables the submit button while waiting, and handles the returned success flag and reference_id to show confirmation or error messages in the support-request-confirmation component.

If getInformationalPageContent fails or returns no sections, the help-content-loader component shows an error state and a retry action. Clicking the retry action re-invokes getInformationalPageContent without altering URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary cross-page state for this Help & Support page is managed through the URL query parameter topic. On every page load, the help-topic-router component parses the topic parameter from the current URL (e.g., help.html?topic=contact). If topic is not provided, a default topic (such as the general FAQ section) is assumed. If topic is provided and matches a known section key (for example, a key representing the contact section), that section/tab becomes active, and the page may scroll to the corresponding anchor (e.g., contact form) after content has loaded. The selected topic is propagated to the help-topic-tabs and contact-support-form components so they can render the appropriate tab as active and pre-select an appropriate topic value in the form's topic field. When a user manually switches topic via the tabs, the help-topic-router updates the topic parameter in the URL (e.g., help.html?topic=guides or help.html?topic=contact) using history APIs without triggering a full reload; the new topic value then becomes the single source of truth for the active section and can be bookmarked or shared. The help-content-loader itself does not depend on the topic parameter; it always fetches the same Help & Support content via getInformationalPageContent based on the page slug, and the topic parameter only controls which subset of that content is visible and focused. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used for state; all shareable state is reflected in the URL (via topic), while transient UI states such as expanded FAQ items, search query contents, and form input values are kept in in-memory component state only.

components.functionality: Fetches the structured Help & Support informational content from the backend using getInformationalPageContent when the page loads or when the user explicitly retries after an error. It normalizes the returned sections into internal structures for FAQs, how-to guides, and contact information that other components can consume.

Reads the topic query parameter from the current URL to determine the active help topic (e.g., FAQs, guides, contact) and exposes this state to other components. When the user changes the active topic via the UI, it updates the topic parameter in the URL without a full page reload so that the selection is shareable and persists across refresh.

Displays topic tabs (such as FAQs, How-to guides, Contact) and lets the user switch between them. It receives the currently active topic from help-topic-router and renders the corresponding tab as active. When a user clicks a different tab, it notifies help-topic-router so the topic URL parameter and active view are updated.

Provides a text input field and optional submit/clear controls that allow the user to search within FAQ content loaded from InformationalPageContent. It maintains the current query string and emits filtered results criteria to the faq-list component without calling any additional backend interface.

Renders a list of FAQ items derived from the Help & Support content, optionally filtered according to the current search query from faq-search-form. Each FAQ can be expanded or collapsed to show or hide its answer. Any inline links within answers (e.g., to tracking or duties estimator pages) are rendered as navigable anchors.

Displays procedural or how-to sections from the loaded InformationalPageContent, such as instructions for creating shipments, scheduling pickups, or changing delivery options. It organizes content into clickable headings and bodies and exposes inline links to other functional pages when present.

Provides prominent self-service links to key functional pages, such as tracking a shipment, creating a shipment, and estimating duties. Clicking these links navigates to track.html, ship_rates.html with mode=create_shipment, or customs_estimator.html respectively using standard URL navigation.

Shows non-form contact options such as support phone number, support email, and operating hours as provided by the loaded InformationalPageContent. It may also include a button or link that scrolls down to or focuses the contact-support-form component to encourage form submission when appropriate.

Collects user details to submit a support request. Required fields include name, email, a topic/category, and a message. It pre-fills or pre-selects the topic field based on the URL topic parameter when applicable. On submission, it validates the inputs and, if valid, calls submitSupportRequest with name, email, topic, and message. It then exposes the API response (success flag and reference_id) to the support-request-confirmation component.

Displays the outcome of a submitted support request. When submitSupportRequest returns success=true, it shows a confirmation message including the provided reference_id and may optionally indicate that a confirmation email has been sent (if part of the content or response). If success=false or the request errors, it shows an error message and allows the user to retry submission via the contact-support-form.

Generates an in-page navigation list based on headings within the loaded InformationalPageContent (such as sections for tracking, shipping, customs, pickups, and returns). Clicking an anchor scrolls the viewport to the corresponding section. It also reacts to the active topic from help-topic-router to highlight or focus the relevant anchor (e.g., the contact section when topic=contact).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a thin utility strip and primary navigation (shared across the site), then a dominant Help & Support hero section that fills most of the first viewport. Within this hero, a left-aligned content column holds the help headline, description, and a prominent search form, along with topic tabs. The right side of the hero is visually lighter, showing a technology-forward global illustration. Below the hero, the main help content is arranged in a two-column layout: a wider left column for FAQs/guides and contact form, and a narrower right column for quick links and contact options. A floating vertical anchor navigation on the far right mirrors the mockup’s vertical contact bar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a thin utility strip and primary navigation (shared across the site), then a dominant Help & Support hero section that fills most of the first viewport. Within this hero, a left-aligned content column holds the help headline, description, and a prominent search form, along with topic tabs. The right side of the hero is visually lighter, showing a technology-forward global illustration. Below the hero, the main help content is arranged in a two-column layout: a wider left column for FAQs/guides and contact form, and a narrower right column for quick links and contact options. A floating vertical anchor navigation on the far right mirrors the mockup’s vertical contact bar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height, full width, with a full-bleed background image (earth/satellite motif). The inner content container is constrained to about 1140px and centered. Inside the hero, the left content block takes about 45–50% of the container width, while the right side is kept open for the background imagery and breathing room. Below the hero, the main content container retains the same width and is split into a 2/3 left column (for faq/how-to/contact form content) and 1/3 right column (quick links and contact options). The vertical anchor navigation occupies a slim 5–7% slice at the far right of the viewport, floating above content. Vertical spacing between major bands (hero to main content, main sections) uses generous 48–64px gaps.
layout_strategies.content_density: The overall density stays marketing-style and spacious: large typography in the hero, generous spacing between headline, search, tabs, and primary CTA. The search form is visually strong but not cramped. Below the hero, the FAQ and guide lists become more content-dense but still use clear 24–32px vertical spacing between groups and list items. Sidebar panels (quick links, contact options) are compact yet breathable, with 16–24px padding inside cards. No area feels like a dense dashboard; it’s a guided, approachable help hub.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F/Z pattern. The eye starts at the top left with the logo and page title, moves right across the main navigation, then diagonally down to the left hero headline and help search form (the primary focal point). From there, the eye moves right to the lighter hero imagery and the vertical anchor navigation on the far right. As users scroll, the gaze is drawn down the left column through tabs, FAQs/guides, then further down to the contact form, while the right column provides quick actions and contact options. The orange primary CTA elements (e.g., search button, key links, submit button) act as anchor points in this flow.

layout.overall_description: The Help & Support page opens on a full-width hero with a dark, technology-forward global background image. Centered within a 1140px content container, the left half of the hero shows a large white headline like “How can we help with your shipment?”, a short supporting line, and a dominant help search form that spans most of that column. Just beneath or aligned closely under the search, a horizontal tab row lets users toggle between FAQs, how-to guides, and contact support content. The right half of the hero is visually lighter, allowing the satellite/earth imagery to show through and subtly reinforcing the global logistics theme. Along the far right edge of the viewport, a slim vertical anchor navigation floats mid-height, echoing the mockup’s right-side toolbar and providing quick jumps to “Top,” “FAQs,” “Guides,” and “Contact support.” As the user scrolls down, the hero gives way to a structured two-column main content area on a clean light background. The left 2/3 width becomes the primary reading column where the active tab content (FAQ list or how-to guides) appears with spacious headings and accordions. Further down in this left column, a prominent contact support band houses the support request form. On the right 1/3, compact cards present quick self-service links (like tracking and shipping tools) followed by a contact options block listing phone, email, or chat channels. Throughout, the spacing is generous, the primary actions are highlighted in orange, and the floating right-side anchor nav remains visible, tying the whole layout back to the original marketing hero aesthetic while making the help experience structured and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-content-loader is non-visual by default but occupies the same logical footprint as the main help content area. It is positioned immediately below the hero section, aligned to the central content container that spans roughly 1140px wide and is centered on the page. When active (e.g., while fetching FAQs or guides), it overlays a subtle loading layer over the left main content column and, if needed, the right sidebar, dimming that area from just below the hero tabs down to the start of the page footer. The loader’s visual indicators (spinner or skeleton rows) are centered within the left content column, occupying about 50–60% of its width and leaving generous 32px+ padding around, to stay consistent with the spacious marketing feel. It does not interfere with the top navigation or the floating vertical anchor bar on the far right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay within the main content region: positioned relative to the main content container, covering the FAQ/guides/contact form area with a semi-transparent layer while keeping the global header and anchor-navigation accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-topic-router is entirely structural and non-visual; it resides logically at the page level, managing which help topic or tab is currently active based on the URL. Its influence is spatial only in that it determines which content set (FAQ list, how-to guides list, or contact form section) appears in the primary left content column below the hero. When the router changes state (e.g., user clicks an anchor-navigation link or a deep link in the URL), the viewport smoothly scrolls the central content container so that the associated section’s heading is aligned slightly below the bottom edge of the main navigation bar, preserving a consistent visual rhythm between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; controls scroll positioning and which components occupy the main content region, ensuring the target section is brought into view just under the fixed top navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-topic-tabs sit horizontally near the bottom of the hero’s left content block, acting as a bridge between the hero and the main content. They are left-aligned within the central content container, starting directly under or with about 24px–32px vertical gap below the FAQ search form. The tab bar spans roughly 60–70% of the container width, matching the width of the search input above it, and remains within the left 45–50% hero column. Each tab label (e.g., FAQs, How-to guides, Contact support) is given generous horizontal padding (~16px–24px) and enough spacing between labels (~24px) to keep the navigation airy. Visually, this creates a clear horizontal strip that caps the hero content and visually anchors the top of the main content area that scrolls into view below. On smaller screens, the tabs collapse to full-width, stacking slightly tighter but still maintaining comfortable vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; scrolls out of view with the hero. It does not stick to the viewport, but it defines which content region appears directly beneath the hero when the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-search-form is the focal interactive element of the hero. It is positioned within the left half of the hero, centered vertically in that hero band but slightly weighted toward the upper-middle area to align with eye-scanning patterns. It sits directly below the hero headline with a comfortable 24–32px gap and above the help-topic-tabs by about 24px. The search form itself spans about 60% of the inner content container width, extending across much of the left hero column but not reaching the hero’s centerline, leaving visual room to the right for the background imagery. The input field is large and horizontally oriented, with approximately 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding inside, and an attached or adjacent orange search button on the right edge. Above and below the form, there is generous whitespace (at least 24px above, 32–40px to the tabs below), reinforcing its primary importance and aligning with the spacious marketing aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; it does not stick or float. On scroll, it moves up with the hero background, yielding space to the main content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-list occupies the primary left column in the main content area, directly below the hero section and aligned under the help-topic-tabs when the FAQ tab is active. It is constrained within the central 1140px container, taking approximately 66–70% of that width, starting from the left edge of the container. A clear section heading like “Frequently Asked Questions” appears at the top of this column with about 48px of vertical space separating it from the bottom edge of the hero. Individual FAQ items are stacked vertically, each spanning nearly the full width of the left column, with 16–24px vertical spacing between items. To mirror the clean corporate feel, FAQ questions may appear as accordions with subtle dividing lines and 16–24px inner padding. The right 30–34% of the container is reserved for the sidebar panels (quick-links-panel, contact-options-block), so the faq-list never extends into that region. Adequate bottom margin (48–64px) separates the end of the FAQ list from the contact-support-form section that follows below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable main content. It shares the same vertical scroll region as the howto-guides-list and contact-support-form, appearing or hiding based on the state controlled by help-topic-tabs and help-topic-router.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The howto-guides-list uses the same spatial footprint as the faq-list. When the ‘How-to guides’ tab is active, this list replaces the FAQ content in the left main column beneath the hero. It aligns to the left edge of the central content container and spans roughly 66–70% of that width. A section title such as “Step-by-step guides” is placed at the top of this area with about 48px margin from the hero’s bottom edge or tab strip. Guides are presented as vertically stacked items—either as cards or simple list entries—each occupying the full width of the left column, separated by 16–24px vertical spacing. The right-hand sidebar (quick-links-panel and contact-options-block) remains visible to the right in its narrow column, so the guides list never overlaps that space. Vertical spacing at the bottom (48–64px) creates a clear break before the contact-support-form or any subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and mutually exclusive with the faq-list in the same left-column region, governed by tab selection and routing. Scrolls naturally within the main page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-links-panel is the upper card in the right sidebar of the main content area. It begins at the same vertical level as the top of the faq-list or howto-guides-list—approximately 48px below the bottom of the hero—and is right-aligned within the central 1140px container. It occupies about 30–34% of the container width, forming a clean card with rounded corners and a light background that stands out from the page background. Inside this panel, quick actions such as ‘Track a shipment’ or ‘Calculate shipping’ are listed vertically, each link given robust click targets and about 12–16px vertical padding, with 16–24px inner padding around the card’s edges. There is at least 24–32px of vertical space between this panel and the contact-options-block that sits below it, keeping the sidebar aligned with the airy marketing feel rather than appearing stacked and cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column; it scrolls with the main content. It stays aligned visually with the top of the FAQ/guides section, forming a balanced two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-options-block is positioned in the right sidebar directly beneath the quick-links-panel, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It shares the same width, roughly 30–34% of the central content container, and the same right alignment. Visually, it reads as another card: a title like ‘Other ways to contact us’ at the top, followed by channels such as phone numbers, email, or live chat presented as short rows or small tiles. Each option uses 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding within the card, mirroring the component padding system. The height of this block is flexible but generally shorter than the main left column content, leaving some whitespace below in most viewports so the layout doesn’t feel bottom-heavy on the right. It functions as a quick reference area that stays visually secondary to the main FAQ/guides content but more prominent than purely structural elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked vertically under quick-links-panel within the right sidebar column, scrolling together with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-support-form is placed below the primary informational content (FAQ list or how-to guides) in the left main column, forming a distinct contact section that feels like a secondary hero within the page. It begins after a generous 64px vertical gap from the end of the FAQ/guides content, with a clear section heading such as ‘Still need help? Contact our support team’ aligned to the left edge of the container. On wide screens, the form itself spans roughly 70–80% of the central content container, slightly overlapping into where the right sidebar was, so this section feels more full-width and important. The form fields are arranged in one or two columns depending on viewport width; on desktop, two-column field rows (e.g., name and email) align nicely with the underlying 12-column grid, while larger fields like message span the full form width. The entire form is wrapped with ample padding (24–32px on all sides), and the primary submit button is an orange, full-width or large button aligned left, with about 24px of top margin. There is 64px or more of bottom margin before the footer, giving the form room to breathe and echoing the generous spacing of the top hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow. On smaller screens, the form collapses to full-width and stacks beneath the FAQ/guides content, with the right sidebar panels stacking above or below as appropriate.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-request-confirmation appears only after the contact-support-form is successfully submitted. Visually, it appears as a prominent notification band centered near the top of the viewport, overlaying the content just above the contact-support-form area. It has a maximum width of around 480–560px and is horizontally centered within the page, with 24–32px of internal padding and a subtle drop shadow to stand off from the background. Vertically, it floats slightly below the main navigation bar—leaving about 16–24px of space from the bottom of the nav—so it is immediately in the user’s line of sight without obscuring the global header. The band may use a light success color accent and concise text, with a close/dismiss icon aligned to the right. When the user scrolls, the confirmation band scrolls away like normal content unless designed as transient/toast-style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the main content region as a centered notification near the top of the viewport; positioned relative to the viewport rather than the document flow while visible, then removed or hidden after a period or when dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The anchor-navigation is a slim, vertical toolbar fixed along the far right side of the viewport, visually echoing the vertical contact bar from the mockup. It occupies about 5–7% of the viewport width and is positioned so its center aligns roughly with the mid-height of the hero on initial load, then remains vertically centered as the user scrolls. The bar sits outside the main 1140px content container, leaving clear separation between it and the right sidebar cards (quick-links-panel and contact-options-block). Inside the bar, 3–5 anchor items (e.g., ‘Top,’ ‘FAQs,’ ‘Guides,’ ‘Contact’) are stacked vertically with equal spacing, each shown as a small icon-and-label or icon-only button inside a square or rounded square with 8–12px internal padding. There is a consistent gap (12–16px) between these buttons, and the active section’s anchor is highlighted in the brand orange. The bar does not extend to the top or bottom of the screen; it floats, with visible background around it, reinforcing its role as an auxiliary navigation element rather than a full-height sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the viewport on desktop, maintaining its position as the user scrolls. Clicking items triggers smooth scroll to the corresponding section headings in the main content (hero, FAQ/Guides, contact-support-form). On smaller screens, it may dock to the bottom as a horizontal bar or be hidden behind a toggle to preserve space.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Displays the dynamically loaded legal terms governing the use of the international logistics and shipping services, including links to related policies such as the Privacy Policy.

core_features: Fetch and display structured Terms & Conditions content using the getInformationalPageContent interface based on the current page URL.
Render each section of the Terms & Conditions with headings and rich text, allowing deep-linking to individual sections through URL parameters.
Provide an in-page navigation (table of contents) derived from the loaded sections so users can jump directly to specific clauses.
Support deep-link access via URL parameters to automatically scroll to and highlight a specific section (for example after clicking a link from another page or email).
Handle internal links embedded in the terms content, including routing clicks on the Privacy Policy link to privacy.html with appropriate URL parameters.
Handle loading, empty state, and error states for the terms content in a functional way without hardcoded fallback text.
user_workflows:
Viewing the Terms & Conditions from the footer:
1. User clicks the “Terms & Conditions” link in the site footer (which navigates to terms.html with any existing query parameters preserved or added).
2. The page initializes and the content loader component derives a page_slug from the current route (e.g., from the filename or a URL parameter such as ?page_slug=...).
3. The page calls getInformationalPageContent with the computed page_slug.
4. While the request is in progress, a loading state is shown in place of the content.
5. When data is returned, the table of contents is generated from the sections array and the full Terms & Conditions text is rendered.
6. If a section parameter is present in the URL (e.g., ?section=...), the matching section is scrolled into view and functionally highlighted.

Navigating between sections of the Terms & Conditions:
1. User opens terms.html and the sections are loaded via getInformationalPageContent.
2. The table of contents component lists all section headings as clickable items.
3. User clicks a heading in the table of contents.
4. The click updates the URL query parameter (e.g., ?section=<section-identifier>) without reloading the page where possible, or triggers a navigation that preserves all other query parameters.
5. The section viewer component reacts to the URL change event, scrolls to the corresponding section element, and applies a functional highlight state (e.g., via a CSS class) to that section.

Opening the Privacy Policy from within the Terms & Conditions:
1. User is reading the Terms & Conditions content loaded via getInformationalPageContent.
2. Within the body_html of one or more sections, there is an anchor link that points to the Privacy Policy (e.g., an href that targets privacy.html or an equivalent internal URL).
3. The rich-text link router component intercepts the click event on this anchor.
4. The component determines that the link is an internal navigation to the Privacy Policy page and constructs a navigation URL to privacy.html, preserving relevant query parameters such as language or version if present (e.g., ?lang=...&version=...).
5. The browser navigates to privacy.html; the Terms & Conditions page is unloaded and control passes to the Privacy Policy page.

Deep-linking directly to a specific Terms & Conditions section:
1. User receives or clicks a link such as terms.html?section=<section-identifier>&lang=<code>&version=<value> (actual parameter values depend on the implementation and are not hardcoded).
2. The browser loads terms.html with these query parameters.
3. On initialization, the content loader component computes the page_slug and calls getInformationalPageContent.
4. After the content is loaded, the section viewer component reads the section parameter from the URL.
5. The component matches the section parameter to the corresponding section element (e.g., via an id based on the section heading or a mapping defined by the content) and scrolls that section into view.
6. The matching section is functionally highlighted so the user understands which part of the terms was referenced.

Handling errors or missing Terms & Conditions content:
1. User navigates to terms.html.
2. The content loader calls getInformationalPageContent with the computed page_slug.
3. If the API returns an error or an empty sections array, the loader component enters an error/empty state.
4. In error state, the component shows a generic error message and a retry control that re-triggers the same getInformationalPageContent call when activated.
5. In empty state (no title or sections), the component shows a generic informational message indicating that the content is currently unavailable, without displaying any hardcoded terms text.
interactions: On page load, the content loader component reads URL parameters (such as page_slug, lang, version, section) and triggers a call to getInformationalPageContent with a derived page_slug value.
While getInformationalPageContent is in progress, the page displays a functional loading state (e.g., spinner, skeleton, or placeholder container) instead of static terms content.
On successful response from getInformationalPageContent, the page binds the returned title and sections to the heading display, table of contents, and body section components.
On failed response from getInformationalPageContent, the page displays an error state with an action control that, when clicked, retries the same API request using the current page_slug.
When the user clicks an item in the table of contents, the click handler updates the URL’s section query parameter and triggers scrolling to the corresponding section.

When the URL’s query parameters change (for example, via in-page navigation or browser back/forward), the section viewer component reacts to the new section value and adjusts the visible highlighted section and scroll position accordingly.

When the user clicks an anchor inside the terms rich-text content:
  - If the href is an internal navigation to privacy.html, the link router intercepts the click and triggers navigation to privacy.html, preserving relevant URL parameters (such as lang and version).
  - If the href is an internal navigation to another section of terms.html, the link router prevents a full reload and instead updates the section parameter and scroll position.
  - If the href is external, the router lets the default browser behavior occur (e.g., navigation to an external URL).
When the user clicks the retry action in the error state, the content loader re-invokes getInformationalPageContent with the same calculated page_slug and updates the UI based on the new response.

When the user uses the browser’s back or forward buttons after in-page navigation, the page listens to the URL change event and re-applies the appropriate section selection and scroll position, without reloading content if the page_slug has not changed.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Terms & Conditions page state is controlled entirely via URL parameters and the response from getInformationalPageContent.

URL parameters:
- page_slug: Optional. If present (e.g., terms.html?page_slug=<value>), this value is passed directly to getInformationalPageContent. If not present, the component derives a page_slug from the current route (for example, using the filename portion of the URL such as 'terms' or 'terms-conditions'). The derived strategy must be implemented generically without embedding static page content.
- lang: Optional. Used to represent the current language or locale. While getInformationalPageContent only accepts page_slug, the lang parameter can be encoded into the page_slug if required by the backend (e.g., combining base slug and lang into a single string). The lang parameter is preserved when navigating from terms.html to privacy.html.
- version: Optional. Represents a specific version of the Terms & Conditions (e.g., a date or version identifier). As with lang, this can be encoded into page_slug if versioned content is supported. If version changes, the content loader treats the page as a different logical resource and re-fetches content.
- section: Optional. Represents the currently focused or highlighted section within the Terms & Conditions (e.g., a normalized section identifier derived from the heading text or provided by the backend within sections). On initial load or when section changes, the section viewer locates the corresponding DOM element and scrolls it into view and applies a highlight state.

State transitions:
- Initial load: Read page_slug|lang|version|section from URL. Compute final page_slug. Invoke getInformationalPageContent. Set UI to 'loading'.
- Load success: Store the returned title and sections in component state. Regenerate the table of contents. If section is present, scroll to the matching section; otherwise, show the top of the document.
- Load error: Store an error flag in state. Show an error message and a retry control. No terms sections are rendered while in error state.
- Section change (via user click or URL change): Update 'currentSectionId' in state based on the URL's section parameter. Scroll to the matching section element if it exists. Update highlight state for sections and table of contents entries.
- Version/lang change (if implemented via URL): When either parameter changes, recompute page_slug and re-call getInformationalPageContent. Clear previous terms data and revert to loading state to avoid showing stale content.
- Navigation to Privacy Policy: When routing to privacy.html, include lang and version URL parameters (if present) so the Privacy Policy page can apply similar logic. The Terms & Conditions page does not maintain state after navigation away.

components.functionality: Initializes the Terms & Conditions page by deriving the page_slug from the URL, calling getInformationalPageContent, managing loading/error/empty states, and providing the loaded title and sections to child components.
Displays the main page heading using the title returned by getInformationalPageContent, falling back to a generic functional label when the title is missing while avoiding hardcoded legal text.

Generates an in-page navigation list (table of contents) from the sections array and allows the user to jump to any section. Updates the URL section parameter on click and reflects the currently active section based on URL state.

Renders each section returned by getInformationalPageContent as a scrollable block with a unique identifier for deep-linking. Listens for section parameter changes in the URL to scroll to and highlight the target section.

Intercepts click events on anchor tags inside the rendered body_html, determines whether the target is an internal section link, an internal page (such as privacy.html), or an external URL, and performs the appropriate navigation while preserving relevant URL parameters such as lang and version.

Displays loading, error, or empty states for the Terms & Conditions content. In loading state, shows a generic placeholder; in error state, shows a functional error message and exposes a retry action; in empty state, indicates that no content is available.

Ensures that when the global footer 'Terms & Conditions' link is clicked, navigation routes to terms.html with appropriate query parameters, and that from this page any footer 'Privacy Policy' link routes to privacy.html using URL-based navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global utility strip and primary nav at the very top (outside this spec), then a reduced-height hero-style banner dedicated to the Terms & Conditions heading, followed by the main content band. Inside the main content band, layout splits into a left main column for the terms body and a narrower right column for supportive navigation (table of contents and related links), mirroring the mockup’s pattern of primary content on the left and secondary controls on the right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same linear top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global utility strip and primary nav at the very top (outside this spec), then a reduced-height hero-style banner dedicated to the Terms & Conditions heading, followed by the main content band. Inside the main content band, layout splits into a left main column for the terms body and a narrower right column for supportive navigation (table of contents and related links), mirroring the mockup’s pattern of primary content on the left and secondary controls on the right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content is constrained to a max width of ~1140px within a full-width background. The hero-style heading band occupies roughly 35–45% of the viewport height on this legal page (shorter than the marketing hero but visually consistent). Below it, the content area uses a two-column layout: the terms sections take about 65–70% of the content width on the left, while the table of contents and related link controls occupy about 30–35% on the right. Generous vertical spacing (48–64px) separates the hero, the main content band, and the footer navigation to maintain the airy marketing feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The heading area remains very spacious with a large H1 and ample breathing room above and below. The rich-text terms body is denser by necessity, but is visually moderated with clear section breaks, 24–32px spacing between major clauses, and readable line lengths in the left column. The right column (TOC and link router) remains light and card-like, with 16–24px internal padding and clear item spacing, matching the low-density, marketing-style control surfaces from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows the same F/Z pattern: the user’s eye comes from the global logo and nav at the top, then cuts diagonally down to the large left-aligned ‘Terms & Conditions’ heading in the hero band. From there, the eye is drawn to the right side of that band where related-policy links are surfaced, then drops down into the left column to read the terms content. As the user scrolls, the sticky right-column table of contents remains in view, continually pulling the eye to the right for quick section jumps, echoing the mockup’s use of right-edge secondary controls and maintaining a left-to-right scanning rhythm.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing utility strip and primary navigation, the Terms & Conditions page opens with a full-width, reduced-height hero-style banner that uses the same deep blue, tech-forward background (with faint global or network imagery) and a subtle dark overlay. Centered within this band is the main content container. On the left half of this container sits a large, white ‘Terms & Conditions’ heading with a short optional supporting line beneath, all generously spaced to feel clean and authoritative. On the right half of the same band, a slim, unobtrusive row of related-policy links aligns to the right edge, visually tying back to the hero’s right-side control pattern. Below this banner, the layout transitions into a two-column content section: the left column holds the scrollable legal text, clearly broken into sections, while the right column presents a vertically organized table of contents in a card-like panel that sticks as the user scrolls. Any loading state appears centered over the main content area, dimming but not obscuring the structural layout. At the bottom of the terms content, a low-profile internal footer navigation bar spans the central width above the global site footer, offering quick navigation to other key legal pages and back-to-top controls, all aligned with the same 12-column grid and generous spacing as the rest of the site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader functions as the structural wrapper for the entire Terms & Conditions content area, beginning just below the hero-style heading band and ending just above the internal footer navigation. It spans the full width of the central 1140px content container, centered on the page, and controls the visibility of the heading, table of contents, and rich-text viewer. When the page is loading, this component reserves the standard two-column layout space (about 65–70% width left, 30–35% width right) so that the page does not visually jump; skeleton or placeholder elements appear in the positions where the heading and content columns will be, respecting the same vertical spacing (32–48px between blocks).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal, full-width content container within the central page column. It manages child components but does not itself float or stick; instead it defines the vertical slice of the page from the bottom of the hero band down to just above the internal footer navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main heading sits in a dedicated, reduced-height hero-style band immediately beneath the primary navigation. This band stretches full-width across the viewport with a deep blue background and subtle global imagery, but the actual heading text is constrained to the central 1140px container. Within that container, the heading is aligned to the left, occupying roughly the left 50–60% of the container width. The H1 ‘Terms & Conditions’ is large and bold, set in white, with approximately 24–32px of vertical space above and 16–24px below it. If a subheading or last-updated line is present, it appears directly beneath the H1, also left-aligned, in smaller text with 8–16px vertical spacing. The right half of this band is visually open, with the related link router positioned towards the top-right of the container, preserving the mockup’s left-primary/right-secondary composition. Overall, there is about 64px of padding above and below the heading block within this band, making the hero roughly 35–45% of the viewport height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero band, anchored to the central grid. It scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page, allowing the main content to take over visual focus.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents appears below the hero band on the right side of the main content region, mirroring the mockup’s right-edge secondary controls. Within the central 1140px container, the main content area is split into a 2-column layout: the TOC occupies the right column, about 30–35% of the container width. It is presented as a tall, card-like panel with a white or very light background, subtle border or shadow, and 16–24px of internal padding. The top of the TOC aligns vertically with the top of the first visible section of the terms body on the left, with a horizontal gap of about 32px between columns. Inside the TOC, section titles are listed in a vertical stack with 8–12px spacing between items and ~4–8px of extra spacing between major section groups, maintaining a clean, scannable structure. On wide screens, this panel extends down the page but never exceeds the bottom of the viewport; as content scrolls, the panel remains visible, allowing quick jumps to specific sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport on desktop and larger tablet breakpoints, anchored to the right side of the central content container with a small top offset (e.g., 24–32px from the top of the viewport as the user scrolls). On smaller screens, it stacks above or below the main terms content as a full-width block and loses its sticky behavior to prioritize vertical reading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section viewer is the primary reading area and occupies the left column of the main content region below the hero band. Within the 1140px container, it spans roughly 65–70% of the width, starting from the left edge of the content grid, with the table of contents column to its right. The top of the first section aligns horizontally with the top of the TOC card, with a comfortable 48–64px vertical gap from the bottom of the hero heading band. Text lines are limited to a readable measure (roughly 60–80 characters) and are left-aligned, with 24–32px spacing between major sections and 16–24px between subsections. Each section heading is slightly larger and bolder than the body copy and separated from the preceding section by additional vertical spacing (24–32px) to create clear visual breaks. Anchor targets for the TOC align just above each section heading so that, when clicked, the section title lands slightly below the top of the viewport, keeping it visible beneath any fixed navigation. On mobile, this viewer becomes a full-width column stacked above or below the TOC, with the same internal spacing and typography hierarchy preserved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing vertically as the main scrollable content. It expands in height according to the length of the terms and sets the overall scroll length of the page. It responds to TOC interactions by scrolling to anchored sections but does not otherwise float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The link router is positioned within the hero-style heading band, on the right side of the central content container, echoing the mockup’s use of secondary controls toward the right edge. Horizontally, it aligns with the right 40–50% of the container width, opposite the main heading block. Vertically, it sits roughly in the upper half of the band, aligning its baseline with or slightly below the main heading’s baseline to create a natural Z-shaped reading path: heading on the left, then related links on the right. The router displays related legal documents (e.g., Privacy Policy, Cookie Policy) as a compact horizontal row of links or pill-style buttons, right-aligned within its zone, with 8–16px spacing between each. There is at least 16–24px of padding between the link router and the top and right edges of the content container, keeping it comfortably away from the viewport edges and the nav above. On smaller screens, this component shifts below the main heading, expanding to nearly full width and center- or left-aligning the links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero band. It scrolls away along with the heading when the user moves down the page and does not remain sticky, reinforcing its role as an entry-point to related documents rather than a persistent control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The load state indicator appears above the main content columns when the terms content is being fetched. Visually, it is centered horizontally within the 1140px content container, roughly aligned with the midpoint between the left section viewer column and the right TOC column. Vertically, it sits in the upper third of the main content area, about 64–96px below the bottom of the hero band, so that it is clearly visible even on shorter viewports. If implemented as a spinner or progress indicator, it is accompanied by a short status message directly beneath it, with 8–12px of vertical spacing between icon and text. The surrounding area maintains the standard page background, but the content loader may optionally apply a light dimming overlay to the underlying columns, signaling that the structure is present but content is not yet interactive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the section viewer and TOC within their allocated space while loading. It is centrally aligned but does not fix to the viewport; instead, it remains in the normal flow and disappears once content has loaded, handing visual focus back to the heading and terms content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The internal footer navigation sits at the bottom of the Terms & Conditions content, directly below the last section of the rich-text viewer and just above the global site footer. It spans the full width of the central 1140px container, centered on the page, and appears as a low-profile horizontal bar or simple text row with 16–24px of vertical padding. A thin top border or subtle background tint separates it from the main content above. Links such as ‘Back to top’, ‘Privacy Policy’, and ‘Shipping Policy’ are laid out in a single row on desktop, either center-aligned or left-aligned with 16–24px spacing between each link, maintaining ample click targets. There is a comfortable vertical gap (32–48px) between the end of the last terms section and this footer nav, and another 32–48px down to the global footer, preserving the page’s spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the Terms page content section. It does not stick or float; it becomes visible only once the user scrolls to the end of the legal text, acting as a natural transition into the global footer and other site-wide navigation.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays dynamically loaded privacy policy content explaining how personal and shipment-related data is collected, used, stored, and shared, and enables users to navigate between sections and related legal pages.

core_features: Fetch and render structured Privacy Policy content using the getInformationalPageContent interface based on a page slug derived from the privacy.html context and URL parameters.
Display the policy in multiple structured sections with headings and rich-text body content returned by the API.

Generate an in-page table of contents from the loaded sections to allow quick navigation and deep linking to specific topics (e.g., data collection, data usage, user rights, shipment data processing).
Support deep linking to specific policy sections using URL parameters so that external links can open the page at a specific section (e.g., privacy.html?section=user-rights).
Synchronize in-page navigation (clicking table-of-contents entries or section anchors) with URL parameters so that the current section can be bookmarked or shared.

Provide a functional link from the Privacy Policy content to the Terms & Conditions page (terms.html), optionally passing context via URL parameters (e.g., terms.html?from=privacy&section=data-protection) without hardcoding any text content.
Expose privacy contact details and user rights information strictly through the structured content returned by getInformationalPageContent, without hardcoded policy text.
user_workflows: View the Privacy Policy from footer navigation: 1) User clicks the "Privacy Policy" link in the global footer. 2) Browser navigates to privacy.html (optionally with parameters such as ?lang=xx). 3) On page load, the content loader component derives a page_slug for the privacy policy (e.g., combining a base slug with any language or version parameters) and calls getInformationalPageContent with that slug. 4) The page renders the returned title and sections. 5) The user scrolls through sections or uses the table of contents to jump to specific topics. 6) If the user clicks a "Terms & Conditions" link in the content, the browser navigates to terms.html with relevant URL parameters (e.g., ?from=privacy).

Open the Privacy Policy at a specific section via deep link: 1) User follows a link such as privacy.html?section=data-rights (created elsewhere in the site or shared by another user). 2) On load, the content loader fetches the policy via getInformationalPageContent. 3) After content is loaded, a URL–content sync component reads the section parameter and identifies the matching section by its generated ID. 4) The page scrolls to that section and may visually emphasize it (e.g., focus) using the section anchor. 5) If the user clicks another entry in the table of contents, the visible section changes and the section parameter in the URL is updated accordingly.

Navigate between Privacy Policy and Terms & Conditions: 1) While reading the Privacy Policy, the user clicks a contextual "Terms & Conditions" link embedded in the policy content (provided by the API as part of body_html or added as a functional link within a section). 2) The click handler constructs a URL to terms.html and may include parameters like from=privacy and, where applicable, section=privacy-related. 3) Browser navigates to terms.html with those parameters. 4) If the user navigates back (using the browser back button), privacy.html reloads, re-fetches its content through getInformationalPageContent, and restores the section view based on any section parameter still present.
interactions: On initial page load, the privacy-content-loader component triggers a call to getInformationalPageContent using a page_slug that represents the Privacy Policy for the current language or context, then emits an onLoad event when the content is available.

When the user clicks an entry in the privacy-toc (table of contents), the page scrolls to the corresponding section element, updates the section URL parameter using the History API, and emits an onSectionChange event.

When the user scrolls manually through the content, a scroll listener in the section-url-sync component can detect the currently dominant section and update the section URL parameter to reflect it (if configured), enabling shareable deep links.

When the user clicks a "Terms & Conditions" link within the policy content (either provided via body_html or rendered via a dedicated component), a click handler navigates the browser to terms.html while appending context parameters such as from=privacy and, optionally, section values relevant to the clicked context.

If the getInformationalPageContent call fails (for example, network or server error), the privacy-content-loader component emits an onError event and displays a generic, non-hardcoded error state, guiding the user to retry the load or navigate elsewhere without exposing static policy text.

If a lang URL parameter is present (e.g., privacy.html?lang=en), the privacy-content-loader uses it to construct the page_slug or pass it as part of slug logic so the correct localized Privacy Policy version is requested from getInformationalPageContent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely through URL parameters and the response from getInformationalPageContent; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. Key parameters include: 1) section: A string representing the current or targeted section of the Privacy Policy (e.g., 'data-collection', 'user-rights', 'shipments'). On initial load, if section is present, the page waits for the content from getInformationalPageContent, maps each returned section.heading to a sanitized section ID, and scrolls to the element whose ID matches the section parameter. When the user clicks within the table of contents or certain in-content anchors, the section parameter is updated to the corresponding section ID via the History API, without reloading the page. If the user changes the section parameter in the URL manually and reloads the page, the same mapping and scroll behavior applies. 2) lang: Indicates the language or locale of the policy (e.g., 'en', 'fr'). The privacy-content-loader uses this parameter to generate the page_slug passed to getInformationalPageContent, allowing the backend to determine which localized content to return. If lang is absent, a default localization strategy (defined server-side or globally) is applied and reflected in the chosen page_slug. 3) version (optional): If present (e.g., 'current', '2024-01'), this parameter can be incorporated into the page_slug to request a specific policy version from getInformationalPageContent. This allows links from older emails or archived pages to point to historical versions of the policy without relying on hardcoded text. 4) from: Used when the user arrives from another page (e.g., from=checkout) or navigates away (e.g., to terms.html?from=privacy). The Privacy Policy page can read this parameter to adjust contextual behaviors (such as default target section) without changing the content returned by getInformationalPageContent. All such behaviors derive from state and interface responses; no static policy text or hardcoded legal copy is embedded in the page.

components.functionality: Initializes the Privacy Policy page by constructing an appropriate page_slug (for example, based on the fixed page identity for privacy.html, optionally combined with lang and version URL parameters) and calling getInformationalPageContent. It receives the structured response object with title and sections and passes this data down to content-rendering components. It manages loading and error states without providing any hardcoded policy content.

Renders the Privacy Policy sections returned by getInformationalPageContent. For each section object, it outputs a heading and body_html, assigns a deterministic DOM id to the section (derived from the heading text and/or section index), and exposes anchors that other components can link to. It does not alter the substantive content, only structures it for navigation, scrolling, and accessibility.

Builds an in-page table of contents using the list of sections from getInformationalPageContent. Each table-of-contents item corresponds to one section heading. When an item is clicked, it scrolls to the associated section in privacy-section-list and updates the section URL parameter to match the target section's ID, enabling deep linking and back/forward navigation.

Synchronizes the URL's section parameter with the visible section in the Privacy Policy content. On page load, it reads the section parameter and, once privacy-section-list has rendered all sections, scrolls to the section whose ID matches the parameter. During user scroll or table-of-contents navigation, it detects which section is currently in view and updates the section parameter in the URL via the History API without reloading the page. This component ensures that the URL always reflects the user's current position within the policy.

Handles navigation from the Privacy Policy to the Terms & Conditions page. It can be rendered either as part of a dedicated section or interact with links embedded in the body_html returned by getInformationalPageContent. When activated, it constructs a URL to terms.html and may append parameters such as from=privacy and a contextual section value. The component does not define any link text itself; it only manages click handling and URL construction to maintain consistent, parameter-based navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container. The hero text block appears constrained to a content width approximately 1140px within a full-bleed background image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: global utility bar at the very top, then the primary navigation header, followed by a visually strong but slightly shorter hero band dedicated to “Privacy Policy.” Directly beneath the hero, the main privacy content area adopts a two-column layout inside the same centered 1140px container the mockup suggests: a dominant left column for the policy text and a narrower right column for section navigation and related legal links. Functional/technical components (loader, URL sync) are aligned to these columns without introducing new visual elements.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: global utility bar at the very top, then the primary navigation header, followed by a visually strong but slightly shorter hero band dedicated to “Privacy Policy.” Directly beneath the hero, the main privacy content area adopts a two-column layout inside the same centered 1140px container the mockup suggests: a dominant left column for the policy text and a narrower right column for section navigation and related legal links. Functional/technical components (loader, URL sync) are aligned to these columns without introducing new visual elements.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 50–60% of the initial viewport height, full-bleed with satellite/global imagery, and a left-aligned title block that stays within the 1140px content width. Below it, the privacy content container spans about 80–90% of the page width on large screens, centered. Inside this container, the left content column (privacy-section-list) takes about 8 of 12 grid columns (~65–70% width), while the right column (privacy-toc plus privacy-terms-link) uses the remaining 4 columns (~30–35%). Vertical spacing between the hero and the first line of policy text is generous (48–64px), and between major policy sections is similarly spaced to preserve a marketing-like sense of air even in a text-heavy layout.
layout_strategies.content_density: Despite the legal nature of the page, the layout preserves the mockup’s spacious marketing feel: large, clear headings, comfortable line heights, and consistent section breaks using the 24–48px spacing scale. The left column presents text in readable, medium-width paragraphs rather than full-width lines, and the right column keeps the TOC concise, with vertically stacked links and ample padding. There is minimal visual noise—no dense tables or cramped blocks—so users can skim sections easily via the TOC and then read comfortably in the main column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z pattern from the mockup: the eye starts at the top-left logo, scans right across the navigation, then drops diagonally to the left-aligned “Privacy Policy” hero headline. From there, attention moves down into the left content column where reading begins, while the right side hosts the vertical TOC panel, echoing the mockup’s right-edge action bar. As users scroll, the sticky TOC on the right and the active-section highlighting (via URL sync) create a secondary vertical focal path, subtly drawing the eye from left (current text) to right (navigation state and related legal link).

layout.overall_description: Below the global utility bar and primary navigation, a full-width hero band introduces the Privacy Policy over a darkened, technology-forward earth/satellite background. Within the centered 1140px content container, the hero’s left half carries a bold white “Privacy Policy” heading, a one- or two-line explanatory subheading, and a subtle secondary CTA style for legal navigation, all aligned to the left and framed by generous top and bottom padding. The right side of the hero remains visually open, letting the global imagery breathe. As the hero ends, the main legal content area begins with a clear vertical gap; here the layout shifts into a two-column structure mirroring the hero’s left-dominant composition. On the left, the policy text flows down in well-separated sections, each with a strong heading and comfortable white space. On the right, a vertically stacked, card-like TOC panel floats within the column, staying visible as users scroll so it resembles the mockup’s right-edge toolbar but integrated within the grid. At the bottom of this TOC panel, a clearly marked link to the Terms & Conditions page provides cross-navigation to related legal content. Under the hood, a loader gracefully occupies this same two-column frame while content is fetched, and URL synchronization keeps TOC items and browser address aligned with the section currently in view. The overall effect is a modern, corporate, and calm reading environment that still feels consistent with the dynamic hero of the marketing site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-content-loader sits at the top of the main privacy content area, directly below the hero section and within the centered 1140px content container. Visually, when active, it mimics the final two-column layout: on the left, wider skeleton blocks (about 65–70% of the container width) are stacked vertically to suggest upcoming headings and paragraphs; on the right, a narrower column of shorter skeleton lines (about 30–35% width) hints at the future TOC panel. There is 48–64px of vertical space between the bottom of the hero and the first loader element, echoing the spacious marketing sections. Horizontally, loader elements align to the same left and right margins as the eventual text and TOC, reinforcing the grid structure even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the normal document flow; it temporarily occupies the space of both the left content column and the right TOC column. Once content has loaded, it collapses out without affecting the established two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-section-list forms the main reading column and is anchored to the left side of the central content container, directly under the hero and any page-level title/subtitle copy. It occupies roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (about 65–70% of the container width), starting flush with the left content margin and leaving a consistent horizontal gap of 48–64px between its right edge and the TOC column. Each individual section within the list is visually separated using 32–48px of vertical space, with section headings set slightly larger and bolder to create clear anchors. The first section begins approximately 24–32px below the page-level “Privacy Policy” heading, preserving air above the copy. As users scroll, this left column remains the primary vertical reading path, while section anchors are aligned so that headings appear just below the sticky header and TOC top edge, avoiding collisions and maintaining a clean viewport framing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable page area; it participates in normal scrolling while respecting the two-column grid. Its vertical position relative to the viewport is coordinated with the URL sync so that as sections cross a defined threshold (e.g., one-third from the top), the corresponding TOC item updates without altering its spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-toc is rendered as a right-hand navigation panel, visually echoing the mockup’s vertical action bar but integrated inside the content container. It is aligned to the right edge of the 1140px container and occupies approximately 4 of 12 grid columns (~30–35% width). The panel starts near the top of the main content area, aligning vertically with the first section’s heading in the left column, and is contained within a card-like block with subtle background contrast and 16–24px of internal padding. TOC items are stacked vertically in a single column with 12–16px spacing between them, creating a clear, scannable list of section titles. The panel has a comfortable margin of 32–48px above and below its content to avoid feeling cramped and maintains at least 32px of inner padding on all sides so links feel like generous touch targets. This column does not compete with the hero; instead, it becomes visually active once users scroll past the hero into the reading area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport: on larger screens, the TOC panel remains fixed relative to the viewport once the user has scrolled slightly past the hero, maintaining a top offset of roughly 96–120px to clear the main navigation/header. It scrolls with the page only when reaching the natural top or bottom bounds of the content. On smaller screens, it degrades gracefully to an inline block above the main content, but its conceptual position remains ‘right-hand navigation’ in the design system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-url-sync component is invisible but conceptually tied to the vertical rhythm of the left content column and the TOC on the right. It tracks the vertical positions of each section within the privacy-section-list and coordinates them with the corresponding entries in the privacy-toc. While it does not occupy visible space, its logic is aligned to the same content container and uses the boundaries defined by the sticky header and sticky TOC to determine when a section is considered ‘active.’
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and overlay-like: it attaches scroll listeners to the main content area and updates the browser URL (e.g., hash or query) as sections come into focus, without shifting or reserving any physical layout space. Its behavior affects visual emphasis only indirectly by triggering active-state styling on TOC items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-terms-link is presented as a compact but noticeable navigation link to the Terms & Conditions page, positioned within the same right-hand column as the privacy-toc to keep all legal navigation consolidated. It appears at the bottom of the TOC panel, separated from the section list by a subtle horizontal divider or increased spacing (16–24px). Horizontally, it aligns with the left edge of the TOC’s text content and may be styled as an inline text link with an accent color (orange) or a minimal button/pill, keeping it visually subordinate to the main navigation but clearly discoverable. This placement lets the user’s eye naturally fall to it after scanning the vertical TOC, mirroring how the mockup’s right-side controls encourage further interaction after reading the hero content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the TOC panel’s layout; it scrolls and sticks together with the privacy-toc, remaining visible whenever the TOC is visible. It does not introduce its own sticky or floating behavior beyond what the TOC already provides.
